column4: 1.1 Technology Business Impact Assessments
column5:
Further to the establishment of a business resilience framework and formalisation of the resilience program, Afterpay will: 
 - Establish a process to analyse business impact taking into consideration potential realistic and worst-case business impact across different categories of impacts (e.g. financial, operational, legal and regulatory compliance, reputational, health and safety, etc). 
 - Document a BIA for the business processes and seek approval for the recovery time objective (RTO), recovery point objective (RPO), maximum acceptable outage (MAO) for information, systems and services and minimum acceptable service level (MASL)
 - Additionally, analyse impact associated with disruptions at critical services providers, partners and loss/unavailability of key staff.

column4: 1.4 Cross Skilling
column5:
Identify and require key people to cross skill a few other team members through knowledge sharing sessions, coaching and mentoring on the job thus strengthening the knowledge base. This will be documented in a Developer Portal
 The developer portal will reduce any risk associated with single personal dependency to a level within Afterpay's risk appetite by:
 - Identifying the key individuals associate with each service. 
 - Identify individuals or teams that need to be cross-trained and knowledgeable on the key aspects of the service. Developing a documentation plan for capturing key knowledge within a knowledge base or document repository

column4: 3.1 Service testing rotation scheme/ roster
column5:
In conjunction with the formalisation of the strategy testing processes at Afterpay, management will develop a service testing rotation scheme/roster to include within the periodic DR testing scope based on the criticality of services and the dependencies identified. Establish a process to routinely monitor the performance of the testing
 process and report on testing coverage and effectiveness. The process will aim to perform testing every 6 months
column20:
Recoverability (AKA DR) testing was performed in Sept 2021. See project charter and test strategy attached. The testing identified a few limitation in the recoverability architecture which are to be actioned before December 2021and the testing recertified before the end of December 2021. 
 A process is now in place to execute recoverability testing at least once every quarter. On going engineering recoverability strategy work will focus on reducing the reliance on process heavy quarter testing towards continuous certification.

column4: 3.2 Test plan that tests ability to meet targets defined by BIAs
column5:
Identify key scenarios to test that take into consideration various levels of impact from the lowest component level to the highest-level regional level failure.
 Explore the option of leveraging automated testing approaches that will test resilience of the AWS services to instance failures on a continuous basis so that there is confidence in the built-in resilience arrangements. Document and save the details in the knowledge repository.
 The deliverable here is a test plan that tests ability to meet targets defined by BIAs
column20: IA was informed that due to the integration of Afterpay processes into Block Inc. systems, processes and controls, this action plan was no longer relevant.

column4: 3.3 Individual component level testing
column5: In addition to full regional level failover testing, identify and perform individual component level testing in a controlled environment for all key services. Management will assess and analyse the impact of the same on other dependent components or services. Document any dependencies and incorporate learnings into enhancement of the recovery plans. The deliverable is a document that outlines the testing performed and the ability of systems to meet targets defined by the BIAs.

column4: 4.1 Audit report of dependencies
column5: Following the establishment of a Risk Assessment and Business Impact Analysis Process, management will establish a holistic view of all services (internal and external to AWS), document their inter dependencies with other services and the risk of a disruption on one causing a cascading effect to another. An audit report of dependencies and their ability to meet targets defined by the BIAs will be prepared.
column20:
Taking the holistic view of services ('Capabilities') the interdependencies are defined in 2 areas: Internal and External.
 For Internal - the workloads in New Relic (See 1000365 -> 3.1.2) identify the internal services upon which a holistic view of services depends.
 For External - the tables on attachments EC1, EC2 and EC3 (See 1000383 -> 3.4.1) listed the external services ('Vendors') upon which the holistic view depends.
 The nature of the dependency defines the risk of disruption. This definition has been included in a supplemental table that I have just uploaded.

column4: 4.2 Identify components within AWS where resilience features not enabled
column5: Validate resilience needs and identify components within AWS for which resilience features are not enabled by default. An audit report of AWS components and their ability to meet targets defined by the BIAs will be prepared.

re: the incident simulation - at this stage this was intended only for security team consumption as a way of improving our technology and processes. The team had a session this week to review our internal report and the report of the external provider and to identify the areas that we want to improve over the next quarter. Those items are now being entered into our backlog and will get prioritized every couple of weeks as part of our team planning processes.
 

 I'll put some thought into whether this type of report is a good candidate for regularly sharing with a governance committee. Its worth noting that we carefully selected the scope of this exercise (and will do in the future) to overlap with areas where we knew we'd already made good progress. It intentionally skipped a number of areas where we know we have significant deficiencies. If we did report it to a larger forum we'd have to be careful to ensure that we shared that context too so it didn't give a false sense of security or alarm.

column4: 4.2 Collections data quality
column5: Collections will investigate the data quality insights identified by Internal Audit and assess if there are broader implications of data quality and resolve these appropriately.
column20:
A reconciliation was completed in May 2021. As a result of the reconciliation, the following accounts discrepancies were identified:
 1) Accounts referred to Collections House AU & NZ under a legacy Hardship process (Biller Codes). These accounts were not flagged "in collections" in the Afterpay system.  
 2) Chargebacks - A legacy process whereby we would request DCAs manually adjust balances to reflect chargebacks cardholders had made on accounts already referred with overdue balances, resulting in a balance discrepancy. 
 

 As a result the following data clean up has been completed with each of the DCA's
 

 Collection House
 NZ Hardship Biller Code accounts suspended - 238 accounts at $53,500 outstanding value
 AU Hardship Biller Code accounts suspended - 5512 accounts at $1,975,500 outstanding value
 Chargeback flags lifted - 3603 accounts at $1,894,270 outstanding value
 

 Probe
 Chargeback flags lifted - 3383 accounts at $1,423,552.62 outstanding value 
 

 The ANZ Collections Team completed a second reconciliation in September 2021. All above mentioned discrepancies were resolved. No broader implications of data quality were identified.  The reconciliation did however identify 102 accounts with discrepancies as a result of agent error or DCA process automation (PIF accounts); these have all been resolved.
 

 The Collections and Support Agency Oversight Framework requires that data reconciliation is completed at least quarterly for all DCAs who hold any of Afterpay's inventory.
 

 Due to reconcilation file sizes exceeding the 10MB limit, only summary tables have been uploaded, however the full reconcilation files can be located at:
 Probe: https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1S--5-m5CiaWYC5DGYsSFcSiZskxC_UCF/edit?usp=drive_web&ouid=117897176363236370379&rtpof=true
 CHNZ: https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1FPlMo1fad1l0CJ1qIl5tCvy9cmVi8a0l/edit?usp=drive_web&ouid=117897176363236370379&rtpof=true
 CH AU: https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1YXwzh40l1_f44IcCHQLEkaN1iEJ8Cdtc/edit?usp=drive_web&ouid=117897176363236370379&rtpof=true

column4: 4.3 Data quality KPI for Debt collection agencies
column5: Collections will incorporate data quality as a KPI to ensure Debt Collection Agencies are held accountable for maintaining accurate customer data.
column20:
The following KPI has been updated into all new existing DCA Contracts (Statement of Work) in respect to Data Quality:
 

 *6.2 Quarterly Portfolio Reconciliations - At a minimum a quarterly reconciliation will be completed
 where Client and Supplier will review all accounts in inventory during a specified period and ensure 100%
 of the customer data is reconciled between the Supplier’s and the Client’s data sources. Any discrepancies
 will be documented, and Supplier will provide resources to correct within 72 hours of notice. (Client
 reserves the right to request this more often if they believe there are inventory issues.*
 

 In addition all new and existing contracts have been updated to include Afterpay's Global Data Processing addendum in all Master Service Agreements.
 

 In addition Regional Collections Mangers are responsible for ensuring data reconciliations are completed at least quarterly as per the Collections & Support Agency Oversight Framework. The scope of the reconciliations includes: File transfer accuracy, Root Cause Analysis & Remediation.  
 

 A new role has been created in the Credit Risk Team for a Collections Data Analyst (Global Reconciliations) to support the Regional Collection Managers with completing this. The successful candidate is due to start on 14th September.

column4: 8.2 Periodic review of journals
column5: Financial Control to implement a periodic review of journals to ensure posting and approval by the same individual are on an exception basis and supported by a documented approval, including a rationale.
column20:
General Ledger Policy clause 4.5 covers the reporting and review of journals posted and approved by the same team member and will be finalised in September (see draft clause below). It includes report and review requirements.
 

  The Finance Systems team is updating the approach to system based reports and will include the "Journal Posting" report so that it is produce monthly and issued to the appropriate reviewer (Group Financial Controller). The "Journal Posting" report is currently produced manually (see sample).
 

 Draft Clause 4.5:
 

 To ensure finance assertions of completeness, accuracy, valuation and disclosure are met, manual journals are to be posted and reviewed by different Finance Team members. In practice, the posting and approval of manual journals are segregated ensuring segregation of duties for journal posting and approval. From time to time, approval and posting of journals may need to be completed by the same finance team member (ie: absence of approver). In these instances a retrospective review and approval is to be completed, documented and provided to the Group Financial Controller. This documentation is to include the reason why the posting and approving of the journal was completed by the same team member.
 

 Ensuring the process is followed a monthly report listing all journals posted and approved by the same individual will be issued by the Finance Systems Team. This report will be reviewed by Group Financial Control to ensure the appropriate review and approval documentation has been provided.

column4: Establishment of appropriate controls
column5:
A decision is required for the controls to meet regulatory expectations in Australia. I.e. Implement stronger preventative controls, Implement detective controls only, Implement preventative and detective controls. 
 Option 1 - Implement stronger preventative controls 
 Create new Registration Controls which identify linkages to customers with previous accounts including prior written off/hardship/suspended accounts in AU to close off the vulnerability risk.
 

 Stronger duplicate account control i.e. alternative logic. (see appendix 1 of the attachment for current and potential alternative logic) The proposed alternative logic solution will have the addition of an IP address linkage at account registration.
 

 Option 2 Implement detective controls only
 At Hard ID stage - when the customer submits their information, complete a check for duplicate profiles (planned - Sept/Oct)
 

 Monitor accounts for order amounts and/or balances > $3,001 and formalise a duplicate account closure process to remediate and correct balance/account status.
 

 Option 3 - Preventative and detective controls - Recommended
 Option 1 & 2 completed in full.

column4: Scope and implementation approach to solutions
column5:
Decision to be made i.e. Roll out solutions in Australia only, Roll out Australia first and plan for global roll out (subject to control performance in Australia), Global Roll Out
 

 Option 1 - Roll out solutions in Australia only. Define, develop and implement product control solutions to meet Australian regulatory expectations only
 Option 2 - Roll out Australia first and plan for global roll out (subject to control performance in Australia i.e what is decided for Option 1 above) - Recommended
 Define and develop product readiness globally, but phase roll out with initial focus on Australia first. Assess performance and then decide for implementation
 Option 3 - Global roll out Define, develop and implement product control solutions to meet the needs of all regions

column4: Financial Hardship - Update Training & Standard Operating Procedures
column5:
Update the Hardship Standard Operating Procedures & Training to include guidance on how to;
 a)provide a customer their options under the financial hardship program and be proactive in responding to each customer’s unique situation or vulnerabilities. (in line with the Afterpay commitment to assist people get back on track).
 b) explain to the customer what the payment arrangement is (re explain where necessary)
 c) the requirement to review the entire interaction from the start, so as to prevent asking the customer for repeat information. 
 d) assist CSO's to make relevant updates to the macros so customer are receiving appropriate communication for their circumstances
 e) clarify that a payment can be only moved once in 21 days. 
 f) update UK SOP for arrangement going over 180 days the CSO will need approval from the TL.
 g) escalation to T/l where a customer is vulnerable, mentally unstable or threatens self harm as a result of the CSO dealings
 

 Update the payment arrangement SOP with Improvement opportunities for the current process when dealing with a financial hardship customers include: 
 a) Providing the full balance at the time of the call, prior to setting up the arrangement so that the customer is fully informed when making choices about the terms 
 b) When a customer is setting up a PA and has several credit/debit cards or bank accounts, CSO to confirm what the primary card/account is and if the customer is ok to proceed. 
 c) For digital tickets, the reference at the bottom of the macro which tells that customer that if we do not hear back from them where more information is requested, the current macro has led to instances where the customer does not link this to the hold on their payments will cease at that time.
 d) make the macro content consistent in terms of the information that CSO’s provide to customer’s. For example will it include the order number Or include the order number plus the date the payment was moved to and from. 
 e) standardize the macros. For example where a move payments additional email is not generated (such as for payment arrangements) a table or information relating to dates moved to may be beneficial for the customer.
column20:

column4: Reinstatement /Written Off Accounts
column5:

column3: Cash - Afterpay - Customer Operations - Customer Service
column4: Remediating the incorrectly verified consumers
column5:
Steps need to be taken to: 
 Remediation options
 Below are a set of progressive treatments that can be applied to the concerned user segment to re-verify the users: 
 Automated steps
 Mark all concerned users as unverified
 Taking all available addresses on the file for a user and running soft checks in bulk in Frankie with a reduced threshold value for address match score
 Creating another list of addresses matched within a reasonable range of address match score (less than the threshold value above) and using human evaluators to match the addresses to mark them as matched and unmatched.
 For remaining users, ask for an update on the address and send them for re-verification flow for just the address, giving them a few reminders before freezing the account completely.
 Manual remediation
 For the remaining users who are active and also failed to reverify through address update flow, send them for manual verification and set up a priority queue based on the activity history of the user.

Update the Business Resilience Policy and Standard, including suitable approval of the documents (e.g. Core for Policy, Core + 1 for Standard), with the following changes. Noted that additional changes from the annual review and uplift will also be included in the updated policy and standard.
 

 * Update policy to include the principle that ‘business units with critical business processes should consider degradation or failure of critical outsourced third parties as part of their plans and testing where appropriate’ (or similar language) (management action plan reference: #2)
 * Update the policy to reference (and explain the interactions with) the existing crisis communication plans/processes, such as Data Security (Incident Response Plan), Physical Safety/Security (Crisis Management Plan) and Product/Service Operational Events (SEV process) (management action plan reference: #12)
 * Update the standard with the definition of RPO, and that RPO should be used as one of the metrics of a successful DR test (management action plan reference: #8)

column4: Block Business Resilience guidance enhancements
column5:
Update the Business Resilience guidance, including approval from the Head of Business Resilience (Hunter Francoeur) with: 
 

 * Update the Practical Disaster Recovery guide provided to engineering teams to include guidance on planning for and testing for business units with critical business processes who have critical outsourced third parties (management action reference: #2)
 * Update the Practical Disaster Recovery guide provided to engineering teams to include guidance on when and how to perform a post-mortem so any testing identified deficiencies should be addressed and reported (management action reference: #11)
column20:
Practical guide (https://docs.google.com/document/d/1xUCl-FSYqmUi-8LnLeQiMq9zD1Q4oHh7lBKSyYUtMYI/edit?usp=sharing) has been updated to include: 
 * Are you able to run the DR test in conjunction with your upstream and downstream dependencies? Testing with dependencies can include testing with critical vendors and 3rd parties
 * Some examples of the types of events you should consider: Failure of a dependency including failure of a critical vendor or other 3rd party 
 * After you’ve completed the test please hold a postmortem as an opportunity to improve the DR plan. 
 * In addition to a final status at least one artifact (and preferably more than one) is needed to consider a DR test complete. In order of preference::

column5:
1. Training controls are in place for the delivery, attendance tracking, and reporting of vulnerable customer training through our HRIS, Workday, and is managed by Block compliance. CS leadership to ensure that all CS employees complete the training annually. 
 2.Vulnerable Customer procedures will be developed by CS Talent Enablement teams (Content Operations) in partnership with regional compliance teams to outline the expected approach and process to vulnerable customer conversations. 
 3. CS will measure against established procedures and interactions with vulnerable customers through the Quality Assurance (QA) process
 4. CS will pursue options to record and track vulnerable customer interactions and complaints through the CS Governance process, sponsored by CS Intl leadership. We expect this will be a long term effort that will require engineering support and likely ownership from other teams in CS such as complaints

column20: February 21, 2025: GCMT reviewed the revised FCRA Policy [01-22-25] in go/policy. The 'Suspicious Personal Identifying Information' category was not updated to reflect the same detailed format as the 'Potentially Suspicious Activity & Documentation' and 'Unusual Use of, or Suspicious Activity Related to, the Covered Account' categories on pages 18-19. GCMT follow-up sent to the business.
column25:
February 24, 2025: The business created the Fair Credit Reporting Act (FCRA) Policy which includes requirements for detecting, preventing, and mitigating identity theft- 
 

  1. Page 16 outlines the requirement for-
  a. A periodic risk assessment to identify covered accounts.
  b. Program Administration Methods-
  i. Approval from the Board of Directors or an appropriate committee; 
  ii. Involving a designated Employee in the oversight, development, implementation and administration of the Program;
  iii. Training employees, as necessary; and
  iv. Exercising oversight of service provider arrangements. 
 

  2. Pages 17 highlights-
  a. Red Flags categories:
  i. Alerts, Notifications, or Warnings from a Consumer Reporting Agency; 
  ii. Suspicious Documents;
  iii. Suspicious Personal Identifying Information;
  iv. Suspicious Account activity; and
  v. Notices from customers, victims of identity theft, Law Enforcement authorities, or other person regarding possible identity theft in connection with accounts held by Cash App.
  b. The detection of Red Flags for new and existing accounts, and mitigation of identity theft such as monitoring, customer contact, and closing accounts.
 

  c. Page 18-19 provides additional examples for Alerts, Notifications, or Warnings from a Consumer Reporting Agency; Suspicious Documents; and Suspicious Account Activity. 
 

 Note: Although the Identity Theft Program requirements are in the FCRA Policy, a written Identity Theft Prevention Program has yet to be established. The business opened an issue, #1194106, to address the gap. Therefore, no additional tracking by GCMT for a written Identity Theft Program is required.
 

 The management action plan as it pertains to a Red Flags framework is complete.

column25:
June 14, 2024: The Cash App Lending Collections Policy and Notion Bankruptcy Procedure are now aligned regarding Legal escalations. Lending Compliance and Lending Operations collaborated to update the Bankruptcy Procedure:
 

 When a customer informs CCO that they are in bankruptcy, CCO agents are instructed to send a Quick Text (QT) message requesting the customer to securely upload bankruptcy documents. Refer to the CCO Notion Bankruptcy Procedures > Overview and ‘US: Cash App {Borrow - Bankruptcy NMI}’ QT. 
 

 When bankruptcy documents are received, Lending Operations will notify the Manager on Duty (MOD) or Escalations. MOD or Escalations will notify Legal of the case. Lending Operations will await Legal acknowledgment before proceeding with the case. Refer to ‘Workflow After We Receive Contact Info or Docs’ > Steps 1-4.
 

 When customers communicate that they have attorney or legal representation, CCO agents must seek direction from Lending Product Counsel and notify the Manager on Duty (MOD) before further proceeding with collection efforts. Refer to the CCO Notion Bankruptcy Procedures > ‘Header’ and ‘Attorney’s Contact information’ section. 
 

 This management action plan is complete.

column25:
July 9, 2024: Lending Eng updated the system behavior to meet the SCRA requirements: 1) Confirmed SCRA requests will be refunded the setup and overdue charges on loans originated prior to the SCRA effective date and are still active/open as of the SCRA effective date. 2) Loans originating on or after the SCRA effective date will be provided with MLA benefits. column4: Regularly scheduled OFAC screening did not occur for Cash Pro business accounts
column5:
A manual rescreening process will occur to ensure that all accounts that upgraded to C4B prior to completing IDV are rescreened every 30 days after IDV until the long term fix is implemented (see below). Once an account is manually rescreened, it will be incorporated into Cash App's 30 day rescreen cadence and will not have to be manually screened again. This will commence no later than June 23, 2023.
 

 Informational purposes: The long term fix will require that all accounts upgrading from personal to business must pass identity verification before a successful upgrade. This will go live in Q3.
column20: May 9, 2024: Further information required. Management action plan received does not adequately address the finding. Issue and action plan reopened. Stakeholder to provide new expected remediation date.
column25: June 20, 2024: On June 12, 2024, Cash for Business (C4B) Borrow accounts were updated to repayment only; C4B may no longer access Borrow. Past Borrow records are available for customers to view only. The C4B customers were notified of the change on June 5, 2024. C4B customers who attempt to use Borrow will receive an in-app notice informing them that the product is only available to personal accounts. As the identified issue was concerning OFAC screening for C4B with Borrow, and Borrow is no longer available to C4B, this issue is now closed.

column25:
November 27, 2024: As a third-party sender, Square created the “Square ACH Risk Management Program'' to provide a baseline overview of its risk management program expectations.
  - Square will annually engage a third-party audit firm to complete a risk assessment. Gaps and findings identified from the annual ACH assessment are shared with ACH stakeholders for review, approval, and if applicable, entered in Block’s issue management process for remediation.
  - Originator due diligence and monitoring will be completed to assess originator risks such as the nature of ACH activity, establishing exposure limits, and AML controls (e.g. Know your customer, OFAC screenings, account reviews).  Caelen Schreiber
 3:21 PM (1 hour ago)
 to me, Samantha, Keith, Alex
 

 Hi Donna,
 

 I am writing this email to confirm that item ID 1029764 Open The Underwriting guide can be updated to align with credit decisioning practices. [Square Banking Credit Card - US ] in Protect has been resolved as of 07/31/2024.

column25:
Received an email from Issue Owner William Matthews on February 6, 2024 with attached SOP Section and Corresponding Asana Tasks (google document) outline the CAI MAP Compliance Response: 
 

 CAI MAP Compliance Response:
 The root cause of the issue lied in the handover document that was provided to the individual covering. 
 

 The document stated “See SOPs. The scheduled dates are in Asana in the Communications and Advertising projects”
 

 The individual did not go through the SOPs but instead relied exclusively on the scheduled dates in Asana, a task management tool. Not all tasks however were included in Asana. 
 

 In order to eliminate this point of failure in the future, CAI Compliance is implementing a new procedures in its SOPs,
 

 Coverage of Designated Persons
 During absences of designated persons, each task to be completed should be appropriately recorded in a tool with the due date and the covering person assigned as the DRI. If a task can not be completed in time, a rationale should be entered as to the reasons why. 
 

 We are also ensuring that the SOP tasks have a matching Asana task which has been done as shown by the screenshots showing the one to one mapping.

column4: REF 2
column5:
CERT acknowledges the current limitations of the Google Sheets tracker our team uses to manage Financial Ombudsman Service (FOS) customer complaints for cases originating from the United Kingdom (UK). Moreover, CERT was well aware of these limitations prior to engaging in this review process with Block Global Compliance Monitoring and Testing (GCMT), including engaging with partners across Cash App to devise and implement considerable complaints management tooling improvements. Since filing a Jira with them on April 19, 2023, CERT has worked closely with Cash Business Systems (CBS) in the development of improvements to Cash App’s customer relationship management solution Cash Force 1 (CF1), including moving all FOS customer complaints cases out of Google Sheets and into CF1. That project continues apace; given its large scope and complexity, we anticipate meaningful improvements to FOS management within the next 9-12 months.
 

 In the interim, CERT has implemented manual stop gaps within the Cash App UK FOS Tracker: 
 - Record due dates of multiple requests from the FOS in a multi-line text / word wrap cell, thereby ensuring a centralised listing of dates and mitigating the risk of missing deadlines. The autopopulated "Date Response Due" field, will be amended to 10 business days from the date of initial complaint response, instead of 15 bsuiness days. 
 - Instructed the Internal Complaints Specialists to use the date of FOS complaint closure as the "Date Resolved" 
 - Instructed the Internal Complaints Specialists to avoid using the "Date Holding Letter Sent" and "Date Response Due > 15 days" fields in the tracker as these fields are not applicable to FOS complaints
 - In lieu of a field to identify remediation in the complaint form, CERT has instructed Internal Complaints Specialists to use the “Disputed” and “Refunded” fields as substitutes, which has proven effective
 

 As part of the long term solution, CERT will continue to collaborate closely with partner teams across Cash App, especially CBS, to develop, test, and implement major tooling improvements to our complaints management program, including moving FOS complaint tracking and management out of Google Sheets and into CF1.
 

 in our April Monthly Report on UK complaints, we included additional language to clarify FOS SLA requirements, writing “The FOS dictates the response due date for each complaint they receive from Cash App customers.”

column4: The firm is ensuring that payments are in line with expectations through the Continuous CRR model
column5:
"The Firm acknowledges the comments from the Skilled person. The Firm has intentionally chosen to only review sellers after they have taken a certain amount of transactions because this allows agents during manual review to have sufficient information to evaluate and make a solid judgement. This was true under all of our programs: legacy CRR, legacy KYC, new CRR-based KYC, aCRR. Effectively the Firms position is that if a seller has done minimal processing that they pose minimal risk.
 

 The Firm acknowledges the comments from the Skilled person. The firm is ensuring that payments are in line with expectations through the Continuous CRR model, where payment volumes, sizes, and timing is compared against other sellers in their location and business type. It cannot be done within aCRR, since aCRR runs during onboarding, where transactions have not actually occurred yet. .
 The intention of aCRR is not to surface all sellers for review but to focus on the sellers who pose elevated risk to SEL. The objective of SEL's CRR process is to identify those that pose the greatest risk, but more importantly to distill the risk of certain factors at onboarding. If a seller poses sufficient risk on these factors alone, SEL evaluates them and has chosen to draw this line at the top 1%. However, the aCRR score will be consumed downstream in a second model called cCRR, currently in pre-production. Thus the risk observed at activation via aCRR is propagated to subsequent monitoring. Since cCRR will begin evaluating sellers upon their first day, it will quickly leverage aCRR score in combination with transaction activity to identify risk with greater precision than aCRR alone. Thus we choose to only evaluate the top 1% at activation time, knowing that the other sellers will be captured with greater confidence subsequently."

column25:
July 8, 2025: The business launched the continuous Customer Risk Rating (cCRR) model v2 on May 19, 2025. During model training, the business tested Borrow features that are listed in the ‘CashApp cCRR v2 Model Specs Tables’ > ‘all_features_used_in_training’ tab. The final cCRR model v2 includes the ‘ratio of the customer’s average weekly Borrow Repayment total transaction count in the past 14 days relative to the weekly average from the customer’s historical behavior in the past three months.’ Refer to the ‘Cash App cCRR vs Model Spec Tables’ > ‘final_model_selected_features_01_07_tune_25’ tab > Row 552. 
 

 Model design included a training period of active Cash App accounts between January 1, 2020 - March 31, 2024. The model assessed an account’s AML risk by reviewing the account’s creation date and any negative action date to capture a variety of behaviors and aid model development. For model design and specification details, refer to the Cash App - US cCRR v2 System Design and Model Spec documents.
 

 All Cash App accounts receive a cCRR score seven days after account creation and every seven days for any attempted money movement within the preceding 30 days. The score is formed on the account level which links customer information by SSN for a holistic view. The scores will assign a risk level of high, medium, and low. Refer to the Cash App - US cCRR v2 System Design and Model Spec.
 

 Account scores resulting in a high risk level will prompt an EDD review. The EDD team holistically reviews the customer’s account such as customer information (adverse media, criminal records, PEP status, etc.), connected activities, linked bank information, and transactions analysis including Borrow activity (payment sources, reasonable customer activity, unusual velocity, etc.) Refer to the EDD Walkthrough example.
 

 This management action plan is complete.

column25:
As of July, 7th, the 314(a) procedure reflects how lending handles 314(a) requests with its bank partner FEB. See section titled "Cash App Lending Products": Cash App Lending Products
 Our 314(a) information sharing process for Cash Lending products is fully automated and occurs on a bi-weekly basis. The process is as follows:
 Data Transmission: First Electronic Bank (FEB) transmits a CSV file directly from the Financial Crimes Enforcement Network (FinCEN) containing 314(a) subject lists to Block via SFTP. This encrypted file is sent to a designated FEB folder location (toFEB/FinCEN).
 Matching Criteria: Block screens all the accounts of all Cash App Lending borrowers against the 314(a) subject list provided by FEB. Matches are identified based on the following criteria:
 Name + Date of Birth (DOB) + Zip Code; or
 Full Nine-Digit Social Security Number (SSN)
 Report Generation: Block generates an external report for transmission back to FEB, via SFTP, with the results of any matches, if there are no matches the report is still generated with 0 results. 
 External Report Details: The external report sent to FEB includes the following data points for each identified match: Tracking number, date of search, match method, customer’s full name, account number, account open date, account status, SSN, DOB, street, city, state, zip code, and country. 
 Record Retention: An internal report of all matches is stored securely within an S3 bucket for internal record-keeping and review.

March 18, 2025: Cash Lending OFAC reporting to its bank partner, First Electronic Bank (FEB), occurs monthly via the “OFAC Periodic Screening Results” report submitted by Strategic Partnership. Data for the report is compiled from the data from the FEB Borrow Monthly OFAC Report.
 

  - The FEB Borrow Monthly OFAC Report provides a list of potential OFAC screening hits. The most recent report, February 2025, had two false positives results.
 

  - The most recent February 2025 OFAC Periodic Screening Results reported to FEB accurately reflects the searched records and false positives.
 

  - The business also implemented a weekly BSA report which provides the ‘Number Borrow Customers w/o 7d OFAC / % Borrow Customers w/o 7d OFAC.” February weekly reporting and the most recent week, commencing March 10, 2025, shows that all Borrow Customers were screened for OFAC within 7 days of disbursing a loan (an FEB requirement).
 

 The Cash Lending OFAC Policy was archived. Cash Lending follows the block level AML/CTF/Sanctions-related Policies. The Global Sanctions Compliance Program Standard, Page 10, Section 4.2.4 requires a daily sanctions screening which exceeds FEB requirements. The daily screening is fulfilled at the Cash App account level. This process is completed for Cash App Lending in addition to OFAC screening prior to loan disbursement as demonstrated in the BSA Report (FEB requires screening within 7 days of loan disbursement).
 

 This management action plan is complete.

EU Compliance confirmed on the 5 October 2023 in the SEL Client Onboarding Results Matrix the below: 
 

 "We do not report discrepancies to Companies House instead, we will instruct the customer to either provide documentation that the individual that they have said is no longer a BO, or if they are unable to provide this, then we will instruct them to contact Companies House to update this information from their end."
 

 Per this conversation the action was then determined to be to update the SOP to include detail to note that the customer is instructed to contact Companies House where the information is not up-to-date. 
 

 Confirmed on 2/04/2024 that section 3.2 sub-section 4C of the SOP has been updated with the below: 
 

 c. If the Account holder has specified that the Beneficial Owner in question is no longer a Person of Significant Control of the business it is possible that they have not been updated in Companies House. 
 i. Combos -> Create Compliance SSP (or hotkey “V”)
 1. In the reason box, enter the following #NMI:KYC Manual Onboarding:unable to identify BO, requesting additional information for [NAME ] 
 2. Scroll down and click “Freeform Question YES/NO” in the box that appears and enter the following: Public records reflect [NAME] is still a Person with Significant Control of your business.If this information is incorrect, please contact Companies House to update this information. Once the information has been updated with Companies House, please confirm by responding “Yes” below.
 ii. In the onboarding case, Go to Combos -> Approve Activation and Close with the following note: 'Requesting additional information from SSP. Approving activation at this stage.'
 iii. Upon Receipt of the SSP Response (this response will be received in the SSP queue within GB and EU Compliance Review Workflow)
 1. Review the information provided in Companies House and to determine if the BO in question’s identity can be manually confirmed.
 a. If the individual's IDV information can be manually confirmed, Action the case. Use hotkey 'R' with the following Comment: #SSP:R Information provided is accurate and complete. Approving activation.
 b. If the individual’s IDV information needs to be updated, go to Actions > Update Legal Entity Node -> Select the appropriate entry that needs to be updated -> Update the necessary fields and add the reason: 'PII updated per: [INSERT METHOD USED TO VERIFY THE PII]'

column4: 4. forged documents, and documents in different languages requirement
column5: UK SOP will be updated to include information in regards to forged docs and docs in different languages.
column25:
Confirmed on 2/04/2024 that Section 5.2 Subsection 2(b) and 3 has been updated to include the process for escalating documents which appear to be forged/fake/altered. See below: 
 

 b. Verify proof of address.
 i. Check to see if the following are present:
 1. Letterhead
 2. Issued to the Account Owner, not to a business entity, or an account which is held by a business entity
 3. Account owner’s home/ residential address in the UK
 4. Issue date within the last 90 days.
 ii. Review the contents of the document to verify authenticity. If for any reason you feel the document is not authentic, or has been altered in any way, , search for an example/template of the document on the internet and compare the results. (Common features of an altered document include, but are not limited to: uneven fonts, misspelled and incorrectly spaced words, and blurry images)
 1. If you feel a document has been altered or is not authentic, but you do not have concrete evidence, reject the document, and give the merchant another opportunity to upload a different document.
 2. If you are confident the document has been altered or is not authentic (such as: you find a document on the internet with the same account number on a template website and/or showing someone else’s name) deactivate the account using the steps found in 3.b.
 

 3. Review SSP Request. (If this is the second manual IDV review and the documents are acceptable, proceed to step 3.d)
 a. If, after verification of the ID using Onfido, and review of the Proof of address, both the account owner’s ID and proof of address appear to be legitimate:
 i. Select Combos → Approve Activation, unfreeze and Close. In the Comment box, leave the following: #activate #unfreeze Manual IDV - ID and POA verified AO. Enabling card processing.
 b. If either or both documents are suspected to fraudulent, altered, or not legitimate in anyway: 
 i. Escalate to SAR team using the following general steps here ensuring the following boxes specific to this case action are are manually selected:
 1. POINT_OF_SALE
 2. FAKE_OR_ALTERED_IDENTIFICATION
 ii. Deactivate the merchant using the following steps:
 1.  Hotkey T (Deactivated and Close) -> eave the following comment in the Comment box: #DA #TOSV #KYC:Fail Manual IDV - Unable to verify AO.
 a. All comments regarding specific aspects of the documents suspected to be fraudulent should be left in the Compliance comment section only 
 2. Ensure the following checkboxes are selected:
 a. Notification of deactivation
 b. Is hardblocking 
 c. If either or both documents are invalid (but NOT clearly fraudulent/ altered): 
 1.  SSP -> Requests -> Review Requests -> Comment box: Manual IDV - [Proof of address, Government ID, or Both proof of address and government ID] is/are not valid documents for verification. Rejecting SSP and re-asking for documents.
 a. Optional step to better assist CS
 i. Leave detailed comments as to the rejection reason 
 2. Select which documents are being rejected via the following step:
 d. Reject Fields tick boxes -> Submit Second submission of documents if previously rejected:
 i. Repeat step 2. If documentation is legitimate, revert back to step 3.a.
 ii. If documentation is fraudulent revert back to step 3.b
 iii. If documents are again invalid, revert back to step 3.c.
 1. Repeat step 3.c a maximum number of 3 times. If after 3 attempts the merchant is unable to provide valid documents, deactivate the merchant using the following steps:
 iv. Hotkey T (Deactivated and Close) -> If fraudulent, leave the following comment in the Comment box: Manual IDV - #DA #TOSV #KYC:Fail Manual IDV - Unable to verify AO.

column25:
Closed on 02.09.2025-
 GCMT validated Issue ID 1048258 and Action Plan ID 1068118 on 2.9.2024. See below for details. No exceptions were noted. GCMT concludes issue and risk has been remediate. 
 

 

 GCMT notes the following: 
 1. The ATO Square Loan SOP (Issue ID 1068118 Copy of ATO Square Loan SOP - att a) was updated to reflect the new manual notification process. See page 16/22 where #2 was added. 
 “Once the loan is written off, SFS will notify the ATO risk agent that they can remove the freeze and debit block. 
 If the Settlement Write-off has a “Good Bank ATO” tag, the seller will not receive an automated cancellation email. 
 Ops will need to manually send the seller a {Loans - Good Bank ATO Write Off} email once the plan has been written off. “
 

 2. Agents were trained- 
 Per Lauren McCaughey (SFS - Strategy & Operations), she held a meeting with Nicholas Padilla to discuss this new procedural update (see meeting screenshot on 12/5/2023 - Issue ID 1068118 - image - meeting invite.png - att b) 
 Also, see training deck slide 28/33 which shows the updated procedure, Issue ID 1068118 - Copy of Square Loan ATO - Ref slide 28 - att b
 

 3. Legal approval of loan cancellation macro
 Per Lauren McCaughey (SFS - Strategy & Operations), Legal approved the Loan Cancellation email marco - for this manual process of sending an email once the plan has been written off. See JIRA ticket https://block.atlassian.net/browse/SFSLR-756 , where Legal Counsel Gavin Murphy approved email marco on 12/01/2023.
  Issue ID 1068118 [SFSLR-756] Good Bank ATO Write Off Macro - Jira.pdf - att c
 Attached marco that was approved is here- 
 Issue ID 1068118 - Copy of Loans {Good Bank ATO Write Off} - macro - att c
 

 4. GCMT held a walkthrough with Nicholas Padilla & Benitez Jones to discuss this new procedural update on 2/7/2024. 
 Meeting Invite:Walkthrough meeting with Nick Padilla and Benitez Jones.png
 

 5. GCMT received a population of SFS loans that had a Good Bank ATO tag within scope period review 12/12/2023 to 01/31/2024 from Lauren McCaughey on 02.06.24. Lauren stated that there was only one Merchant that had a Good Bank ATO write off within 12/12/2023 to 01/31/2024.
 Population of 1: Population of Good Bank ATO.png
 

 6. GMCT performed testing of the updated manual process of sending an email if it’s a Good Bank ATO. Testing resulted in no exceptions in the manual process of sending an email when the loan is tagged with Good Bank ATO within the scope period of 12/12/2023 to 01/31/2024. 
 

 Testing -Good Bank ATO within scope period 12/12/2023 to 01/31/2024

column20: GCMT reviewed evidence which shows the application used publishes a metric every 5 minutes that determines the oldest Unscreened Customer in seconds (by vendor). The Foundational Compliance Engineering team use the metric and create a Monitor that alerts the OnCall if that metric gets above 72,000 (20 hours * 60 minutes/ hour * 60 seconds / minute). The evidence GCMT reviewed, showed the dashboard was set to '20 hours'. GCMT has stored the evidence in the Remediation folder for the Adverse Media, Sanctions and PEP Screening Review.

column4: REF 1B
column5: Increase QA on complaints language and guidance and training on missed complaints opportunities (i.e. acknowledging complaints) by the staff.
column20:
As of Sep 2023, CERT has created a IE feedback loop workflow process to provide feedback on complaint handling with PSS Messaging leads, BPO Messaging leads and Messaging Advocates to promote growth and learning. 
 

 The feedback collected by IE complaint handlers includes identifying the following errors: if a complaint was incorrectly routed, if the complaint was acknowledged but was not flagged, if the advocate did not capture the customer’s email, if the complaint was flagged without
 acknowledging complaint.

column4: REF 1C
column5: To align with current practices and the public Support Complaints Process Article of providing a customer with an update within 3 working days if Cash cannot resolve the complaint within 3 working days, update the IE Complaint Handling Procedure. CERT will work to ensure that the information shared to our customers in this resource is aligned with the SLA information contained in our supporting procedure documents.
column20:
Cash Complaint Operations Team (used to be CERT) have deprecated separate documentation for the Ireland complaints program as the business has sunsetted the Ireland pilot program.
 

 Should any complaints come through within the same country, Cash Complaint Operations Team will follow the UK process. The team has updated the complaint SLA information in the Cash UK complaint program document to correspond to the public-facing customer information on complaint SLA's. The desired result is the same; the information has been successfully reconciled. CCOT simply fixed the issue for internal documentation rather than external documentation.
 GCMT noted that the documentation of the requirement to provide acknowledgement of the complaint if the complaint cannot be resolved within 3 working days. Within 15 business days of receipt of the complaint, a final response will be given to the complainant. In the exceptional event that a final response cannot be sent within 15 business days, a holding letter is to be sent instead (within 15 business days of the receipt of the complaint). This is consistent to the public Support Complaints Process Article.

column4: 1A
column5: Tier 1 and Tier 2 complaints will be escalated to Complaints Management and have an acknowledgement and a final resolution sent to each complainant from Salesforce under the new program.
column20:
In the previous program, if the frontline has not been able to resolve a complaint to the customer’s satisfaction, the customer is referred to the ‘Complaint Process Support Article’ and provided with a ‘Complaints (Pre-Complaint Information) Macro’ to file a formal complaint with Square. This escalation can be completed by the customer directly sending their complaints to the complaints-au@squareup.com or the customer has the option for the agent to escalate to the AU inbox on the customers behalf.
 

 In the new complaints program, frontline advocates no longer need to ask sellers if they would like to file a complaint. If they identify a complaint during an interaction, they should attempt to resolve it immediately. In some cases, this may not be possible and the complaint should be escalated as needed.
  In all complaints cases, the advocates must flag the complaint and categorize it as Tier 1, or 3 or 3-Escalated where Tier 1 represents the greatest risk to Square and Block, while Tier 3 represents lower- risk complaints.
 

 Tier 2 Complaints will ONLY be flagged by SAS and Complaints Management. Frontline Advocates will only flag Tier 1 & Tier 3 Complaints.
 

 Tiers 1 and 2 will be escalated to Complaints Management team for review and resolution. Automatic acknowledgement emails will be sent once a complaint has been filed for these complaints. The Complaints team will follow up after review and provide final resolution. 
 

 Refer to the Square Complaints Guide and Training linked.

column4: 1B
column5: For non-agency complaints, complaint responses will be sent from Salesforce. This process will be documented in the AU Complaints Runbook.
column20: As the Runbook is being finalised, DRI has provided the relevant section detailing the process to ensure responses to sellers that email the complaints-au@ email address need to be sent through Salesforce. Snippet of the runbook is attached.

column4: 1C
column5:
Under the new complaints program, Square will have new form tooling on Salesforce which will address the alignment/accuracy of complaint case dates such as: 
 - initial complaint date received from first point resolution as the "initial complaint date" and 
 - the date when formal final responses are sent as the "resolution date"
column20:
The Flag a Complaint form in Salesforce has been updated to include the following fields: Initial Date of Complaint, Date of Initial Acknowledgement, Closed Date and Reviewed Date. Refer to the screenshots of the Flag a Complaint form for the updated fields. 
 The definitions of the various date fields has been documented in Block Global Complaint Program for clarity. As per the Block Global Complaint Program, the “Closed Date” signifies that the complaint has been fully addressed, investigated and Final Response has been sent to the complainant. It indicates that all necessary actions have been taken and the complaint case has concluded. 
 The "Initial Complaint Date" is the date a complaint is received via a complaint channel, regardless of whether it is immediately escalated to the business unit of review. The complaint date is Day 0 of the complaint SLA. 
 The "Review Date" is the date the Complaint Managers review the complaint to ensure that all necessary actions have been taken and data is accurate. 
 Square AU Complaints Management Program has been modified to align with Block's Global Complaint Program.

column4: 2A
column5: The requirement to flag the complaint on Salesforce where expressions of dissatisfaction will be communicated within the updated training and guidance of the new Square complaints program.
column20:
Further context: The Block Global Complaint Policy defines a complaint as "An expression of dissatisfaction or grievance (oral or written) by an individual, business entity, or someone authorized to act on their behalf, with a Block product, process, service, decision, vendor, or interaction regardless of validity. Complaints are more than just the ‘words’ but also are identified by the tone of and intention behind the customer outreach, as well as sentiments of a breach in the business’ responsibilities Complaints may also include allegations that Block engaged in wrongful or unfair conduct." 
 As per the training materials, Frontline advocates are now required to flag all seller dissatisfaction independent of any trigger words/phrases or taxonomies that may or may not have been used. All complaints which meet the definitions outlined in the Global Block Complaints policy must be flagged.

column4: 2C
column5: In the new program, a complaint will not be escalated based on customers' explicit requests to make a complaint. Only Tier 3 complaints will automatically resolve. All other tiers will require review by the Complaints Management team.
column20:
In the new complaints program, APAC Complaints marked as ‘Tier 3’ will not be escalated to the CM team. Should the customer require further response or where complaints requiring escalation to the CM team, the complaint must be marked as Tier 1, Tier 2, or Tier 3 - Escalated, depending on the severity of the complaint. 
 Frontline advocates must attempt resolution for all tiers of complaints and has always been an expectation. 
 

 This process is documented and training has been provided to the frontline advocates. Refer to pages 38 and 39 of the SQ Complaints Management training 2024 for the APAC Frontline Advocates process.

column4: 2B
column5: The requirement to refer the customer to the Complaints Process Support Centre Article and/or send an email using the Complaints {Pre-complaint Information} macro will be communicated to frontline advocates in the updated training and guidance. We anticipate these new tooling and process solutions will resolve these concerns.
column20:
In the new complaints program, frontline advocates will only provide the seller with the complaints policy if the seller would prefer to put their complaint in writing. A complaint will not be escalated based on customers' explicit requests to make a complaint. All frontline advocates are still required to attempt to resolve a complaint. 
 

 This process is documented and training has been provided to the frontline advocates. Refer to pages 39 of the SQ Complaints Management training 2024 for the APAC Frontline Advocates process.

column4: 2D
column5: Complaints Management will receive updated guidance on the requirements for storing complaint documentation and will ensure the process outlined in the runbook is followed exactly. Further, the Complaints Management team will be more cognizant of the dates included on Salesforce complaint cases, ensuring they are accurate and on parity with other resources where included.
column20: Complaint Managers have received guidance to ensure that complaint dates must be accurate on the Flag a Complaint form on Salesforce and ensuring that it matches up with the dates on GRIT cases. DRI provided screenshot of communication.

column4: CBD seller account restrictions
column5:
SFS must update its CBD Procedure to reference that customers who are sent SSP for CBD documents are given seven days to respond prior to their POS account being frozen.
 

 SFS has identified that delayed deactivations by Square Compliance on sellers' POS accounts is creating a window in which savings account restrictions are lifted when they should not be. 
 

 SFS must update the logic for un-restricting savings to identify when POS is pending deactivation, and in such cases, keep the restriction in place.

January 22, 2025: Cash Product Bitcoin conducted an analysis of the BTC withdrawal timeframes and determined that >95% of the Priority transactions are completed in about 20 minutes. Therefore, the following updates were made:
 

 ‘Speed Selection’ screen (Image 1 - Speed Selection Screen): 
 The ‘Priority’ option was revised to reflect ‘~20 minutes’ (previously ~10 minutes).
 The network congestion status is shown at the top of the ‘Speed Selection.’ 
 Network Traffic will reflect the options below. (Image 2 - Network Traffic Language)
 Normal: “Demand is normal and transactions should process as expected” or
 Higher than usual: “Demand is higher than usual and may lead to higher fees and possible delays.”
    
 Customers may view the status of pending transactions by selecting the transaction in their Activity screen. The ‘Bitcoin withdrawal’ > Transaction details was also updated to display the Network traffic at the time of withdrawal until the transaction is completed; see Image 3 - Bitcoin Withdrawal Transaction Details. The network traffic status is removed from the screen once the transaction is complete; see Image 4 - Bitcoin Withdrawal Completed.  
 

 Due to >95% reliability for Priority withdrawals, and >99% reliability for Standard withdrawals, there were no changes to Cash Customer Operations (CCO) customer talking points. Customer cases regarding missed SLAs caused by network congestion are handled through customer education. The staff inform customers of bitcoin network traffic. There are no refunds since congestion is based on the bitcoin network, and not business processes. If missed SLAs are due to business activities (such as SEVs), customer cases will be reviewed for escalation and refunds. 
 

 This management action plan is complete.

column5:
Dispute Operations is raising the minimum standards for our agents target quality assurance scores. Agents today (as of 1/1/24) are required to have a minimum score of 90% on their quality assurance sample scores. column4: REF #1 - SIL Protected Disclosures Q1 2024 (Employment Counsel & Employee Relations)
column5:
Regulatory requirement:
 Section 16C(6) states that where a reporting person has disclosed his or her identity, and the person to whom this section applies has documented the reporting in the form of a transcript or minutes in accordance with subsection (4) or (5), as the case may be, the person to whom this section applies shall offer the reporting person the opportunity to check, rectify and agree by way of signature the transcript or minutes.
 

 Testing Results:
 The SIL Speak Up Policy does not include the transcript or minutes review process as deemed appropriate in accordance with the regulatory requirement.

column4: REF #1 - SIL Protected Disclosures Q1 2024 (Employment Counsel & Employee Relations)
column5:
Regulatory requirement:
 Section 16(3)(a) of the Protected Disclosures Act 2014 (Revised, Updated to 22 July 2023) states that where the identity of the reporting person or any other information referred to in subsection (1) is disclosed to another person in accordance with paragraph (a) or (b)(ii) of subsection (2), the reporting person shall be notified, in writing, before their identity or the information concerned is disclosed unless such notification would jeopardise—
 (i) the effective investigation of the relevant wrongdoing concerned,
 (ii) the prevention of serious risk to the security of the State, public health, public safety or the environment, or
 (iii) the prevention of crime or the prosecution of a criminal offence.
 (b) A notification under paragraph (a) shall include the reasons for the disclosure referred to in that paragraph.
 

 Testing Results:
 The SIL Speak Up Policy does not include the process for notifying the reporting person in writing before their identity is disclosed to another person as deemed appropriate in accordance with the regulatory requirement.

column20: Documentation has been submitted for closure by the Action Plan owner Michael Rihani on June 4, 2024.

column4: Bug Remediation
column5:
The incident summary initially provided to the GCMT team is further supplemented to clarify the identification of a failure in a multi-system synchronization, corrective action plan undertaken by several separate engineering teams (Bitcoin, Health and OS), and requisite updates. The following remediation actions have been and/or are in the process of being performed:
 1. Bitcoin Engineering Team: Produce report of number of customers impacted (completed on 03/14/2024)
 2. Health Engineering Team: Identify and fix the bug in cash-address (targeting completion on or before 5/1/2024)
 3. Health Engineering Team: Backfill in cash-address and propogation to downstream systems (targeting completion on or before 5/1/2024)
 Due to the complexity of engineering work and the cross-functional work of two engineering teams, an incrementally longer action completion due date is identified.
column20:
Response provided by Michael Rihani, CAP Owner: 
 

 The Trust Identity Engineering Team completed the backfill process in the cash-address system. This includes the propagation of corrected data to all relevant downstream systems. The successful completion of this task ensures that all systems are synchronized and operating with accurate data. This comprehensive approach mitigates any potential discrepancies and reinforces the consistency of our data management practices.

column4: Global Sanctions Team Enhancements
column5:
The Global Sanctions Team (GST) will take the following steps:
 

 Revise roles and responsibilities within GST to increase oversight over sanctions escalations carrying OFAC reporting obligations.
 

 Automate metrics within JIRA to capture (1) the total number of escalations pending review by GST, and (2) the total number of rejected and blocked reports filed with OFAC and corresponding dates of filing,(3) the date(s) the funds related to rejected transactions were refunded to customers and originators, and (4) the date(s) funds related to blocked transactions or accounts credited an OFAC designated blocked account.
 

 Incorporate OFAC reporting metrics into existing management reporting. 
 

 Develop Global Sanctions Regulatory Procedures which capture (1) roles and responsibilities of all teams involved in the OFAC reporting alert review, escalation and reporting process, (2) applicable OFAC reporting requirements and filing deadlines, and (3) OFAC reporting metrics maintained by the Global Sanctions Team.

column4: Implementation of Controls for Sponsored Cash Accounts
column5:
Cash App will implement due diligence procedures / controls in order to assess / understand the relationship between Sponsors and Teen users. This work and the timelines have been agreed and committed to by Product. The work will include the following controls to establish a Sponsorship: 
 The Teen DOB, provided separately by the Sponsor and the Teen, must match. 
 The Teen / Sponsor relationship, provided separately by the Sponsor and the Teen, must match. 
 There must be at least a 15-year age difference between the Sponsor and the Teen.
 The Sponsor must attest that he/she is the parent or guardian of the minor. 
 Update TOS to clarify the meaning of the parent or guardian relationship. 
 Work completion is expected by the end of Q1 2024

column4: Assessment of Risk-Based Decisions and Processes
column5:
1: Conduct an assessment of the below listed risk-based decisions and processes to ensure these are in line with Block’s AML/CFT strategy and risk appetite and UK local AML/CFT regulatory requirements,
 

 2: Formally document the following procedures/actions: (1) Customer Risk Rating Methodology; (2) Procedural steps to identify and AML risk assess merchants associated with higher-risk countries in relevant AML program documentation; (3) Agreed Service Level Agreements (SLAs) for SARs cases, Sanctions and Transaction Monitoring alerts. Updated relevant AML program documentation to document these agreed SLA timelines. Include these SLA timelines in AML operations reporting; (4) Risk-based criteria for the request and validation of SoW/SoF at onboarding for customers identified as PEPs in relevant AML program documentation; (5) Controls in place to prevent tipping off of ongoing investigations in relevant AML program documentation and operational procedures; (6) Criteria substantiating the risk based approach to PEP/Sanctions and Adverse Media screening outlined in the observation in relevant AML program documentation.
 

 3:Roll out these changes with appropriate communication and training to responsible individuals.

 2: Review and update Clearpay UK’s AML/CTF procedures to ensure they are consistent with regulatory requirements, expectations, and internal practices. This includes updating relevant AML program documentation to correctly state that adverse media screening is conducted during periodic review only.

column25:
Cash Lending Operations created the ‘Lending Confirmed Identity Theft Procedure.’ When a Level 3 (IDV was compromised and used on or to create a Cash App Account) or Level 4 (Compromised IDV and banking information used to create a Cash App Account) case with a lending product is identified, Cash App Risk ID Theft will escalate the case to Lending Operations.
 

 Lending Ops will review the account to confirm that the account has been denylisted. The account is then reviewed for open loans. No further action is required for accounts that have been denylisted as the loans will close after ~30 days. If the account has yet to be denylisted, the case will be escalated back to Cash App ID Theft for proper denylisting. Loans will not be voided or refunded in instances where the customers have filed chargeback with their bank on loan payments.
 

 GCMT has confirmed that procedures have been drafted but the tracker is not fully in use. Due to this, the action plan Action 1114944 Lending ID Theft Case Handling will be closed and a secondary action 1364504 has been created to capture the go-live functionality of the tracker and to allow GCMT to validate the implementation, not just the drafting of procedures. 
 

 This management action plan is complete.

column4: The control to detect and block Emulator devices is limited in its design due to data delays.
column5:
Out of the 18 exceptions reported:
 

 - 9 exceptions occurred because the client version used to sign-up was <4.23 which is when the attestation data fixes were implemented on the client-end. This population will naturally decrease as older client versions reach end of life, and as such Management will not take further action.
 

 - 7 of the reported exceptions were cases where the attestation data was received outside of the 3 hour window which is beyond the timeframe for action as defined by the control. Management will continue to monitor these exceptions to evaluate the risk associated with delayed data returned from the vendor and take further control measures as needed. 
 

 - 2 of the reported exceptions were due to delays in the attestation data being ingested by backend ML systems and making it available for the sign-up risk evaluation. This occurs approximately 0.04% of the time. We are proposing that this is an acceptable level of risk and don't recommend any actions to further mitigate this. 
 

 - Management acknowledges the risk associated with limitations of the current control design and not adequately identifying Emulators for iOS devices. Management plans to address this control design opportunity by May 1, 2025

column4: Perform lookback on all existing CAWL customers to collect full legal name from photo ID for rescreening purposes
column5:
Management has performed a lookback of CAWL's existing customers to address the points raised in the Audit observation. The lookback was completed on 3/13/2024 and all customers were rescreened for sanctions and PEPs.
 

 The lookback included a review of all CAWL customer accounts to collect the full legal name from the photo ID for sanctions screening. Of the 69 individuals who attempted identity verification, 57 individuals completed the process successfully, and 12 individuals did not. It was confirmed that for these 12 individual records, the accounts were either unverified (and did not have access to BTC100 features) or were duplicate test accounts. For the 57 successfully verified customer accounts, the full legal name was screened for sanctions and PEP on 3/13/24. No confirmed sanctions or PEP matches were identified.
 

 Currently there are no plans to expand the customer base or services offered under the CAWL entity, however when that changes, we will update the program, including evaluating existing and new vendors to ensure that all relevant customer identity data points are being collected and screened (where applicable)."

column4: Enhance the KYC program to monitoring and review at a customer level
column5:
1) Establish customer level risk rating across accounts connected by a single social security number.
 2) For verified accounts, perform holistic EDD at the customer level by reviewing accounts connected by device and SSN, and perform CDD on customers with red flag identity information (i.e. name mismatch across accounts) and linked instrument activity. 
 3) Implement selective adverse media screening for verified accounts using a risk based approach.
 4) For Restricted accounts, perform ongoing EDD on high risk accounts based on the Continuous Customer Risk Rating (cCRR) score.
 5) Update enhanced due diligence guidelines and establish training on how to perform customer level reviews across linked accounts associated with the same customer, including understanding customer level risk score, nature/ intended use of accounts, and executing customer level denylisting (across SSN connected accounts belonging to the customer).

Design and implement controls to collect and cross-check sponsor and minor relationship and age information in order to: (i) substantiate that the sponsor is the minor's parent or guardian; and (ii) ensure alignment with Cash App's Terms of Service. 
 

 Note: Luke Zhu updated the Action Plan Description on July 22, 2025 to ensure alignment with the documented issue. The previous version did not accurately reflect the underlying gaps identified. The revised description now clearly maps to the issue and remediation implemented.
column20: Action Plan Owner updated to Luke Zhu as previous owner is no longer with the company.
column25:
Brian Kearns on behalf of Luke Zhu: 
 

 On May 7, 2024, Cash App rolled Sponsor Attestation to 100%. This launched included, inter alia, the following changes to better substantiate the appropriateness of the relationship between a sponsor and its sponsored account(s):
 Independently collect relationship from the minor and the sponsor
 Require the minor to select their relationship with the sponsor: a) parent b) guardian
 Require the sponsor to select their relationship with the sponsor: a) parent b) guardian
 Independently collect the teen’s birthday from the sponsor.
 Require sponsors to see the full legal name used by the minor.
 Require sponsors to attest that they are the parent or guardian of the minor.
 Implement age and relationship based heuristics to deny sponsorships that may be inconsistent with our terms of service. Decline sponsorship if:
 DOB entered by the minor and the sponsor mismatch
 Relationship entered by the sponsor is “Parent” and the sponsor age is <28
 

 See attached evidence: Airtable screenshot, PRD and CRD.

column4: Conduct Review of Indepedent Model Validation of aCRR Model
column5:
The Financial Crimes Technology Model Governance team will conduct an independent model validation of Block Inc’s aCRR model. This validation will be included in Block Inc's 2022 Annual Model Validation Report. In accordance with Model Validation Policy & Procedures, the following components will be considered as part of this assessment: 
  Data Quality 
  Model Design 
  Model Implementation/Conceptual Soundness 
  Model Performance 
  Model Governance
 

 Additionally, a pre-implementation model validation checklist will be required to be completed prior to the launch of all machine learning models within compliance.

column4: Square Credit Card - Creation of Square Capital CMS program and/or inclusion of the Square Capital CMS program into the Block level CMS.
column5:
Celtic requires a formal, written, Square Capital Credit Card CMS program for the Square Credit Card. In the short term, the Compliance Governance Team intends to produce a Square Credit Card CMS Program document for Square Capital, LLC. which will inform the Square Capital Board of Managers as to roles and responsiblities for key stakeholders in connection with the CMS Program for the Square Credit Card as well as approval cadences and items for review. column4: Performing KYC on a Risk Based Approach
column5:
SEL acknowledges the comments above. 
 

 UK regulations do not specify that firms need to perform periodic validation of KYC on medium and low risk customers; it is an industry practice that has developed over time. As discussed during the review meetings, SEL is a low risk firm from an enterprise risk basis. SEL applies a risk based approach to performing KYC on its seller base and reviews High Risk customers on an annual basis. All customers are subject to ongoing monitoring. 
 

 A large part of SEL’s sellers are sole traders of small business and as such their information would not change on a regular basis. In addition, SEL has implemented a process for legal entities registered with Companies House using third party vendor DueDil, which generates alerts for review by SEL when information has changed since onboarding. These alerts are reviewed by the Compliance Operations team in the US. SEL will improve its documentation surrounding its decision not to conduct such reviews on its low and medium risk customers, and will pass this for formal risk acceptance at the Regulatory Governance Committee.

column4: Remove GPV as EDD trigger threshold
column5:
The Firm acknowledges the comments from the Skilled person. The FIrm has intentioanlly chosen to only review sellers after they have taken a certain amount of transactions because this allows agents during manual review to have sufficient information to evaluate and make a solid judgement. 
 This was true under all of our programs: legacy CRR, legacy KYC, new CRR-based KYC, aCRR. 
 Effectively the Firms position is that if a seller has done minimal processing that they pose minimal risk.

column4: Ensure payments align with expectations in cCRR model
column5:
The Firm acknowledges the comments from the Skilled person. The firm is ensuring that payments are in line with expectations through the Continuous CRR model, where payment volumes, sizes, and timing is compared against other sellers in their location and business type. It cannot be done within aCRR, since aCRR runs during onboarding, where transactions have not actually occured yet. .
 The intention of aCRR is not to surface all sellers for review but to focus on the sellers who pose elevated risk to SEL. The objective of Sel's CRR process is to identify those that pose the greatest risk, but more importantly to distill the risk of certain factors at onboarding. If a seller poses sufficient risk on these factors alone, SEL evaluates them and has chosen to draw this line at the top 1%. However, the aCRR score will be consumed downstream in a second model called cCRR, currently in pre-production. Thus the risk observed at activation via aCRR is propagated to subsequent monitoring. Since cCRR will begin evaluating sellers upon their first day, it will quickly leverage aCRR score in combination with transaction activity to identify risk with greater precision than aCRR alone. Thus we choose to only evaluate the top 1% at activation time, knowing that the other sellers will be captured with greater confidence subsequently.
