 In relation to Cash App, the Firm will conduct a detailed review of its AML/CTF/Sanctions Program in advance of providing the Cash
 App service outside of its testing program and will take the items outlined above into account when doing so. The Firm will make any
 required amendments to its Policies and Procedures as part of this process.
 Please note that, while Cash App has not been generally launched to customers or actively marketed, a limited extension of the scope
 of the testing population has occurred in order to enhance the testing process. In the course of May 2022, the number of Monthly
 Active Users was 892 of which 300 were Green Customers and 592 were Gold Customers. Gold Customers are those to which the
 Firm has applied full Customer Due Diligence and Green are customers to which the Firm has applied Simplified Due Diligence.
 

 CBI Finding 2 Action 1 02/08/22
 

 The contents of the Firm’s response are noted, however, the first paragraph of the Firm’s response would not appear to align with the
 action required (the same text was provided by the Firm in respect of action 1 of finding 1). This action specifically relates to the Firm
 reviewing and updating its AML/CFT Program and underlying AML/CFT procedures.
 In relation to the Cash App, the Central Bank notes the Firm’s comments in this regard. However, as part of this action, the Firm is
 required to review its entire AML/CFT & FS suite of compliance documentation and not just its procedural documents in respect of the
 Cash App.
 The Firm is required to review the details of this finding again and to provide an update on the progress of its remediation, in its next
 response.
 

 Firm Finding 2 Action 1 04/11/22
 

 The firm acknowledges that its initial response regarding this item included a duplication of information included in respect of action 1
 of finding 1. The firm has set out its progress regarding this action below:
 The Square AML/CTF/FS Program has been updated and it was approved at the 28th September SIL Board meeting.
 In particular the Firm would like to confirm the following updates have been made:
 1. GBP has been removed along with the detail on how the CRR model works and moved to a CDD procedure.
 2. Added in a link to all procedures for staff to be able to access procedures.
 3. Included a list of documents that a seller provides in the CDD procedure
 4. Linked bank accounts for Sellers can be across the EU and are only Euro currency; this represents that funds can cross
 borders, but do not go to/from high-risk countries.
 In addition, the firm will be providing separate program and procedure documents for each product Square (Seller) and Cash App. As
 will be detailed later in the specific findings, all of the SIL procedures will reflect consistency in the drafting, formatting and layout and
 are aligned to the AML Program. The following have been reviewed, updated and approved:
 ● SIL Square EEA KYC Procedure, approved by the SIL CCO on 28th October 2022.
 ● SIL Square EEA Suspicious Transaction Reporting Procedure, approved by the SIL CCO on 7th October 2022.
 ● SIL Square EEA Transaction Monitoring Procedure, approved by the SIL CCO on 12th October 2022.
 ● SIL Square EEA Screening Procedure, approved by the SIL CCO on 6th October 2022.
 The firm is still in the process of reviewing the Cash App AML/CTF/FS Program, in particular the firm will:
 1. Ensure that the differences between both IE Gold and IE Green products are described fully.
 2. Include details of the customers documentation required at onboarding in the Cash App KYC Procedures.
 3. The program will make reference to the KYC process.
 

 4. The risk assessment will be updated to reflect the possible cross-border transactions possible.
 The Cash App AML/CFT/FS Program and all underlying AML/CTF/FS Procedures will be updated as necessary and approved in
 advance of providing the Cash App service outside of its testing program.
 

 CBI Finding 2 Action 1 16/01/23
 

 The contents of the Firm’s response are noted. In relation to the Square (Seller) AML/CTF/FS Program, the Central Bank requests a
 copy of this document to be provided with the Firm’s next response. In relation to the Cash App AML/CTF/FS Program, the Central
 Bank requests an update in relation to the finalisation of this document (and underlying operational procedures) and also, an update
 on when the Firm expects the Cash App to be rolled out, outside of its current testing phase.
 

 Firm Finding 2 Action 1 16/03/23

 At this time, a date for making Cash App available outside of its current testing programme has not been confirmed as the Firm

 Firm Finding 4 Action 2 04/11/22
 

 The Firm has updated the STR Procedures for SIL Seller product to reflect the feedback above, references to high/low priority alerts have
 been removed as well as a reference to 90 days. The firm has removed the categorisation of high and low risk alerts and treats all alerts
 equally. However, if the firm were to suspect serious incidents of for example terrorist financing or human trafficking the MLRO would
 alert the FIU as soon as possible.
 The Firm is still in the process of reviewing the Cash App AML/CTF/FS Program including the Cash App STR Procedures, however the Firm
 would like to take this opportunity to advise the CBI that alerts will be treated equally and will not be considered as high/low priority. In
 addition, STR’s will be reported as soon as practicable. Similar to the Square product, if the firm were to suspect serious incidents of for
 example terrorist financing or human trafficking the MLRO would alert the FIU as soon as possible.
 The Cash App AML/CFT/FS Program and all underlying AML/CTF/FS Procedures will be updated as necessary and approved in advance of

 provided with the Firm’s next response.
 

 Firm Finding 4 Action 2 16/03/23
 

 The Firm considers serious incidents to be for example terrorist financing or human trafficking . If during the course of the investigation the
 analyst suspects serious incidents, for example terrorist financing or human trafficking, the analyst will escalate to the MLRO as soon as

 CBI Finding 4 Action 3 02/08/22 
 

 The contents of the Firm’s response are noted and the Central Bank requests an update on the progress of the remediation of this finding, in the Firm’s next response.
 

 Firm Finding 4 Action 3 04/11/22
 

 The Firm would like to take this opportunity to clarify the timeframe used for reporting STR’s to the Irish authorities. Alerts that are
 generated as a result of transaction monitoring rules will be reviewed within 90 days of alerting, however once a decision has been formed that the alerted activity is suspicious the Firm is committed to filing the STR as soon as practicable, within 24 hours of approval from the
 MLRO.
 The Firm has updated the SIL Square (formerly Seller) STR procedure to reflect the filing requirements of the CJA.
 The Firm is still in the process of reviewing the Cash App AML/CTF/FS Program including the Cash App STR Procedure. When updated, the
 STR procedure will reflect the Firm's approach to STR filing, which is as soon as practicable.
 The Cash App AML/CFT/FS Program and all underlying AML/CTF/FS Procedures will be updated as necessary and approved in advance of
 providing the Cash App service outside of its testing program.
 

 CBI Finding 4 Action 3 16/01/23
 

 The contents of the Firm’s response are noted. However, it remains the Central Bank’s view that a 90 day timeframe for the investigation of
 a suspicion of ML/TF is not in line with the provisions contained in the CJA 2010 (nor is it in line with the Central Bank’s AML and CFT
 Guidelines for the Financial Sector). The Central Bank requests the Firm to review this Action again and to re-consider its 90 day timeframe
 for the investigation of alerts/suspicions of ML/TF, prior to those alerts/suspicions being discounted or reported to the relevant authorities.
 

 Firm Finding 4 Action 3 16/03/23
 

 The firm would like to apologize for any confusion caused -the firm would like to clarify that the 90 days was for investigating unusual
 activity before a suspicion has been fully formed. Once a suspicion is formed the STR will be filed as soon as possible. The firm takes the
 approach that a transaction which appears unusual is not necessarily suspicious. We acknowledge that customers with stable and
 predictable transaction profiles may from time to time have transactions that are unusual for them. So the unusual is, in the first instance,
 only a basis for further enquiry, which may in turn require judgment as to whether it is suspicious.
 The firm has taken this opportunity to consider the time frames utilized and has recently changed the investigation period. When the TM
 alert is generated the analysts will have 30 days to determine whether further investigation is warranted. If the case is considered unusual
 the case will be sent to the STR investigations team where they will have 30 days for further investigation and to put forward a rationale
 relative to STR filing to the MLRO. If the STR investigation team recommends that a STR is required the recommendation is sent to the
 firm's MLRO. The MLRO will make a decision based on the investigation and the STR will be filled within 7 days. In Q4 2022 the firm filed 2
 STR's for IE sellers and 1 for an ES seller.
 

 CBI Finding 4 Action 3 13/04/23 
 

 The contents of the Firm’s response above, and the information provided by the Firm during the meeting of 4th April 2023, are noted and the
 Central Bank is closing this action. However, the Central Bank is of the view that the Firm should keep its timeline for the investigation of suspicions of ML/TF under review and the Firm should note that this area may be subject to further review during future supervisory engagements.

column4: Monitoring and Testing Program
column5:
To further clarify the Firm’s prior responses, as it may have caused confusion. Square operates a 3 LOD program, there is QC performed on the 1 LOD (Line of defence) operational activity, there is a 2 LOD monitoring and testing team that performs targeted risk based testing and IA provides independent IA annually, program document (F10.1.1 ) is being provided at this time.  This plan states what is tested and when it will be tested. The scope documents for each review will detail who the results of the review will be presented to.
 The document, ‘Quality Compliance Program EU Seller Compliance’ applies to the 1LOD QC only and does not reflect the 2LOD monitoring and testing program.  This document has been updated and is provided as evidence [Square Compliance QC] [EU] Quality Control - European Union.pdf.
 The Firm is providing the Global Independent Testing and Monitoring Program document which details who gets the final reports.
 The Firm is also providing the approved monitoring and testing plan (F10.1.2) for SIL.

 PF 10.1.1 Global Independent Testing and Monitoring Program and

 queues within the following areas:

column25:

Firm Finding 12 Action 2 06/07/22

 The Firm is in the process of enhancing the CRR model and the firm will review its CRR process to ensure a consistent approach across all
 customers.
 The Firm will take this item into account in the course of the overall review it will conduct in respect of its AML/CTF/Sanctions Program
 related to Cash App.
 

 CBI Finding 12 Action 2 02/08/22 
 

 The contents of the Firm’s response are noted and the Central Bank requests an update in respect of the Firm’s review of its CRR process, in its next response.
 

 Firm Finding 12 Action 2 04/11/22 
 

 The Firm has reviewed and enhanced the CRR model resulting in a consistent approach to all customers. The Firm would like to advise the CBI that Square performs a CRR at the time a merchant is onboarded, activation CRR (aCRR) and also throughout the lifetime of the
 merchant, continuous CRR (cCRR). The cCRR model, is unlike the one-time aCRR model, in that it will evaluate Financial Crime risk on a daily basis for all sellers using multiple factors/attributes. In addition, to the normal suite of signals, customer attributes. available to CRR models, Squares aCRR and cCRR models have at their disposal a wide range of signals that are used to feed into the risk score. Square has built a number of signals to address specific feedback, such as comparisons to business type benchmarks and out-of-pattern activity. The Firm would also like to clarify that there are different situations when Square will apply EDD to its Merchants. A Merchant may be deemed High Risk and therefore Enhanced Due Diligence will be completed, if certain thresholds are met, for example;
 1. Have an aCRR score that is considered High risk
 2. If there is a PEP connected to the Merchant this could be identified throughout the lifetime of the Merchant.
 3. If during the lifetime of the Merchant the cCRR score indicates high risk.
 4. Have a high risk merchant code, transacted a certain number of transactions and transacted a certain EUR of Gross Payment Volume (GPV)
 The rationale for requiring the Merchant to have transacted to certain volume/values is so that Square can better assess the Merchants
 transaction patterns.
 The Firm is still in the process of reviewing the Cash App AML/CTF/FS Program and all underlying AML/CTF/FS Procedures. As outlined in
 the Firm’s response of 6 July 2022, this item will be taken into account in the course of the ongoing review.
 The Cash App AML/CFT/FS Program and all underlying AML/CTF/FS Procedures will be updated as necessary and approved in advance of
 providing the Cash App service outside of its testing program.
 

 CBI Finding 12 Action 16/01/23
 

 The contents of the Firm’s response are noted. Could the Firm advise as to whether or not the changes to the Firm’s CRR model are
 documented (in the Square AML/CTF/FS Program or the Square (Seller) KYC/CDD Procedure (or elsewhere), and if so, if a copy of where
 this is documented could be provided with the Firm’s next response.
 

 Firm Finding 12 Action 2 16/03/23 
 

 The firm has documented the CRR model in its KYC Procedures which are attached as 12.2 FINAL SIL Square EEA KYC Procedures
 CBI 12 2 13/04/23

 considers this action closed. However, the Firm should note that this area may be reviewed further during future supervisory
 engagements.

 do not appear to be verified with any additional documentation. The Central Bank requests an update on the Firm’s review, in its next
 response.
 

 Firm Finding 12 Action 5 04/11/22
 

 The Firm has reviewed and updated the SIL Square EEA KYC Procedure to outline in more detail when and how both residential and
 business addresses are verified. Residential addresses are verified by requesting recent proof of residential address (utility bill or bank
 statement not older than 90 days). Business addresses are verified through the use of third party information on CRO. For sole traders,
 address is gathered at onboarding and maybe verified through applied risk-based methods after onboarding.
 Regarding Cash App, the Firm is still in the process of reviewing the Cash App AML/CTF/FS Program and all underlying AML/CTF/FS
 Procedures. As outlined in the Firm’s response of 6 July 2022, this item will be taken into account in the course of the ongoing review.
 The Cash App AML/CFT/FS Program and all underlying AML/CTF/FS Procedures will be updated as necessary and approved in advance of
 providing the Cash App service outside of its testing program.
 

 CBI Finding 12 Action 5 16/01/23
 

 The contents of the Firm’s response are noted. However, the Central Bank requests further detail in relation to address verification. The
 Central Bank requests that the Firm address the following:
 1. The Firm has advised “Business addresses are verified through the use of third party information on the CRO”. Is the Firm satisfied that
 the information maintained by the CRO is correct and up to date and that this information has already been verified?
 2. Could the Firm advise as to whether or not it intends to conduct BVD checks on all incorporated entities – including entities
 incorporated outside the State? If not, could the Firm advise as to how address verification is conducted for entities incorporated
 outside the State?
 3. The Firm has advised that for sole traders “address is gathered at onboarding and maybe verified through applied risk-based methods
 after onboarding”. Could the Firm provide further detail in relation to this statement? How is a sole trader’s address verified after
 onboarding?
 Further to the above, could the Firm provide an update (if available) in relation to the due diligence requirements for Cash App customers,
 including whether or not the Firm intends to apply due diligence thresholds to Cash App customers (please see Action 5 above for full
 details).
 

 Firm Finding 12 Action 5 16/03/23
 

 1. There is a legal requirement on companies to ensure that their registered office details are kept up-to-date with the CRO.
 Furthermore, under the Companies Act 2014, any document left at or sent by post to the place recorded at the CRO as the
 registered office of a company is deemed by law to have been left at or sent by post to the registered office of the company. For
 these reasons, the firm is satisfied that it is reasonable to rely on the information maintained by the CRO.
 2. The firm can confirm that BVD checks will take place, or similar checks using company registration databases, on all incorporated
 entities including entities incorporated outside the State as per the SIL KYC Procedures.
 3. The firm will request recent proof of residential address (utility bill or bank statement not older than 90 days) to verify the address
 of sole traders.
 The Cash App program document and accompanying procedures remain under review. As per the response in 2.1 the CCO will update the
 SIL Cash App Program document and accompanying procedures. The program document will be provided to the SIL Board in Q2 for
 approval with procedures also approved in Q2. Currently Cash App is not applying thresholds for the application of CDD as part of its
 ongoing testing program. This approach may be reviewed as part of the program and procedure update or at a later stage, provided all
 requirements and criteria for the application of such thresholds are met and the firm is satisfied that it is appropriate to do so.
 

 CBI Finding 12 Action 5 13/04/23
 

 The contents of the Firm’s response above, and the information provided by the Firm during the meeting of 4th April 2023, are noted and the
 Central Bank considers this action closed. However, the Firm should note that this area may be subject to further review during future
 supervisory engagements.

 Firm Finding 4 Action 3 04/11/22
 

 The Firm would like to take this opportunity to clarify the timeframe used for reporting STR’s to the Irish authorities. Alerts that are
 generated as a result of transaction monitoring rules will be reviewed within 90 days of alerting, however once a decision has been formed that the alerted activity is suspicious the Firm is committed to filing the STR as soon as practicable, within 24 hours of approval from the
 MLRO.
 The Firm has updated the SIL Square (formerly Seller) STR procedure to reflect the filing requirements of the CJA.
 The Firm is still in the process of reviewing the Cash App AML/CTF/FS Program including the Cash App STR Procedure. When updated, the
 STR procedure will reflect the Firm's approach to STR filing, which is as soon as practicable.
 The Cash App AML/CFT/FS Program and all underlying AML/CTF/FS Procedures will be updated as necessary and approved in advance of
 providing the Cash App service outside of its testing program.
 

 CBI Finding 4 Action 3 16/01/23
 

 The contents of the Firm’s response are noted. However, it remains the Central Bank’s view that a 90 day timeframe for the investigation of
 a suspicion of ML/TF is not in line with the provisions contained in the CJA 2010 (nor is it in line with the Central Bank’s AML and CFT
 Guidelines for the Financial Sector). The Central Bank requests the Firm to review this Action again and to re-consider its 90 day timeframe
 for the investigation of alerts/suspicions of ML/TF, prior to those alerts/suspicions being discounted or reported to the relevant authorities.
 

 Firm Finding 4 Action 3 16/03/23
 

 The firm would like to apologize for any confusion caused -the firm would like to clarify that the 90 days was for investigating unusual
 activity before a suspicion has been fully formed. Once a suspicion is formed the STR will be filed as soon as possible. The firm takes the
 approach that a transaction which appears unusual is not necessarily suspicious. We acknowledge that customers with stable and
 predictable transaction profiles may from time to time have transactions that are unusual for them. So the unusual is, in the first instance,
 only a basis for further enquiry, which may in turn require judgment as to whether it is suspicious.
 The firm has taken this opportunity to consider the time frames utilized and has recently changed the investigation period. When the TM
 alert is generated the analysts will have 30 days to determine whether further investigation is warranted. If the case is considered unusual
 the case will be sent to the STR investigations team where they will have 30 days for further investigation and to put forward a rationale
 relative to STR filing to the MLRO. If the STR investigation team recommends that a STR is required the recommendation is sent to the
 firm's MLRO. The MLRO will make a decision based on the investigation and the STR will be filled within 7 days. In Q4 2022 the firm filed 2
 STR's for IE sellers and 1 for an ES seller.

column4: Monitoring and Testing Program (CASH)
column5:
10.1.1 To further clarify the Firm’s prior responses, as it may have caused confusion. Square operates a 3 LOD program, there is QC performed on the 1 LOD (Line of defence) operational activity, there is a 2 LOD monitoring and testing team that performs targeted risk based testing and IA provides independent IA annually, program document (F10.1.1 ) is being provided at this time.  This plan states what is tested and when it will be tested. The scope documents for each review will detail who the results of the review will be presented to.
 The document, ‘Quality Compliance Program EU Seller Compliance’ applies to the 1LOD QC only and does not reflect the 2LOD monitoring and testing program.  This document has been updated and is provided as evidence [Square Compliance QC] [EU] Quality Control - European Union.pdf.
 The Firm is providing the Global Independent Testing and Monitoring Program document which details who gets the final reports.
 The Firm is also providing the approved monitoring and testing plan (F10.1.2) for SIL. 
 

 The firm are providing to the CBI the following documents:
 PF 10.1.1 Global Independent Testing and Monitoring Program and 
 PF 10.1.2 Approved 2022 Test Plan for SEL & SIL - Sheet1
 PF 10.1.3 [Square Compliance QC] [EU] Quality Control - European Union.pdf
 

 The firm will continue to assess the effectiveness of the monitoring and testing program as required.

The Firm acknowledges the comments provided by the CBI above.
 To further clarify the Firm’s prior responses, as it may have caused confusion. Square operates a 3 LOD program, there is QC performed on
 the 1 LOD (Line of defence) operational activity, there is a 2 LOD monitoring and testing team that performs targeted risk based testing and
 IA provides independent IA annually, program document (F10.1.1 ) is being provided at this time. This plan states what is tested and when it
 will be tested. The scope documents for each review will detail who the results of the review will be presented to.
 The document, ‘Quality Compliance Program EU Seller Compliance’ applies to the 1LOD QC only and does not reflect the 2LOD monitoring and
 testing program. This document has been updated and is provided as evidence [Square Compliance QC] [EU] Quality Control - European
 Union.pdf.
 The Firm is providing the Global Independent Testing and Monitoring Program document which details who gets the final reports.
 The Firm is also providing the approved monitoring and testing plan (F10.1.2) for SIL.
 The firm are providing to the CBI the following documents:
 PF 10.1.1 Global Independent Testing and Monitoring Program and
 PF 10.1.2 Approved 2022 Test Plan for SEL & SIL - Sheet1
 PF 10.1.3 [Square Compliance QC] [EU] Quality Control - European Union.pdf
 The firm will continue to assess the effectiveness of the monitoring and testing program as required.
 

 CBI Finding 10 Action 1 02/08/22
 

 The contents of the Firm’s response are noted and the Central Bank acknowledges receipt of the additional documentation provided by the
 Firm.
 It is noted that document PF10.1.3 titled “Quality Control Program EU Square Compliance” applies to 1LOD testing/QC only. It is noted from
 section 5.0 (Road Map) that it provides “End of 2022 - Aim to expand QC program to cover the following Square Compliance Operations
 queues within the following areas:
 ● Ireland (IE)
 - Watchlist (Sanctions)
 - Suspicious Transaction Reports (STR)
 ● As of June 2022, no cases have been generated in the above queues for QC sampling due to insufficient operational volume”.
 Could the Firm please clarify as to whether or not it has implemented a testing plan for both sanctions and STRs – noting that as at end June
 2022 there were no sanctions hits or STRs to test.
 In addition, it is further noted from the document titled “Quality Control Program EU Square Compliance” that it provides “The ultimate goal is to
 have a robust quality control program through the entire operational pipeline”. Could the Firm clarify if an assurance testing/compliance
 monitoring plan for all 1LOD AML/CFT compliance functions is in place.
 In relation to document PF10.1.2 titled “Approved 2022 Test Plan for SEL & SIL” it is noted that testing of AML compliance is scheduled for Q1
 only. Does the Firm intend to undertake 2LOD testing in respect of AML compliance for one quarter only?
 

 Firm Finding 10 Action 1 04/11/22
 

 The Firm can confirm that the testing plan has been created for both sanctions and STR’s, however it should be noted that due to insufficient
 operational volume no QC has taken place yet.
 The Firm can confirm that the QC team currently conducts 1LOD QC on all active compliance operations activity.
 The Firm intends on conducting AML/CTF/FS related testing in each quarter. At this time the 2LOD Monitoring and Testing plan has not
 been completed or approved for 2023.

column4: CRR Model Enhancement (CASH)
column5: 12.2.1 The Firm will take this item into account in the course of the overall review it will conduct in respect of its AML/CTF/Sanctions Program related to Cash App.
column26:
Firm Finding 12 Action 2 06/07/22
 

 The Firm acknowledges the comments provided by the CBI above.
 The Firm is in the process of enhancing the CRR model and the firm will review its CRR process to ensure a consistent approach across all
 customers.
 The Firm will take this item into account in the course of the overall review it will conduct in respect of its AML/CTF/Sanctions Program
 related to Cash App.
 

 CBI Finding 12 Action 2 02/08/22 
 

 The contents of the Firm’s response are noted and the Central Bank requests an update in respect of the Firm’s review of its CRR process, in its next response.
 

 Firm Finding 12 Action 2 04/11/22 
 

 The Firm has reviewed and enhanced the CRR model resulting in a consistent approach to all customers. The Firm would like to advise the CBI that Square performs a CRR at the time a merchant is onboarded, activation CRR (aCRR) and also throughout the lifetime of the
 merchant, continuous CRR (cCRR). The cCRR model, is unlike the one-time aCRR model, in that it will evaluate Financial Crime risk on a daily basis for all sellers using multiple factors/attributes. In addition, to the normal suite of signals, customer attributes. available to CRR models, Squares aCRR and cCRR models have at their disposal a wide range of signals that are used to feed into the risk score. Square has built a number of signals to address specific feedback, such as comparisons to business type benchmarks and out-of-pattern activity. The Firm would also like to clarify that there are different situations when Square will apply EDD to its Merchants. A Merchant may be deemed High Risk and therefore Enhanced Due Diligence will be completed, if certain thresholds are met, for example;
 1. Have an aCRR score that is considered High risk
 2. If there is a PEP connected to the Merchant this could be identified throughout the lifetime of the Merchant.
 3. If during the lifetime of the Merchant the cCRR score indicates high risk.
 4. Have a high risk merchant code, transacted a certain number of transactions and transacted a certain EUR of Gross Payment Volume (GPV)
 The rationale for requiring the Merchant to have transacted to certain volume/values is so that Square can better assess the Merchants
 transaction patterns.
 The Firm is still in the process of reviewing the Cash App AML/CTF/FS Program and all underlying AML/CTF/FS Procedures. As outlined in
 the Firm’s response of 6 July 2022, this item will be taken into account in the course of the ongoing review.
 The Cash App AML/CFT/FS Program and all underlying AML/CTF/FS Procedures will be updated as necessary and approved in advance of
 providing the Cash App service outside of its testing program.
 

 CBI Finding 12 Action 16/01/23
 

 The contents of the Firm’s response are noted. Could the Firm advise as to whether or not the changes to the Firm’s CRR model are
 documented (in the Square AML/CTF/FS Program or the Square (Seller) KYC/CDD Procedure (or elsewhere), and if so, if a copy of where
 this is documented could be provided with the Firm’s next response.
 

 Firm Finding 12 Action 2 16/03/23 
 

 The firm has documented the CRR model in its KYC Procedures which are attached as 12.2 FINAL SIL Square EEA KYC Procedures
 CBI 12 2 13/04/23
 

 The contents of the Firm’s response above, and the information provided by the Firm during the meeting of 4th April 2023, are noted. The
 Central Bank also acknowledges receipt of the Firm’s Square KYC procedures and the content contained therein. As such, the Central Bank
 considers this action closed. However, the Firm should note that this area may be reviewed further during future supervisory
 engagements.

 providing the Cash App service outside of its testing program.
 

 CBI Finding 12 Action 5 16/01/23
 

 The contents of the Firm’s response are noted. However, the Central Bank requests further detail in relation to address verification. The
 Central Bank requests that the Firm address the following:
 1. The Firm has advised “Business addresses are verified through the use of third party information on the CRO”. Is the Firm satisfied that
 the information maintained by the CRO is correct and up to date and that this information has already been verified?
 2. Could the Firm advise as to whether or not it intends to conduct BVD checks on all incorporated entities – including entities
 incorporated outside the State? If not, could the Firm advise as to how address verification is conducted for entities incorporated
 outside the State?
 3. The Firm has advised that for sole traders “address is gathered at onboarding and maybe verified through applied risk-based methods
 after onboarding”. Could the Firm provide further detail in relation to this statement? How is a sole trader’s address verified after
 onboarding?
 Further to the above, could the Firm provide an update (if available) in relation to the due diligence requirements for Cash App customers,
 including whether or not the Firm intends to apply due diligence thresholds to Cash App customers (please see Action 5 above for full
 details).
 

 Firm Finding 12 Action 5 16/03/23
 

 1. There is a legal requirement on companies to ensure that their registered office details are kept up-to-date with the CRO.
 Furthermore, under the Companies Act 2014, any document left at or sent by post to the place recorded at the CRO as the
 registered office of a company is deemed by law to have been left at or sent by post to the registered office of the company. For
 these reasons, the firm is satisfied that it is reasonable to rely on the information maintained by the CRO.
 2. The firm can confirm that BVD checks will take place, or similar checks using company registration databases, on all incorporated
 entities including entities incorporated outside the State as per the SIL KYC Procedures.
 3. The firm will request recent proof of residential address (utility bill or bank statement not older than 90 days) to verify the address
 of sole traders.
 The Cash App program document and accompanying procedures remain under review. As per the response in 2.1 the CCO will update the
 SIL Cash App Program document and accompanying procedures. The program document will be provided to the SIL Board in Q2 for
 approval with procedures also approved in Q2. Currently Cash App is not applying thresholds for the application of CDD as part of its
 ongoing testing program. This approach may be reviewed as part of the program and procedure update or at a later stage, provided all
 requirements and criteria for the application of such thresholds are met and the firm is satisfied that it is appropriate to do so.
 

 CBI Finding 12 Action 5 13/04/23
 

 The contents of the Firm’s response above, and the information provided by the Firm during the meeting of 4th April 2023, are noted and the
 Central Bank considers this action closed. However, the Firm should note that this area may be subject to further review during future
 supervisory engagements.

We suggest that Deloitte request this for the 2025 external audit so that SIl can demonstrate the process that is followed.

column4: 3.1c Know Your Customer: Verifying CDD Information At Onboarding: MAP 3/4
column5:
SIL acknowledges the comments from Deloitte regarding validating the business activity at time of registration. SIL does not ageee wiht Deloittes strict interpretation of the Spanish regualtions. As below Spanish regualtions do allow for online verification and a risk based approach to be applied to sellers during onbaording. 
 

 As per SIL's enterprise wide risk assessment for Spain - the overall rating is LOW. 
 

 SIL makes efforts to verify the accuracy of the information provided at the time of registration by customers in relation to their professional or business activity. SIL currently electronically validates the merchant information via local Spanish Sources of Information.
 

 Where SIL has doubts or there is an increase in risk SIL will apply addiitional measures as described in our CDD procedures. This is also acceptable practise under the Irish Criminal Justice Acts which regulates SIL.
 

 According to Article 4 of Law 10/2010 "Identity verification shall not be mandatory for transactions when there are no doubts surrounding the identity of the party involved...."
 

 Article 6. Reliable documents for formal identification of Law 10/2010 :
 1. ".....formal identification may be established with a certification from the Provincial Commercial Registry provided by the customer, or obtained online".
 3. " Obliged subjects shall identify and verify by means of reliable documents the identity of all those participating in unincorporated businesses. However, in the case of unincorporated businesses that do not conduct economic activities, the identification and verification through reliable documents proving the identity of the person acting on behalf of the entity will generally suffice.'
 

 This item was brough to the SIL RGC 28 May where the committee members agreed that there are no additional actions to be completed.
 

 SIL will review the 20 files mention in the finding and will request additional documents if additional CDD is required.

column4: Dispute Operations - C.05 - Customer Communication (Ineffective Control)
column5:
1 - The Provisional Credit Quick Text manually sent by Dispute Agents does not have required information detailed within communication based on the regulatory requirements. 
 2 - The Dispute Lost Quick Text manually sent by Dispute Agents does not have required information detailed within communication based on the regulatory requirements. 
 

 Action #1: There is a technical fix/initiative in progress that once implemented will result in the elimination of manual communications.
 

 Action Item #2: Deprecate associated Knowledge Article(s) Provisional Credit Quick. Communicate changes to impacted agents.
 

 (3) There were multiple instances identified where the final resolution communication was not sent to the customer after a case was deemed resolved in CF1. In-turn the provisional credits were never made final or removed from the customers account. 
 

 Due to no final resolution being sent to the consumer, there is an adherent risk of not abiding by regulatory SLA requirements. 
 

 Action Item #1: Identify all impacted accounts and finalize provisional credits where applicable.
 

 Action Item #2: Send communication via the normal BAU process to impacted customers documenting actions taken on account (methods include but are not limited to Quick Text or automated notification, etc.). 
 

 Action Item #3: Evaluate existing monitoring and/or reporting requirements to ensure the reporting function is operating as intended. Resolve any gaps that are identified.

column25:
SUP: Based on the business analysis of the CFPB’s ‘Buy Now, Pay Later’ (BNPL) interpretive rule, the business made a determination to discontinue the SUP Product. SUP is no longer offered as of October 1, 2024 and existing customers were notified on September 23, 2024. Therefore, no additional action will be taken by the business to implement the management action plan. 
 

 SUP-related action is closed/cancelled.
 

 Als refer to Action Plan #1141353 SUP Assessment based on Regulatory Counsel Guidance and BNPL Interpretive Rule (https://erm.protecht.com.au/afterpay/worms/client/app/widget.html?tablename=table_101590&appId=3&widget=RegisterPage&incidentId=1141353).
 

 Retro: The Retro Autopay Terms (FEB and Square Capital) were revised and the ‘notice of transfers varying in amount’ language removed because of inapplicability. The business determined that transfers varying in amount are only possible with weekly repayments if the customer does not pay a prior installment. The next transfer will include the sum of the unpaid installment and the current installment. Those equal amounts are disclosed at loan origination and available in the loan details within the app, and therefore, known by the customer. 
 

 This management action plan is complete.

column22: https://docs.google.com/document/d/1AHQw5jW-wjj4v-AYxhhU1sXY2Fx7K3z6j_2rOVXt4TM/edit|ATO-Self Reported: Knowledge Article and QT Updates
column25:
July 29, 2024: The ATO - Self Reported team created the “Risk - ATO Brokerage and Lending Cases” Knowledge Article (KA) which was implemented in Salesforce Cashforce One (CF1) on July 12, 2024. Retro-related ATO case investigations and customer notifications are completed within 10 business days of receipt from Cash App Customer Support. Investigations are documented in a formal write-up and added to the “Self Reported ATO with Loans” Google Sheet for Lending Operations to complete any necessary transactions (i.e loan cancellation). ATO-Self Reported will notify customers of denied claims via the ‘Cash Risk {ATO - Retro - Claim Denied} Quick Text (QT). Confirmed ATO cases are transferred to Lending Ops using the ‘Cash Risk {ATO - Retro - Transfer Add On}’ QT for Lending Ops to action the loan. Lending Ops will utilize the newly implemented ‘Cash App {POT - Retro ATO - Loan voided}’ QT which also went live on July 12, 2024. The QT confirms the claim, and also includes an editable section if the error occurred differently/different amount. When the error differs, the QT includes the consumer’s right to request documentation.
 

 This management action plan is complete.
 

 Note: During validation, GCMT identified that the "Risk - ATO Brokerage and Lending Cases" KA erroneously stated that Retro claims did not require a formal write-up. This was corrected/removed on July 29, 2024.

column25:
October 18, 2024: Cash Customer Operations (CCO) created the Account Take Over (ATO) l Retro procedures on August 29, 2024. The procedures outline the workflow for CCO. First, CCO must confirm that the customer has provided the required information in order to escalate the case to ATO Brokerage and Lending (Risk team) for claim investigation. Following investigation, the Risk team will tag Lending Specialists on the ‘Self-Reported ATO Loans Sheet’ for cases requiring action. CCO will communicate the action to the customer via Cash App {POT - Retro - ATO Reimbursement} Quick Text (QT). The QT was implemented on October 15, 2025. For denied claims, the Risk team will send communications to the customers.
 

 This management action plan is complete.

column25:
December 20, 2024: Cash Customer Operations (CCO) implemented the following quick texts (QTs) in order to notify Borrow customers of Account Takeover (ATO) dispute outcomes - 
 

 1. For denied ATO Borrow claims, Risk will send the ‘Cash Risk {ATO - Lending - Claim - Denied} quick text (QT)’ which includes the outcome of the investigation and the consumer’s right to request documentation.
 

 2. For confirmed ATO Borrow claims, the ‘Cash Risk {ATO - Lending - Transfer Add On} is sent to the customer to describe escalation to Cash App Lending. At the conclusion of the investigation, Lending will send the ‘US: Cash App {Borrow - ATO Reimbursement} which communicates the Borrow outcome of investigation. The template also has an editable section to include an explanation if a different error/different amount occurred and the consumer’s right to request documentation
 

 Processes are described within the ‘Risk - ATO Brokerage and Lending Cases’ Knowledge Article (KA) and the Cash Lending ATO l Borrow Notion page links. 
 

 This management action plan is complete.

September 27, 2024: Lending Operations updated the tracker on July 22, 2024. The enhancements include new fields and formatting to highlight the number of cases requiring action from Lending Operations, the number of cases out of SLA, and the overall Lending status (caught up, need to action, or overdue).
 

 Validation transaction testing was conducted for the period July 22 - August 31, 2024. From the population of 60, a sample size of 10 was randomly selected. 1 out of 10 was not corrected within one business day of the completed investigation. To improve controls, Cash Customer Operations Lending implemented ATO Reporting Statistics at the end of each day beginning on September 13, 2024. It includes the ATO Instances, the number of cases actioned timely (within one business day after the completed investigation), and cases that are outside of SLA. The expectation is to complete any outstanding ATO instances that have not been completed when reporting the statistics. 
 

 This management action plan is complete.

In instances where vendor log availability and quality met the standards of Block’s PAL framework, documentation was recorded to memorialize the effective controls. 
 

 In instances where the log availability or quality did not meet the tiered PAL requirements, data security exceptions were raised to document the deficiency with appropriate exception and risk owners documented on the exception.

The teams worked together to ensure that enabling the log collection did not create instances where sensitive data was exposed to users without the appropriate access rights or documentation.

column4: CAI will work together with the BISO to assess whether there is a need for explicitly defined risk tolerance levels for CAI.
column5:
CAI will work together with the BISO to assess whether there is a need for explicitly defined risk tolerance levels for CAI. Based on the outcome of this assessment, CAI will make appropriate updates to the Cybersecurity Policy as needed.
 

 Recommendation: CAI should evaluate if there is a need for a formally defined risk tolerance level. If so, CAI should evaluate if existing risk tolerance levels, such as those defined for Block Cybersecurity Risk Management Program, are applicable to CAI as a business. If existing risk tolerance levels for Block are insufficient, CAI should explore if there is a risk tolerance defined for Cash App and determine if that is applicable for CAI.
 

 If either Block of Cash App risk tolerance levels are sufficient for CAI purpose or that CAI has determined that it is not sufficiently beneficial for CAI to have a formally defined risk tolerance, the Cybersecurity policy should be updated to reference these risk tolerance levels. If the Block or Cash App risk levels are not compatible with CAI, then CAI should define their own risk tolerance levels and document them in the CAI Cybersecurity Policy. If it was determined that no risk tolerance levels are needed then the Cybersecurity policy should be updated accordingly.

column4: CAI will map required retention periods for different types of data.
column5:
CAI will map required retention periods for different types of data to adhere to FINRA's requirements, and reorganize Box to a structure that fits this before applying retention policies to the reorganized folders to fulfill the retention requirements in a way that avoids infinite retention where possible.
 

 Recommendation: CAI should transition away from having infinite data retention as it is against data retention and disposal requirements set forth in the DSL Framework as documented in the Block Data Safety Guidelines.

column25:
A presentation by Bjorn Hoffman - CAI Compliance on 12/30/2024, with Donna Adams - GCMT Compliance in attendance, covered the following in response to the recommendations of this action plan (#1144657):
 

 1) How CAI organizes and assigns DRIs for books and records line items via the Books and Records Inventory spreadsheet. This inventory also maps FINRA-required retention duration(s) for each line item, and also maps newly modified internal deletion schedules:
 

 https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1dO2HxSwW_L9lrv__EU_pRWprIZDFazH71fgn6iSXuhE/edit?gid=1454193476#gid=1454193476
 

 2) How the design of the new system in Box to fulfill retention requirements will operate. The following training fully describes the new system and was provided on the week of Oct 23, with 100% attendance, to all CAI employees who are DRI for a line item on the Books and Records Inventory:
 

 https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/1Ocp7t35xO055mnl3DCC0V9178X0RiV4SKxwy3-izNHc/edit#slide=id.g15db49e6373_2_444
 

 3) How the newly applied Box "Retention Policies" in Box satisfy the requirement to avoid infinite retention where possible by limiting exposure to infinite retention to only the few files specified to be retained indefinitely on the Books and Records Inventory;
 

 4) How all other books and records items are now applied Box "Retention Policies" with appropriate retention duration(s) and a clear path forward toward deletion for files that have met their retention requirements;
 

 and 5) How all DRIs for Books and Records line items will, on a quarterly basis, review the files for which they are responsible and ensure they are in the correct location(s) before certifying the following quarterly attestation in Compliance Alpha:
 

 "Certification that all records described herein are managed by the assigned DRI(s) and that the records are stored in a compliant location, as well as retained for the required retention period. Any records that have not been finalized must be identified in the questionnaire portion of this attestation, with the expected date of completion. Questions about this attestation may be directed to the Cash App Investing Chief Compliance Officer."
 

 This presentation aimed to demonstrate that CAI's policy changes successfully avoid infinite retention in Box where possible and that CAI's rooks and records retention policies now comply with the DSL Framework (as documented in the Block Data Safety Guidelines).

column4: Fraud Risk will hire a Policy Analyst to update policies and guidelines to ensure Fraud Agent's compliance with procedures when handling forced refunds. A new ATO process is being introduced by Q3.
column5:
Forced refund: 
 We are hiring a new UK/EU Policy Analyst who will own the process to review and assess the Global Forced Refund policy to ensure the size of the market is considered during reviews. For SIL language markets (ES and FR) we have lower headcount than for English markets, and so we may need to remove the second review clause and instead lower the lead approval threshold for these markets. We have already started adding comments to accounts that need lead approval for reimbursement, previously these were approved but no comment was left. The Policy has been updated to reflect the improvements pointed out by GCMT.
 

 The new Policy Analyst will also implement guidelines and procedures for dispute management, along with other topics we've identified, like a standard for escalation for SAR reviews and handling funds remaining in balance as a result of failed refunds and won disputes.
 

 ATO:
 A whole new ATO process is being introduced, expected delivery end of Q3. We will then test the policy for 3 months, to allow for time to amend any potential misses or inconsistencies.

column4: Square Credit Card - The annual review of Square Capital Lending and Consumer Protection Policies must be completed
column5: Square Capital Lending and Consumer Protection policies must be reviewed annually. Policies must be company specific and address areas of the laws, regulations and/or other rules or guidance that apply, not just a recitation of the regulation. Policies must be reviewed and approved annually by the Board or a Committee, even if no changes are being made to the current policy. New policies and policies with material changes must also be reviewed and approved by the Board or a Committee designated by the Board. Square Capital policies review must be included into Compliance Governance annual compliance matrix for review.

column4: Square Credit Card - Square Capital Model Risk Governance Policy must be revised to address the use of model overlays or model overrides
column5: The Square Capital Model Risk Governance Policy must be revised and implemented to address the inclusion and use of models overlays and overrides. The current policy speaks about an independent model inventory warehouse, but does not explicit covers Celtic's requirements. Square Capital Product team must work with data science to address the override and overlays model and then work with the Compliance Governance team to update the policy.
column20: SQ Capital uses SFS Models V7. We have submitted it to Celtic for review and approval. Nevertheless, the Model Governance Policy has been delivered and accepted by Celtic. Model Validation
column25: The audit finding pertains to the lack of information on overlays and overrides. The current policy speaks about an independent model inventory warehouse, but does not explicit covers Celtic's requirements. Square Capital Product team must work with data science to address the override and overlays model and then work with the Compliance Governance team to update the policy. Model Governance Policy has been delivered and accepted by Celtic. Clause 5.1.2 Risk Assessments - Driver of Oversight Frequency describes the risk assessment for each model. The Model risk assessment is refreshed during each model review. Consequently, all models are risk assessed at least annually after initial approval. Risk assessments are recorded in the Model Inventory Database, along with relevant associated information (date of assessment; information supporting the assessment including metric values, links to data sources, and presentation of assessment results documents; the names and titles of the Independent Model Validator and the scheduled date of the next assessment). So any overlay and override would be included in the model validation documents. According to the SQ Capital Program Manager, SQ Capital does not use overlays and overrides in their model. Currently, Square Capital is using SFS V7 model. We have provided V7 models to Celtic, which has been presented to Celtic and verbally approved. Based on the fact that Celtic has accepted the Model Governance Policy and model validations in separate documents, we are closing this item. Model Governance Policy and current model docs are attached.

column4: Square Credit Card - Square Capital Model Risk Governance Policy must be revised to include usage of proxy data.
column5: Square Capital informed Crowe that Square does not use proxy data, nevertheless, Square Capital will update its Model Risk Governance Policy and will formally state the non-usage of proxy data to validate its variables. This Policy update is also a finding for the 2023 audit, and Square Capital has committed to revise the policy by 11.31.24.
column25:
Square does not use Proxy data, therefore this item should not have been accepted as a finding from Crowe. The action plan committed to the solution of this finding was that Square Capital would update its Model Risk Governance Policy and formally state the non-usage of proxy data to validate its variables. This Policy update was also a finding for the 2023 audit, and Square Capital has committed to revise the policy by 11.31.24. The Policy has been updated and approved in June 2024 for the closing of the 2023 finding, submitted and accepted by Celtic. However, no specific language informing about the non-usage of proxy validation was included. Rather clause 5.3.4 discuss the data quality for the evaluation. I suggest, closing this item, or changing the due date for the inclusion of this language, when reviewing the policy again in 2025. The policy seems to have some other editing that needs to be fixed: 5.1.2 and 5.1.3 have same language ( its a duplication); also clause 5.1.5 Governance and Policies have some language that need to be corrected:
 "Model risk governance is provided by the performed by the Square Financial Services Data Science team with annual reporting to Square Capital Credit Product and Data Science Leads" to 5.1.5 Governance and Policies 
 [Model risk governance is provided by AND the performed by the Square Financial Services Data Science team with annual reporting to Square Capital Credit Product and Data Science Leads].

column4: Square Credit Card - Square Capital Model Risk Governance Policy must be revised to include a framework for performance testing and the documentation of quality testing and performance.
column5: Square Capital Model Risk Governance Policy quality testing and performance must be updated to outline a framework for reviewing identified performance issues in a repeatable manner. Log data issues for review and root cause identification.
column25:
Model Risk Governance Policy informs framework for reviewing identified performance issues in a repeatable manner. Also, Log data issues for review and root cause identification are tracked. 
 Policy informs how the risk assessment is conducted, and based on the results the frequency in which the model is reviewed (see policy 5.1.2): 
 Low Risk models will be reviewed at least annually;
 Medium Risk models will be reviewed at least semi-annually; and 
 High Risk models will be reviewed at least quarterly
 The risk assessment is refreshed during each model review. Consequently, all models are risk assessed at least annually after initial approval. Risk assessments are recorded in the Model Inventory Database, along with relevant associated information (date of assessment; information supporting the assessment including metric values, links to data sources, and presentation of assessment results documents; the names and titles of the Independent Model Validator and the scheduled date of the next assessment). See clause: 5.1.4 Model Inventory Warehouse for more details. Information confidential.

column20:
Original action plan owner has left the organisation. GCMT has identified the new action owner and senior business lead. 
 New JIRA ticket was raised for the action item: https://block.atlassian.net/browse/AMS-11857
 

 Afterpay Merchant profiles at Account-level on Salesforce have been updated with an 'AP Account Status' field with the following: 
 > Live - has at least one closed won opportunity
 > Prospect - has at least one open opportunity, and no closed won
 > Rejected - all opportunities are in a declined state
 > No Opportunities - account with no open opportunities, and not closed won
 > Terminated - If all closed won opportunities are terminated
 

 The Account Status field will be used by the Marketo system to prevent the send of marketing emails to ‘Terminated’ merchants. GCMT will validate the Marketo control in action item 1152596. 
 

 Screenshot of Afterpay Merchant accounts with the 'AP Account Status' field attached.

column4: REF 1B - Enhance tooling to prevent sending CEMs to merchants after contract/account termination
column5: A process will be established in Marketo to ensure that terminated merchants will not be sent marketing emails.
column20:
Marketing Ops noted inconsistent status fields in Salesforce: 
 - Account level: AP Account Status shows "Terminated"
 - Contact level: AP Account Status (A) shows "Active"
 This inconsistency caused terminated merchants to continue receiving marketing emails. 
 

 CRM Solutions noted that
 1. Opp moves to terminated, this causes the formula AP Account Status on account to update but there are also other (non-formula) fields which are updated on the account so this triggers a sync to Marketo
 2. The contact AP Account status formula also updates but since there are no other rollup fields on the contact, this likely doesn't trigger a sync back to Marketo. Formula fields are calculated when the field is viewed so they are not treated as an update
 

 As solution to ensure terminated merchants do not receive marketing emails, Marketing Ops enhanced Marketo’s smart list filtering logic:
 Now checks both status fields:
 - Account-level AP Account Status
 - Contact-level AP Account Status
 

 If EITHER field shows "Terminated", the Contact is excluded from marketing email lists. 
 

 GCMT performed sample testing of terminated merchants on Salesforce to ensure that the merchants are unsubscribed from CEMs on Marketo, and noted no exceptions.

column4: Remediate existing accounts with comprehensively sanction TLD
column5:
Remediate current Cash App customers that have an account associated with a sanctioned or high-risk TLD. 
 

 The remediation is broken into two cohorts:
 

 1. Comprehensively Sanctioned Location TLDs
 

 This remediation consists of closing inactive accounts, closing all active accounts that have not attempted or passed IDV, denylist all active customers that have passed IDV, and require all customers denylisted for sanctioned TLD to update their email alias to a non-restricted TLD (if they appeal the DL and have no other sanctions risk).
 

 2. Sanctioned and High Risk Jurisdiction TLDs
 

 If a customer has a block or reject report or a confirmed sanctions transaction match should be DL, if the customer does not meet that criteria they should have their email address unlinked, and be required to update their email address to an approved TLD, and be escalated to Emerging Risk for review; any identified suspicious activity should be escalated to SAR.

column4: Update neutral SSN policy
column5:
Update neutral SSN policy to ensure the SSN is valid and not already associated with an identity, to including the following logic:
 

 If R907 or R909, then Deceased
 If the name is an exact match and KYC_SSN_MATCH is true, then Positive
 If the DOB is an exact match and KYC_SSN_MATCH is true, then Positive
 If R940 is true, then Neutral
 Else Negative
column25:
As per conversation with Laura Butters who is experiencing Protecht functionality issues, Brian Kearns was asked to close the action plan.  
 

 As documented in the attachment, three enhancements were made to the IDV waterfall logic. On June 4, 2024 a Negative SSN signal was assigned if the vendor successfully matched the SSN but not the Name and DOB. On July 2, 2024, a Negative SSN signal was assigned if Socure did not return an SSN and failed to return the R940code indicating National ID not found in public records. On August 5, 2024 a Negative SSN signal was assigned if LexisNexis did not return an SSN and failed to return the risk indicator 71 indicating National ID not found in public records.

column4: Remediate Customers that had neutral signals that should be negative
column5: Remediate customers that received a neutral SSN under the previous policy that should have a negative SSN signal under the new policy.
column20: Justification for closing remediation action plan aligned on with P-Counsel, Product, and PCM and has been included in closure comments.
column25:
In May 2024, Cash App discovered that there were certain neutral SSN signal scenarios in which the customer entered an SSN that was associated with another vendor-maintained identity, which should have been classified as a Negative SSN signal. These customers were allowed to proceed with dIDV fallback, a process in which customers that are unable to pass eIDV, and are assigned either a positive or neutral SSN signal, are prompted to complete document verification in lieu of electronic verification, which represents a gap from our stated IDV policies. Cash App corrected this issue in August 2024 on a go-forward basis, so that customers entering an SSN associated with another vendor maintained identity would not be eligible for dIDV fallback on the basis of that attempt. 
 

 Cash App has since evaluated customers who previously verified their identities via dIDV fallback using an SSN associated with another vendor maintained identity. This cohort demonstrates ~87% authenticity rates, indicating that these customers most likely were thin-filed, meaning the customer is between the ages of ~18-25, or newly immigrated to the United States, and has little to no credit history, and had a typo or error in their inputted-SSN. 
 

 This discrepancy will be further investigated by Trust DRIs from Product, Compliance, and Legal, and it will be determined if data accuracy will be evaluated and tuned by its own project, under an already existing Trust project (e.g. Vendor Governance), or if a separate issue related to data accuracy should be opened in Protecht.

column25:
CG (Action Owner and Head of Afterpay Customer Success/Lending) confirmed on 16th of June 2025 that the action to “Pass feedback to AP Plus Product DRI regarding Audit Finding and design improvement opportunity to “Amend current AU Payment Arrangement SOP for CS team member to refund most recent Plus Monthly subscription fee if AP Plus Subscription is to be cancelled as a result of the Payment Arrangement being established. Note this would be when the subscription is active - do not propose this is refunded for customers who have failed to pay their subscription fee in which case membership would already be cancelled” had been completed. 
 Per the below confirmation and summary below, the decision has been made to not refund Plus Monthly subscription fees on a pro-rata basis. The key factors in the decision include a new feature that was implemented post GCMT review for the management of subscription including pauses, the above action is not commercially viable with strategic objectives for the product including expansion for Plus eligibility and further analysis of hardship or complaint trends. 
 On the below basis, and given the action was originally documented as an ‘intended’ action but was subject to feedback and consultation with the AP Plus DRI, GCMT note this can be closed. 
 

 “Requesting Action ID 1176989 to be closed based on the following justification:
 

 The proposed action to build a Plus Subscription fee refund process in the event of Hardship (and the subscription is active) is not required as already addressed in:
 

 - Ability for customers to now more easily and proactively manage their subscription through the pausing (see feature https://www.afterpay.com/en-AU/afterpay-plus-card ) feature (which I dont think was available at the time of the review) - meaning customers now have more flexibility to proactively pause their subscription and manage their finances
 - As we scale AP Plus across the AU market - its not a commercially viable pathway to proactively offer a Refund of the subscription fee, although we will track the % of customers entering Hardship by Product type. Note plans to expand Plus eligibility. https://docs.google.com/document/d/1VUfP2s9fxURufm2F3TqRToTvfC0ScJaPTx3urrNlhTc/edit?tab=t.0#heading=h.ie3a48ay6z50 
 - Note normal escalation pathways exist to have Escalation TLs assess if refunds for Plus should be applied for vulnerable customers. 
 - We do not see in complaint or Hardship trends this being a required action at this point in time.
 

 We will continue to monitor complaint trends and of course review this approach if complaint data indicates a need to revise this approach.”

column4: Enhancements to card enrollment and policy changes for card ownership
column5:
Unsolicited physical Cash App Cards are being sent in the mail to both existing customers and non-customers. Bad actors are able to evade identity, card ordering, and card activation product and risk controls to successfully initiate these orders. Cash App Product and Compliance aims to reduce the opportunity for bad actors to take advantage of the Cash App Card to perpetrate fraud, money laundering and other illicit activity. This includes the use of stolen identities to order Cash App Cards, and manipulate shipping addresses to circumvent card ordering limits.
 

 In order to reduce the incidence of bad actors sending unsolicited Cash App Cards, the Product team will be implementing more strict card ordering and card shipping controls. The Action Plan to address this issue involves:
 

 1) Address validation and normalization at enrollment of Cash App Card.
 2) Implement new model for targeting and preventing cases where virtual cards are activated without the legitimate identity's knowledge or intent. Blocking a virtual card activation will prevent the corresponding
 physical card from being ordered.
 3) Card ordering heuristic enhancements to block foreign device manufacturers, including an improved card activation ML model to train on foreign manufacturer data and CCO escalations that flag suspected cases of unsolicited card orders. 
 4) Update card ordering notification to provide customers with a phone number to cancel the card if they do not recognize the card order.

column4: Improve 9-digit SSN IDV flow for banking products
column5:
Lending products will adopt the SFS SSN Flow that will trigger IDV re-verification when the newly collected 9-digit SSN does not match the one on file.
 Cash Card remediated the issue on 8/2/2024 during incident resolution by preventing issuing cash cards if there's an SSN mismatch.
column19: Q3 2024 AML/CFT and OFAC Sanctions (Subpart A) (1029256)
column20: 6/5/24 - Per update provided by Lauren Butters (Product Compliance DRI), the issue is anticipating a slight delay of 2-3 weeks due to action owner's PTO schedule. The Senior Business Lead are aware of the delay and has no plan to request an extension request for the issue and action beyond 6/30.
column25:
Block’s Global Compliance Monitoring and Testing (GCMT) team identified and logged an issue relating to Cash App’s Trust’s SSN Comparison flow, used to comply with banking partner requirements to collect a full nine-digit SSN from the customer, when a Cash App customer is initially verified with the last-4 digits of their SSN. The flow initially stepped customers to dIDV when their collected SSN in the flow did not match the prefilled full-9 from the vendor. In August 2024, Cash App removed the dIDV step up process. Instead, if a customer provided a full-9 that did not match the prefilled full-9 when applying for a Cash Card or Borrow loan they would be blocked from moving forward. Cash App is currently working to enhance this flow under this issue, which is slated to launch mid-July.
 

 In addition to determining a go-forward path, Cash App evaluated the need to remediate customers that were allowed access to the above-mentioned products by completing dIDV. During this review, Trust DRIs for Product, Compliance, and Legal, evaluated the root of the issue, in that no investigation occurred when an SSN mismatch was identified. However, completing dIDV on these customers should serve as an investigation, and aligns with Cash App’s risk based approach to identity verification and their KYC Policy. Further, customers that did not complete or did not pass dIDV were not provided access to bank products. 
 

 Cash App tracks potential inauthenticity rates in dIDV, which is less than 2%, and given majority customers in this flow passed dIDV, this leads to a suspicion that these are either: (1) typos by the customer; or (2) inaccurate data provided by the vendor (e.g. prefill error). These potential data inaccuracies will be further investigated by Trust DRIs from Product, Compliance, and Legal, outside of this issue.

column19: Q3 2024 AML/CFT and OFAC Sanctions (Subpart A) (1029256)
column20: Response memo uploaded on 12/30/2024. For questions, please contact Katie Verdelli.
column25:
Sanctions Overview training was provided to COM Sanctions and QC/QA teams from 11/18/24 to 12/20/24. Training material included an overview of Sanctions, Block’s Global Sanctions Compliance Policy, General and Product-specific Red Flags, OFAC fines and penalties, and best practices.
 

 CashApp specific red flags reviewed include lack of transparency for securities transactions and cross-border relationships for CashApp Invest, out of pattern regions and gifted securities for P2P transactions, and obfuscated network transfers for bitcoin transactions. Example case studies of companies charged with OFAC violations and settlements (Binance, Payoneer, and multiple virtual currency companies) were also discussed.
 

 Per review of the attendance records, relevant COM Sanctions and QC/QA team members completed the training. 
 

 Sanctions Queue-Specific training was provided to COM Sanctions from 11/18/24 to 12/29/24. Training material included OFAC internal processes and investigation steps, workflow review, and actions to take for positive matches. 
 The workflow review section specifically goes over the Sanctions Alert Decision Matrix to determine if the case can be cleared as a false positive or escalated for an additional review. This Matrix defines the required review elements for Block and Watchlist types including Name, Date of Birth, Citizenship, Country of Birth, Nationality, and Location (Country). Specialists are instructed to add comments when clearing the case that indicate the reason for clearing, as well as providing information on what resource was used to determine the False Positive.
 

 Per review of the attendance records, relevant COM Sanctions team members completed the training. 
 

 This management action plan is complete.

column25:
A requirements document states that the report is built to enable daily monitoring of a 24 business hour from case creation SLA for Sanctions Watchlist alerts. Specifically, the alert data in the Tableau report is refreshed every 6 hours and excludes weekends and Block-wide holidays to ensure the SLA period is accurate and reflects the operational working hours. Data inputs to the report include Cash App initial and rescreening of sanctions alerts.
 

 Review of the Tableau Report (live link & screenshot) indicates that the data inputs (queues) are appropriate. Additionally, the report is easily filterable, allowing for quick identification of cases that exceed the 24-hour SLA and those in the backlog. 
 

 Out of SLA and backlogged alerts are reviewed daily and tracked and managed in Cash App Customer Onboarding & Management’s (COM) existing tracker. The tracker highlights significant backlogs, remediation actions (if applicable), DRI responsible for remediation, and estimated remediation date.
 

 This management action plan is complete.

column20: IM Analyst Stephen Green closing the action plan per Ramona Andrews.
column25:
A Collection Exclusion report was created in Tableau as an exception report to identify complaints without an applied collection exclusion date. 
 

 Soft launch: : The Collection Exclusion report was introduced during the Global Complaints/Service Excellence Monthly meeting on Dec 20,2024. During this session, the group agreed to proceed with a soft rollout in February 2025, giving team leads time to explore the report and surface any initial feedback or issues to help improve the report accuracy.
 

 Sample reviewing: We conducted a sample review to ensure the report was working and there was no false fail. 
 

 Report Logic: 
 

 ANZ Complaints: Complaints from Day 6 days from when the ticket is created. Exclusions are applied on Day 6 after the complaint sweeps to the Escalations queue.
 NA & UK Complaints: Complaints on Day 16 from when the ticket is created. Exclusions are applied in Escalations after the sweep on Day 15,
 

 The report includes date range, date of acknowledgment, age of ticket, agent name and workgroup filter into the report, enabling Team Leads to easily review data specific to their regions and teams. 
 

 Training with Team Leads: Training was held with all Team Leaders in February 2025 on the use of the report and how to manage exceptions. This training ensured consistent understanding and usage across regions and allowed leaders to ask questions and clarify expectations for daily report review. A SOP for use of the report is on the Info tab of the tableau report. 
 

 Soft Launch: Comms were sent out in the Ops Complaint Leader channel late February marking the official soft launch.Team Leads are now expected to begin using the report daily.
 

 Final Launch: With no issues reporting through March, the report is now considered final as of April 2025.

column4: REF 1A - Confirm the shareholder agreement status and collect the document where appropriate for impacted merchants
column5: The Merchant Onboarding team will remediate by reaching out to the impacted merchants to confirm the shareholder agreement status and collect the document where appropriate.
column20:
Merchant Onboarding has received the responses from the impacted merchants and have amended the shareholder agreement status accordingly. 
 Noted two instances where the merchants responded with documents other than a shareholder agreement (i.e. company extract showing shareholdings from the NZ Business Register). Upon a further review, Merchant Onboarding and ANZ AML Compliance are comfortable in accepting that these merchants do not actually have a shareholder agreement. As an outcome of the review, these merchants were both deemed to be low risk based on Afterpay's Customer Risk Rating practices. If at a later point in the merchants lifecycle Afterpay deems the merchant high risk, Merchant Onboarding will query them on this again. 
 Refer to NZ Shareholder Remediation Worksheet under "GCMT - Impacted Merchants".

column4: REF 1B - Manual process to collect the shareholder agreement upon initial review
column5: A manual process to collect the shareholder agreement upon initial review of the merchant will be implemented
column20:
The Merchant Onboarding team has implemented a manual process to collect the shareholder agreement upon initial review of the merchant. The process is live from 30th October 2024. 
 For Merchants who respond “YES” to having a Shareholder Agreement on the Merchant Onboarding Platform, a request via Formstak will be sent to the Merchant requesting an upload of the Shareholder Agreement. Merchants will also have the option to correct their response if needed. 
 The request is worded as follows, "You have advised that your company has a Shareholder Agreement. If this is correct, please upload a copy of the agreement. If this was selected in error, please let us know and we’ll update our records accordingly and continue our review.”

column4: Tableau Report Review
column5:
Management will review the existing Tableau
 report on Resolution Cost from Admin Portal to
 determine any enhancement needs.
column20: 6/26KP: Added evidence
column25:
The existing Resolution Cost tableau report was reviewed against the Write-off Report to determine if there was a way to cross-reference both reports, which are sources from Zendesk and Admin Portal respectively. There were key limitations which made this unachievable. 
 

 Key limitations: 
 There was no back-end data logic method to tie the Zendesk based tableau report to the Admin portal based tableau report
 The Zendesk report is case or account level, while the Admin Portal report is order based. This means that the Zendesk report shows a much higher value as it sums all refunds for a customer at the account level, rather than the order level.
 Admin Portal does not have an indicator to identify a complaint, so the Admin Portal report includes GWRs for complaint resolution, and refunds on non-complaints in normal course of business.
 

 While we set out to explore this knowing there was no integration between systems to make either report more accurate for complaints, we were able to confirm that there was no other back-end logic way to accomplish this. 
 

 However, there were a few enhancements made to the existing Admin Portal report for overall use. See attachments
