column4: System Automation Capabilities Review
column5:
Management will review additional system
 automation capabilities for key fields like “Tier”,
 “Product Type”, “Root Cause”, “Resulting
 Action”, “Legal Tag”, and “Resolution Cost”.
 Management notes that ‘Legal Tag’ and
 ‘Complaint Tier’ already have automation logic
 and that by the nature of complaints, it is not
 possible to completely remove the human
 element.
column25:
Several enhancements have been made over the past few months to improve data accuracy and resolution tracking.
 A review of complaint logs with ‘null’ values identified that some other forms in Zendesk did not have all the required complaints fields and these were updated as of Feb 27th. 
 Automation Impact on Resolution Fields: We conduct regular reviews of key fields, with incorrectly handled tickets sent to the QA team for further review. A recent review found that tickets with a ‘null’ resulting action were resolved by an automation. The SE lead confirmed that automation overrides required fields, allowing tickets to be marked as solved even if key fields are blank.
 

 Improved Trend Analysis: The Data team added a weekly view to the resolution report, enabling better trend analysis and easier identification of weeks with higher dollar values.
 Mandatory Resolution Fields: The EDR Complaint form was updated to require the following fields as of Feb 28th.  
 Resolution Cost
 Resulting Action
 Other forms which allow for Complaint tags but had none of the additional complaint field options were reviewed and updated. 
 Resolution Cost field was made mandatory on January 23rd. Since this was rolled out, there has been a significantly high error rate of over 40%. The January resolution cost in complaint reporting is highly overstated as agents entered order amounts, hardship amounts etc in this field. We sent refresher comms in February and leaders have been monitoring and fixing daily. With the error rate still over 40%, we will roll back this change before the end of March as this high level of manual review and remediation is not sustainable.

column4: Compliance Breaches Category Evaluation
column5:
With respect to QA, management will evaluate
 categories which are critical to be part of
 ‘compliance breaches’.
column25:
We have reviewed the current categories of deductions and determined that Compliance breaches, which holds the highest deduction, should remain as it is. This category addresses country-specific regulatory requirements and not internal program requirements. 
 

 The QA team will report on the % of times incorrect tiering is scored in the Quality report and the error rate will be reviewed using a risk-based approach to determine if the error rate is within tolerance or outside tolerance. Increasing the weight of a deduction will not close the gap, as the root cause of the tiering misidentification stems from over-tagging of legal tags. Refer to the QA Legal Tags review. The Learning team will be reviewing the existing Complaint Annual Module to enhance for the 2025 Complaint training and we will review the feasibility of a classroom or facilitator led component to that training; or other wants to enhance it.

column25:
1. Conduct an assessment to further test CS team understanding of Complaint Tier identification
 GCMT Have been provided evidence of the assessment undertaken to further understand agent understanding of Complaint Tiers. A copy of the results and a summary have been uploaded in the attachments below. 
 

 2. Conduct appropriate action following baseline of results; likely a team member comms strategy but may extend to revision of annual Complaints training.
 

 Following the assessment of the results noted above Management have documented the proposed strategy which has a target due date by the end of May 2025. 
 

 Facilitated Training Sessions
 (Currently being piloted with the Voice induction going live w/c 10th March)
 1. Instructor-led training sessions delivered by the Partner L&D team (dates TBC).
 2. Approx. 5 hours of classroom training covering:
  -Defining a complaint and differentiating it from general inquiries and feedback.
  -Key elements of effective complaint handling.
  -Understanding Complaint Severity Tiers and their role in resolution.
  -Logging and managing complaints efficiently in Zendesk.
  -Demonstrating empathy and professionalism when assisting customers.
  -Applying Afterpay | Clearpay’s Global Complaints Program policies.
 

 * Phased Implementation Approach
 1. Initial rollout with Digital and Voice teams, including team leads globally.
 2. Subsequent training focuses on Specialty teams.
 

 This will encompass the whole of Customer Ops, so there will be no need for us to complete the annual Complaints training as this will be covered off in this training
 

 Additionally a review of the "threatening harm SOP" will be undertaken to incorporate complaint referral processes, to ensure alignment with Escalation Pathways. 
 

 As the key action was to undertake a further assessment to drive actions/determine what actions to be conducted - GCMT note this can be closed.

 Summary document - https://docs.google.com/document/d/1vkBot6Fjo9uf0_50WjDNNlG-4u4bmopuhFETXx_45UQ/edit?tab=t.0
 

 Evidence folder - https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1n-263smZ2BLmAPlODn5O-MWGPJlCZ3nR
 

 As noted in the action, Management undertook a review to determine if there were opportunities via automation to reduce the number of incomplete or inaccurate fields for complaint reporting, while acknowledging in the initial MAP that the process is still heavily reliant on manual inputs. 
 

 As a result of the review, several forms were updated to ensure that where applicable each form required the mandatory complaint fields. In addition to this agents will receive an automated "error or warning
 message to note if a mandatory complaint reporting field has not been updated. GCMT were provided screenshots of this error message in the above documents. This was a result of the observation noted in the document that previously automation would allow complaints with a 'null' recorded to be marked as solved. 
 

 As part of this exercise Management also required the Resolution Cost field to be mandatory (previously not mandatory and only as applicable) however this resulted in significant errors. Following review by Management and other attempted solutions, this was reversed in March 2025. 
 

 Action deemed completed and ok to close as review was undertaken for any additional opportunities.

Management will commit to launching a new Digital Pay Monthly Overview SOP for all NA CS team members to use as a guide on how to identify and action Pay Monthly.
 

 Note this Finding is consistent with previous reviews conducted in relation to the Pay Monthly product. The observed behaviour is caused primarily due to core system functionality and an approach to reporting; 
 - There are limited solutions available to better flag or route 100% of active Pay Monthly orders to the dedicated teams (although note this is in place for inbound Messaging contacts) - relying instead on manual identification. 
 - Complaint and Contact data for Pay Monthly is overstated due to the tagging process and no integration between the complaint reporting platform and the servicing system. Any complaint for any reason where the customer also asks about Pay Monthly is represented in the data as Pay Monthly complaint.

column4: MAP 2 - Deletion of Customer Data
column5: Block is currently working on a broader Global approach for the destruction of customer data. The current expectation is that the capability will be built by September 2025.
column20:
Jay Clarke 09/09/2025: Mitigation Plan for Accounts Beyond Retention Period (Cash UK)
 As an interim safeguard, all applicable Cash UK accounts, including those beyond the five-year retention threshold, will be vaulted by September 30, 2025. Vaulting provides protection through encryption and partial deletion of non-retained fields, ensuring the data is no longer accessible in production systems and is secured against unauthorized access.
 For long-term compliance, Privacy Engineering will be asked to scope and prioritize the implementation of crypto-shredding functionality in 2026. This future capability would enable permanent deletion of vaulted data that has exceeded its retention limit, thereby fully addressing regulatory expectations under the UK MLRs and reducing residual risk.
 This two-tiered approach, vaulting in the short term, crypto-shredding in the long term—ensures immediate data protection while providing a clear roadmap to full compliance.

column20:
Lance HowardWe utalise the breach reporting guidelines in the AU. 
 We also have the block info security policy which has the continuity plan.
 The view at the time is that any information security incident is picked up in this framework. 
 6/2: The human trafficking queue launched in December 2024. See procedures and queue provided Remediation in evidence folder. 
 

 11/4/25 Compliance responses from Lance Howard:
 1. What does it mean in the tracker for column E "Impact Assessment conduct"? Under what circumstances will we conduct impact assessment? Where we think there will be significant impact or likely impact is traditionally when we will do an assessment. not all revoews will have an impact assessment
 

 2. For the first guidance "Data breaches and AML/CTF considerations":
 a. Just to confirm - were the statement of "Square AU currently handling all deactivation according to the guidance" a typo for this row? i can see that this statement is not relevant to the new guidance here and it was a duplicate form the cell above in the register. Yes confirmed this is a typo from what i can see.
 

 b. The data breaches guidance mentioned that reporting entity should review the current AML program to see if it hits the guidance on ML/TF risk assessment, ongoing monitoring of customer risks and re-verification of customer identities if necessary. Did we do that review? I know that we have considered the data breaches policies but the guidance seem to include more content/requirement other than data breaches.  
 We’ve reviewed AUSTRAC’s Data Breaches and AML/CTF Considerations guidance and confirmed it’s already covered holistically across our frameworks rather than as a standalone item.
 infoSec & Data Controls: Managed under Block’s and Square AU’s information-security standards (ISO 27001-aligned) with prevention, detection, and response controls for data breaches.
 Regulatory Breach Reporting: Our Breach Reporting policy formally includes data-breach scenarios, covering escalation and reporting to both AUSTRAC (where ML/TF risk arises) and the OAIC under the Notifiable Data Breach scheme.
 AML/CTF Program (Dec 2024): While the Program doesn’t explicitly name “data breaches” as ML/TF risk triggers, it captures them within the Operational, Employee and Emerging Risk categories of the ML/TF Risk Assessment. Section 4.4.2 also requires review after major operational or technology changes, which would include a cyber or data-breach event. We’re considering adding an explicit reference in the next update to align with AUSTRAC’s 2023 guidance.
 Cross-Functional Oversight: InfoSec, Privacy and Compliance teams jointly assess any breach for privacy, AML/CTF and governance implications, ensuring consistent response and escalation.
 Enhancements: Ongoing work includes stronger MFA and monitoring, expanded vendor breach-notification requirements, and refreshed training on breach-related ML/TF risks.
 In short — AUSTRAC’s data-breach guidance is already operationalised through our existing frameworks (Regulatory Breach Reporting Policy + InfoSec Standards + AML/CTF Program). At the time, the feeling was that we were aligned in the scope of risk covered by these programs, with the understanding that they will continue to evolve and improve.
 At the time of the assessment, the view was that a combination of frameworks, programs, and ongoing initiatives collectively meet AUSTRAC’s expectations, rather than relying on any single policy. This integrated approach reflects how our controls align across InfoSec, regulatory, and AML/CTF domains. Hope this makes sense — and open to challenge or other perspectives.
 

 3. For the 2nd guidance "Working together to prevent the sexual exploitation of children", the note in the tracker mentioned about "relevant considerations had been taken into building signals in cCRR model " - is there evidence showing this has been done?
 

 Also, for the TM queue that was built, is there evidence showing the queue is up and running? 
 The Human trafficking queue was launched which was the first step in managing this process to provide more oversight also the enhanced cCRR rules. Under the new AMLCTF reforms further work is continuing to be done in this space. Block AU has also significantly lifted its exploitation teams globally to support the porcess was the thinking at the time. Please see queue numbers and sample cases in evidence folder.

 1. The Square CM team has addressed this issue, which originally stemmed from an initial ramp-up period after the new 
  program launched and the design and update of a QA form to fit the updated program requirements. 
  --Response by Reuben Malicoat, Action Owner: The nuance and subject matter requirements for this role result in an 
  approximately 6 month period for new team members to achieve a moderate level of understanding and autonomy. 
  For this reason, the team does not QA new team members for the first 90 days, and instead they work with existing 
  team members and have much more oversight. It would be counterproductive, given the ramp up period required, to 
  subject new team members to QA - which they likely would not pass - only to have to performance manage them out 
  due to failed QA scores. 
   
  In the instance noted for AU on this action item, the CM team hired and onboarded 2 new CMs for that market, and 
  they were subject to the policy described above. 
 

 2. CM leadership will monitor these AIs and increase review of completed QA forms to ensure areas of opportunity 
  highlighted are addressed. 
  --Response from Reuben Malicoat: The CM team has removed the ambiguity around this action item, which did not have 
  a specific set date for QA completion. This was addressed during the investigation/audit period, where it was 
  determined that QA for the preceding month would be completed no later than the 24th day of the following month. 
  This ensures QA is completed consistently. 
 

  Additionally, the CM team will look to enhance the QA form further by including the suggested additions on or by the 
  action implementation date.

column4: The Form3 annual financial and risk profiling review will be included in the PayUK Player sheet for annual monitoring.
column5:
The Form3 annual financial and risk profiling review will be included in the PayUK Player sheet for annual monitoring.
 

 Compliance and Industry Relations have determined that the completion of the annual Form3 financial and risk profiling review will not be monitored by the Outsourcing Oversight Manager as part of the SEL Third Party Risk Register, as the register has been established and
 maintained to meet other key regulatory purposes, limiting the ability to include additional/separate fields.
column25:
Per confirmation with MF, the original action for the Form3 annual risk review to be added into the Go Pay UK Player sheet has changed following discussions with Third Party Risk Management and EU Compliance. 
 

 In its place, TPRM have confirmed that the Form 3 annual risk review will be monitored on a first line basis under their program. 
 

 As a second control Form 3 is monitored and included within SEL's third party register as an outsourced service provider. Whilst not changes will be made to track the Form3 annual risk review separately, the OSP will be subject to ongoing monitoring and review under the EU compliance's BAU Outsourcing processes and controls including an annual review. 
 

 On this basis GCMT is closing this action with responsibilities going fourth confirmed.

column4: Management will update the Back Up Contacts Register and address items with no or inadequate supporting documents in the PayUK Player Sheet
column5:
1. To address back up contacts register
 Update the “FPS Contact Data – SquareUp Europe” Spreadsheet for the two contact areas to ensure the minimum requirement of two contacts has been met. If two contacts are not available this should be noted in the contact area tab with the “minimum of two contact” requirement removed.
 

 2. To address items with no or inadequate supporting documents in the PayUK Player Sheet
 Additional information and enhancements will be made to the PayUK Player sheet and/or associated documents to ensure that in the event that the main DRI is absent that a back-up team member will be able to perform the task/activity required.
 

 o Square IRO will benefit from the establishment of a roles and responsibilities register which captures DRI’s, expected due dates, last completed dates and
 objectives at a requirement level. (To replace the information noted in the PayUK Player Sheet)
 o The current PayUK Player sheet provides cadence, but no indication of dates i.e. annual, bi-annual however requirements across teams may have different months the task is required to be completed annually. This will assist in ensuring resources are available within Square and allow forward planning for teams where large-scale work is required. A roles and responsibilities register/spreadsheet will also allow any change to requirements or information to be easily completed which the current format may lack. The register can also be shared between responsible DR’s with an expectation to update completion dates for tasks to allow ongoing oversight and tracking by the IRO.

Per confirmation with MF the initial recommendation for a seperate register to be completed was discussed with stakeholders, with the decision made to instead update the GoUK Player sheet to have on source of truth. column4: The Conduct Standards Procedure will be updated with regard to delegation and the HRBP will distribute delegation forms to all PCFs and CF1s for comepltion.
column5: The Conduct Standards procedure will be updated with respect to a revised process around ‘Delegation’, whereby HRBP will be responsible for issuing a delegation form to all PCFs/CF1s for PCFs/CF1s to complete where applicable. The onus will be on the PCFs/CF1s to complete and retain a record of these delegation forms. The SIL HRBP will distribute Delegation Forms to all PCFs/CF1s by the MAP completion date.
column25:
The Conduct Standards Delegation process has changed whereby the relevant PCF/CF is now responsible for maintaining the Delegation Form "As part of the Conduct Standards annual notification to PCFs/CF1s, they will be reminded to complete a Delegation Form." GCMT reviewed evidence of the HRBP sending PCFs/CF1s an email to complete a Delegation Form, with a due date of the 11th April. The onus is on the PCF/CF1 to retain, maintain and update their Delegation Form where necessary, which is documented in the Conduct Standards Procedure. The HRBP will be responsible on this occasion for following up on the Delegation Forms by the deadline. Going forward, it will be the PCFs/CF1s responsibility. The annual recertification process is another control in place to trigger an update on if the PCF/CF1 has had any changes to their delegation. 
 

 Additionally, aside from the annual recertification being a trigger to update PCF/CF1s delegation forms, other controls that can be pointed to are the Conduct Standards policy that sets out the requirements around delegation, training provided to PCFs/CF1's which covers delegation, the Conduct Standards procedure and the PCF/CF1 are informed of their responsibilities around delegation via the Conduct Standard notification.

column4: The Conduct Standards Procedure will be updated with a revised process for the quarterly confirmation from leads of PCFs and CFs on performance/conduct issues and if there are any personal changes.
column5: The Conduct Standards procedure will be updated with respect to a revised process whereby HRBP will request in writing, quarterly confirmation from leads of PCFs & CFs whether there are any issues [performance/conduct] that impact their ability to perform their F&P responsibilities and if there are any personnel changes. HRBP will issue these written requests by the MAP due date.

column4: Framework for Square Japan Complaints will be developed and Implemented.
column5:
As the Global Program Manager and Lead for Square Complaints Management, I can confirm that at this time we are still building out the framework for Japan. As required, we have submitted an exception request for all of Japan with the GCP, which has been approved. 
 To address the specific concerns highlighted within, I submit the following:
 1. As noted, we are still building the framework for JP Complaints. As we progress, we will define standards and practices applicable to this market. 
 2. Japan has a part-time complaint manager, who will only just transition to full time starting Feb 1. Once transitioned, this role will be responsible for the timely resolution of complaints. 
 3. Tier 3 complaints are not intended to be reviewed or actioned. The expectations for Tier 3 complaints is for frontline staff to resolve these matters and the complaint is flagged for records only. Frontline staff are expected to accurately document complaint details, and QA is required to monitor these to ensure complaints are accurate and filled out when necessary. 
 Because Tier 3 complaints are not reviewed, the bottom portion of the complaint document will remain incomplete. Per the GCP, the mandatory portions of the complaint are filled in by frontline staff as the complaints are filed.

GCMT concludes management action plan is complete and sufficiently addresses the issue and risk.

column4: Social Listening Evaluation of Elder Abuse Escalations
column5:
Seeking insights on how many Elder abuse or EFE escalations come through socials and understand the routing of these items.  Where are they going if not to the elder abuse risk review team or are they being referred through complaints? 
 Knowing the scope and impacts of these escalations through the social channels will advise which team in socials is most capable of absorbing the responsibility to refer to go/abuseesq; in addition, the volumes will help determine the cadence for referral ;ex. if many than daily may not be feasible as it is a fully manual process.
 

 ellen tseng is the appropriate POC who is responsible for this action item and lead for social listening, however, I cannot tag her in the issue below. Due date to receive the results from her team is 2/25/25
column20:
Data received from Socials regarding EA/ EFE; 
 Training is required for the team and socialization of the referral site, go/abuseesc.  
 Findings are below and have also been considered in both CBT creation training material and Policy.
 he Social Care team had a response rate of 80.7%, meaning 20.3% of cases involving elder abuse or exploitation went unaddressed. Of the cases that went unaddressed, the majority of them (72.7%) were from Reddit. Because Reddit is a community forum that has a history of not being receptive to intervention from companies and brands, Social Care has very specific guidelines on what can and can’t be addressed to avoid negative brand blowback. However, what was revealed during this analysis is that Social Care specialists adhered to these guidelines in an inconsistent manner. In other words, some cases that warranted response didn’t receive them while others similar in nature did. This highlights conflicting understandings amongst specialists regarding what can and can’t be addressed within Reddit. For the minority of cases that weren’t addressed and were escalated outside of Reddit, it cannot be determined why Social Care failed to respond to customer’s inquiries with the exception of one (1) case where a specialist forgot to circle back to a case after seeking outside counsel on how to address it. 
 Authentication of Personal Information & Abuse/Exploitation Escalations
 Despite there being numerous reports of elder abuse or financial exploitation, only around 7% of cases contained information that would warrant further investigation by support. This was due to the majority of reports (~93%) of elder abuse or financial exploitation coming from third parties, meaning customer support was prohibited from collecting the PII of the victim unless speaking with them directly. As a result and in most cases, the reporters of these incidents were instructed to have the victims reach out to Cash App Support directly for further assistance. That said, it’s not known how many of these victims eventually contacted Cash App Support nor is there visibility into which or how many cases were reported via go/abuseesc.

column4: SIU/ SAR Filings Related to Elder Abuse/ EFE to assist in determining gap
column5:
Problem Statement: Currently the Elder Abuse Investigations team is only reviewing customers 80+ @ onboarding, and referrals for 60 + based on additional red flags when made. The determination to review 80 + was based on authentic vs. inauthentic data only, which may not be fully indicative of any elder abuse or fraud related activity.
 To better understand the scope of the issue (1139987 due 6/30/25) and where potential enhancements can be made in to ensure compliance with all state and regulatory Elder Abuse and EFE requirements, data on SARs filed ~2 years on customers aged 60+ was requested, with ability to determine buckets, ex. 60 - 69, 70-79, etc., where we identified confirmed elder abuse and/or EFE. The data can either support the prior decision made or can influence any process, control and model updates that require updates to ensure we are protecting our customers and ensure compliance with the above referenced requirements. In addition, it was requested that we dissect some of this data to advise us the indicator(s) /typologies seen so that they can be built into any future models, controls, etc. as a part of the remediation work.
 Understanding the scope and impact of any changes and enhancements, will also help advise any capacity planning that needs to occur as a result of these changes.
column20: Alek D from FinCrimes has compiled this data and will be sharing with the Elder Abuse / EFE remediation team in 2/5/25 call ; trends and data will be leveraged to ensure elder reviews are scoped in at the appropriate age group, 60 vs. 80

column4: Retro (First Electronic Bank originated) adverse action notices are not sent for Identity Verification (IDV)/Customer Identification Program (CIP) failures. The IDV/CIP flow was not turned on.
column5:
On 1/16/2025 minutes after the issue was discovered, the Business enabled the feature flag to immediately require all customers to complete CIP re-collection before taking out a Retro loan.
 

 As all impacted customers were active customers who successfully completed CIP and IDV verification by Block at the time of onboarding to Cash App and at the time of opening their Cash App Cards, CIP on behalf of FEB will be required when such customers next apply for a Retro loan.
 

 The root cause that led to AANs not being sent was primarily due to handoff of work among Engineering personnel onboarding and offboarding.   Use/Purpose - This macro request serves an explicit purpose: to meet the additional disclosure requirements laid out in Nebraska Revised Statute 45-336, in regards to retail installment plans. Specifically subsection(5) which states: Upon written request from the buyer, the holder of an installment contract shall give or forward to the buyer a written statement of the dates and amounts of payments and the total amount unpaid under such contract. A buyer shall be given a written receipt for any payment when made in cash.

column4: Block has implemented the following enhancements
column5:
From response:
 

 Block has implemented the following
 enhancements:
 ● Implemented IP blocking for log-ins from Russia.
 ● Prohibited users from creating accounts using a mobile device that contains a SIM card with a
 country code associated with Russia.
 ● Prohibited the opening of restricted accounts (previously known as green accounts) using VOIP or
 reflecting any non-U.S. IP address, not just those previously prohibited due their status as a
 sanctioned jurisdiction.
 ● Prohibited the opening of restricted accounts that share attributes with denylisted customers (e.g.,
 email, phone number, device identification, or linked financial instrument). This is a significant
 measure that will mitigate the risk of customers previously denylisted for various forms of
 suspected illicit activity from re-entering the platform. This measure builds on an existing
 foundational control that automatically closes a customer’s account when the customer attempts to
 pass through identity verification using a previously denylisted social security number.
 ● Inclusion of this sanctions risk and compensating controls into Block’s AML/CTF and Sanctions
 Risk Assessment.

column4: Block implemented two principal remedial steps in March 2022 and January 2023, respectively, which it has continued to refine over time
column5:
From response:
 

 Block implemented two principal remedial steps in March 2022 and January 2023, respectively, which it has continued to refine over time:
 ● Additional Training. Block developed and delivered training to the Cash App Sanctions Operations Team, which is responsible for reviewing rejected transaction alerts, to ensure the team is (1) fully aware of the regulatory reporting requirements and (2) understands all procedural steps necessary to review such alerts. The training materials also emphasize expectations for sanctions analysts on when and how to escalate potential true matches with sanctioned parties and/or sanctioned jurisdictions to the Global Sanctions Team for additional investigation and regulatory reporting purposes, as appropriate.

column4: Increased Oversight over Changes to Sanctions Operations Processes
column5: ● Increased Oversight over Changes to Sanctions Operations Processes. To better manage changes made to its sanctions compliance processes, Block implemented a framework that increases management’s oversight of new or materially updated operations processes. The new framework 1) subjects any new sanctions screening queues created within Block’s sanctions screening systems to a more rigorous review and approval process, which gives greater responsibility to the sanctions compliance program managers to assess risk exposure and for program design, and also requires operational leadership sign-off, and 2) requires that senior sanctions compliance personnel sample and review alerts being escalated from Cash App’s Sanctions Compliance team to the Global Sanctions Team under the new or materially changed process to ensure that these referred alerts meet expectations for timeliness, accuracy, and completeness.

column4: Block has recently undertaken significant enhancements to controls applicable to restricted accounts
column5:
From response:
 

 "Block has recently undertaken significant enhancements to controls applicable to restricted accounts, including:
 ● Prohibited the opening of restricted accounts that share attributes with denylisted customers (e.g., email, phone number, device identification, or linked financial instrument) (implemented August 23, 2023). This is a significant measure that will mitigate the risk of customers previously denylisted for various forms of suspected illicit activity from re-entering the platform.
 ● Prohibited the opening of restricted accounts using VOIP or from a non-U.S. IP address (implemented August 21, 2023).
 ● Imposed a limit of 5 monthly transacting active accounts per customer, which is implemented utilizing shared customer attributes (e.g., use of same email, phone number, device identification, or linked financial instrument) (implemented September 30, 2023).
 ● Implemented a new lifetime limit of $1,500 in transaction activity for restricted accounts (to transact above this limit, an account must go through full identity verification) (implemented September 30, 2023).
 ● Aggregating transaction limits (including the new lifetime limit) across restricted accounts by using an additional shared attribute–device identification. This control has been implemented for 95% of restricted accounts; the final 5% of restricted accounts has been used as a control group for testing and analysis purposes and will be covered by year end (expected December 31, 2023).
 ● Implementing sanctions screening at onboarding and daily thereafter (expected November 10, 2023).

column4: No MAP - Block’s Bitcoin P2P feature does not fit within the definition for ST240 and ST250.
column5:
No MAP
 

 "Block’s Bitcoin P2P feature does not fit within the definition for ST240 and ST250. ST240 and ST250 are the total number and dollar value of “virtual currency to virtual currency facilitated exchange transactions which occur on the reporting entity’s platform involving an in-state buyer and/or seller during the quarter where the reporting entity is neither the buyer nor seller.” Block’s Bitcoin P2P transactions are not exchange transactions because they involve a customer sending bitcoin from their Cash App bitcoin balance to another customer's bitcoin balance. The transaction only involves bitcoin; there is no exchange of one type of cryptocurrency for another type of cryptocurrency."

column5:

TrackerPro generates Block's unclaimed property report in the standard National Association of Unclaimed Property Administrators (NAUPA) format, which Block submits to each state. column4: Sanctions GPS Control: Prevent Money Movements for Accounts Seen in Comprehensively or Broadly Sanctioned Locations
column5:
Implement a control that utilize a customer's GPS location to block activity in prohibited jurisdictions and prevent further activity on the Cash App platform while located in an unsupported location. 
 

 Accounts flagged by this control will receive a Sanctions Hold adversity. The accounts will be flagged in real time, as soon as it is discovered through GPS data that the Cash App account is being accessed.
 

 The result of applying a Sanctions Hold is that the entire account (stored balance and all transactional capabilities, applied across all account tokens under a single SAM account holder and any associated sponsor or sponsored accounts) will be frozen. This means the account can not successfully move money in or out of the account (including P2P, cash in/out, BTC, ACH, stocks, etc.). 
 

 The outcome applies to any customer or device where the latest observed GPS location placed the customer in a comprehensively or broadly sanctioned country. The list of countries to which this applies at the time of this Action Plan is:
 Cuba (CU)
 Iran (IR)
 North Korea (KP)
 Syria (SY)
 Ukraine (UA)
 Venezuela (VE)
 Russia (RU)
 Belarus (BY)
 Afghanistan (AF)
  
 After Cash App observes that an account or device’s latest location is in a non-sanctioned country, the Sanctions Hold will be revoked and the customer will be able to resume money movements on the platform.

column4: Update Graduation Process for Sponsored Accounts
column5:
In October 2024, Cash App modified its integration with existing third party vendor, Socure in order to establish a connection with the electronic consent based verification service (eCBSV), which in turn gives Cash App access to the Social Security Administration (SSA) database. This integration allows Cash App to use the SSA as a complimentary verification check during its current identity verification process.
 

 Cash App is in the process of updating its graduation process for sponsored accounts; the updated process will effectively limit most functionality whereby the sponsored account will only be able to liquidate assets and/or withdraw funds if they are unable to pass IDV at age 18.5. Cash App plans to complete this work by Q2 2025.
 

 If the sponsored account fails IDV at 18.5, Cash App will continue its current practice of performing enhanced due diligence on the account to evaluate if any further action needs to be taken on the account.

column4: Remediate DOB capture when onboarding via web
column5:
50K unverified accounts were created using the web in 2024 testing.  
 -- Engage the Sanctions team to ensure these 50K accounts were screened. 
 -- Engage Emerging Risk team to perform a risk analysis between the 50K accounts and app created accounts. 
 -- Confirm with ML Families team that the ML model that looks for teens hiding as green, also includes web-created green accts. 
 -- Ensure DOB is captured at onboarding on web
column20:
>>Engage the Sanctions team to ensure these 50K accounts were screened. 
 On 12/16 Faisal Sohail confirmed that these accounts were screened with some exceptions that are justified as indicated below. Here is the Web Sign-up Screening Check doc.
 

 >>Engage the Emerging Risk team to perform a risk analysis between the 50K accounts and app created accounts. 
 On 1/16/25 Carlie Reynolds provided this analysis where several concerns / gaps and recommendations are detailed which was shared with the PM: 
 Inability to see IP addresses or device info in Snowflake
 Data points like device name, SIM country and IP address are not have no data or have incomplete data.
 Multiple accts using same device or web browser -- and indicators of potential fraud rings
 Time zones include places like Africa / Lagos and Asia / Karachi
 IP Addresses that originate in Canada and Great Britain
 Device used: mostly iPhones but also Galaxy, Moto G phones. Also used Octopus apps which can serve as emulators
 User of certain operating systems also suggest the use of emulators
 Most accounts have not moved money. For many accounts that have moved money data is available in a table in DataBricks but it is currently not retrievable.
 

 >>Confirm with ML Families team that the ML model that looks for teens hiding as green acct users, also looks in web-created green accounts.
 Aditya Joshi confirmed that regardless of how the green accounts are created, the model looks at all green accounts.
column25:
Pilots were conducted between February to October 2024. Trust did not start collecting and storing DOB for users 18+ in onboard until Q1 2025. Therefore, no remediation of DOB is necessary as DOB will be collected and verified if/when the customer attempts identity verification. Further, 95% of Cash App customers are verified, and Cash App has additional controls to further it's verified population, such as a lifetime monetary limit on unverified accounts, and one unverified account per device. 
 

 Additional concerns outlined and mitigated included: (a) Sanctions screening, which was confirmed on all accounts that had legal names, there was a population of ~6k that had no screening record, but those accounts did not appear to have completed onboarding (e.g. dropped from flor after providing alias); and (b) Families model; ML confirmed that the model that looks for teens in green accounts captures all accounts regardless if opened via app or web. 
 

 One final item included an analysis completed by Emerging Risk which highlighted concerns with accounts onboarded during the pilot, include: (1) IP address, SIM, and device information availability and accuracy; (2) multiple accounts using the same device or web browser; (3) time zone information in non-US countries; (4) potential emulator use (e.g. devices included iPhone, galaxy, moto G, and octopus apps; and (5) established accounts with lack of money movement.
 

 As this Action Plan consisted of remediating DOB, it is being submitted for closure. PCM will continue to work with the Web PM, Prod Eng, and ML to address the concerns identified in the Emerging Risk analysis. PCM will determine the need to open a specific issue given Web has launched to GA, and included enhancements to address potential concerns.

column4: Max six (6) active sponsorships per SSN over a trailing 12-month period should be enforced
column5: Cash App Product and Engineering will update system logic to limit sponsors to six active sponsorships per SSN on a go-forward basis; The Product Team will also issue a supplemental document to clarify the updated logic.
column25:
GCMT reviewed the Product Requirements Document (PRD) for Max 5 Sponsored Accounts Per SSN / Account. GCMT noted that this document was created by Dasmer Singh (Families Product Lead) and Clayton Schoeny (Product Data Science), and provided thorough documentation including the current state, rule justification, business impact, and behavioral insights team reviews. The PRD received formal approval from Luke Zhu (Compliance) on 4/7/2025. 
 

 The document proposes revising the concurrent sponsorship limits by increasing the account-level threshold from four to five and establishing an SSN-level threshold of five, where no limit previously existed. The revised control specifications include precise definitions of the enforcement metrics to ensure consistent application of the restrictions. The SSN annual and lifetime thresholds will remain unchanged at six and ten, respectively. 
 

 Per this slack thread and launch checklist, this update was successfully deployed on 8/28/2025. 
 GCMT confirmed that since the implementation date, there have been no sponsors (per both customer token and SSN) that have started sponsoring more than five teen accounts. Refer to Snowflake Query for more details. 
 

 GCMT randomly sampled one customer from those sponsoring five accounts to validate that requests exceeding the sponsorship limit are properly rejected. The sampled customer had five sponsored accounts created on 9/15/2025. A subsequent sixth sponsorship request on 9/16/2025 was denied due to the sponsor being ineligible, confirming the updated requirements are working as intended. 
 

 This management action plan is complete.

The deprecation resulted from a high volume of false positives and the evolution of the Cash for Families (teen) control environment toward a unified Model plus Heuristic documentation approach. The updated heuristics governing teen account monitoring are documented in (https://docs.google.com/document/d/1XoeuDCzSY3wGPKXVTnGVCJo2Ir-x3MV8UMBohlVkwv4/edit?tab=t.koodnkuoepf#heading=h.4vsd5wd0jp2v).
 

 Because the distance and mutual-connection checks embedded in these controls no longer operate as enforceable safeguards, GCMT will not perform additional validation on the historical accuracy or effectiveness of those checks.

column20: August 13, 2025: Retro action owners updated from Global Complaints Oversight (Tiffanie Brown) to Lending Operations (Roy Tahsin).
column25:
Based on the combined commentary from the business and GCMT, no further action is required to close this action plan. 
 

 Third party complaints for Cash lending are sent through the CCOT team who intakes the complaint and routes to Cash lending Ops team for research and draft of the response. Cash lending then sends the response back to CCOT who are then responsible for tagging counsel for approvals before the response can be sent. In order to improve SLAs the following steps are taking place:
 CCOT team is no longer impacted by volume spikes due to the consent order being made public (GCMT confirmed this was the root cause of the initially failed validation due to the timing and influx of complaints)
 Cash lending Ops has partnered with CCOT and the Afterpay External Complaints team (EDR) to route all Cash Lending/Convergence complaints to the EDR team for triage to determine appropriate routing. Specifically, upon triage, EDR will determine if a complaint is Retro and will route to the Retro team. The EDR team will take responsibility for securing counsel approvals and will directly update the external party with a response. This includes CFPB complaints.
 

 

 We’ve created the SLA monitoring report and extended our SLA window from 10 days to 15 days to align with the Global Complaints Program (GCP). We’ve also updated the JIRA form: (https://block.atlassian.net/browse/CSSE-9905?focusedCommentId=11239860&page=com.atlassian.jira.plugin.system.issuetabpanels%3Acomment-tabpanel#comment-11239860) to reflect the new 15-day SLA and deployed the change.

column4: Hardship Policy
column5:
**Action Plan will be deleted as issue will be archived, per attached archive rationale.**
 

 Governing Hardship Framework – 
 

 Step 1. Gap Analysis Between Platforms
 

 - Compare Afterpay’s existing hardship accommodations to Cash App’s current capabilities.
 - Identify missing infrastructure, policies, and tools on the Cash side.
 

 Step 2. Define Unified Hardship Framework
 

 - Establish qualifying hardship scenarios and standardized relief options.
 - Align framework with regulatory guidance and the existing disaster policy.
 - Ensure consistency across both platforms.
 

 Note: As the Issue required Policy creation, addressed by Action Plan steps 1 and 2 above, the issue has been considered addressed and submitted for closure per the closure details provided. The work outlined in step 3 and 4 is considered to apply to a broader systematic approach. Accordingly, an incremental issue will be opened that is specific to this broader approach.
 Step 3. Cross-Functional Alignment
 

 - Engage Product, Legal, Compliance, Ops, and Engineering stakeholders.
 

 Step 4. Build Infrastructure for Cash
 

 - Scope and develop required tooling (customer interface + agent tools).
 - Design operational workflows and train support staff.
 

 Linked to -- ID 1202681

column4: Contracts
column5:
Square AU will partner with Commercial Counsel to include AML/CTF-specific legal clauses—such as suspicious matter reporting (SMR) obligations, AUSTRAC correspondence, and tipping-off prohibitions—in relevant third-party contracts. 
 

 We will also work with Commercial Counsel to ensure there are controls to notify compliance where contract reviews impact AML/CTF obligations by December 2025.
column25:
Summary of Actions Completed
 1. AML/CTF Contractual Requirements Confirmed and Approved
 

 Square AU Compliance met with Commercial Counsel and Procurement Legal to:
 

 Identify required changes to contract templates.
 

 Confirm alignment with AUSTRAC’s Using Outsourcing to Help Meet Your AML/CTF Obligations guidance.
 

 Define AU-specific AML/CTF contractual requirements, including:
 

 SMR escalation obligations
 

 Tipping-off prohibitions
 

 Cooperation with AUSTRAC / FIU requests
 

 Adherence to Square’s AML/CTF Program
 

 Recordkeeping obligations
 

 AML training requirements
 

 Screening obligations
 

 Commercial Counsel (Georgia Westbrook & Allen Babayan) formally accepted the recommendations and agreed to incorporate the standards into all relevant Square AU contracts.
 

 Status: ✔ Complete — Requirements confirmed and legally endorsed.
 

 2. Contract Updates Scheduled with Commercial Counsel (Q1 2026 Delivery)
 

 Commercial Counsel confirmed that 2026 vendor contract templates are currently being drafted and will incorporate the approved AML/CTF clause package.
 

 Counsel has committed to:
 

 Integrate the new AML/CTF rider into the AU contract playbook.
 

 Ensure Procurement triggers a Compliance review whenever AML/CTF-related services are in scope.
 

 Status: ✔ Complete — Contract updates scheduled for Q1 2026 with Legal ownership.
 

 3. Procurement Controls Updated (Implemented October–November 2025)
 a. Enhanced Due Diligence Questionnaire (ThinkSmart → ZIP transition)
 

 Implemented October 2025, including:
 

 New trigger question for AML/CTF-related services.
 

 Expanded AML/CTF compliance questions (SMR capability, Part A/B program structure, screening, training, recordkeeping).
 

 Mandatory disclosure of AML leadership, audits, regulatory actions, and designated service activities.
 

 Clarification of how electronic verification data is validated (AUSTRAC requirement).
 

 Status: ✔ Complete — Updated DDQ deployed prior to ZIP go-live.
 

 b. ZIP Procurement Platform Enhancements (Phase 1 – November 2025)
 

 ZIP implementation introduced:
 

 Mandatory tagging of Square AU entity and country for all procurement requests.
 

 Automated routing of AML-related engagements to:
 

 Commercial Counsel
 

 Square AU Compliance (for AML-related services)
 

 Integrated risk, onboarding, and contracting workflows that ensure visibility of AML/CTF-relevant engagements.
 

 Automatic flagging of offshore vendor engagements that may impact AU AML obligations.
 

 Status: ✔ Complete — AML/CTF controls embedded in the procurement workflow.
 

 4. Governance and Ongoing Control Established
 

 A recurring governance process has been confirmed whereby any contract refresh, renegotiation, or new AML-related vendor engagement must:
 

 Trigger AML DDQ questions
 

 Trigger AU Compliance review
 

 Apply the AU AML/CTF rider
 

 Procurement Counsel is responsible for:
 

 Embedding AML clauses in the standard AU Services Agreement
 

 Maintaining guidance in the contract playbook
 

 Ensuring procurement workflows escalate to Compliance at the appropriate point
 

 Status: ✔ Complete — Sustainable governance and control framework established.
 

 Supporting Documentation in the following File
 

 https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1GVmi-2oPMcLN-SjlyRePzUl2vvS7jJH4?usp=drive_link

column4: Breach and Incidents Communication
column5: Square AU to work with Compliance Program Operations to agree and document communication requirements for breaches or incidents
column25:
Square AU has worked closely with Compliance Program Operations to agree and document the communication requirements for regulatory breaches and compliance incidents. This work has resulted in a comprehensive set of published procedures that formalise how incidents are to be identified, escalated, assessed, and remediated in line with ASIC Regulatory Guide 78 (RG 78) and Square AU’s AFSL obligations.
 

 Key deliverables include:
 

 A fully documented Incident Management & Review Hub
 

 Clear escalation pathways from incident to breach determination
 

 Integrated breach reporting guidance and root cause analysis templates
 

 Governance alignment through the Compliance Issues Register and Breach Register
 

 Supporting links to reporting templates, forms, and policy documents
 

 These updates ensure alignment with both local legal obligations and Block’s global compliance standards.
 

 The framework was socialised with Square AU leadership in July 2025, with strong support for the approach and no objections raised. A copy of the meeting minutes will be attached for recordkeeping.
 

 We are comfortable to consider this item closed, with the process now live and embedded in our compliance ecosystem. Further artefacts or confirmation of session outcomes can be uploaded upon request.

 Action
 Conduct a comprehensive internal assessment.
 Review current monitoring coverage for impacted products/services (e.g. ACH and direct deposit) against the U.S. Cash Transaction Coverage Assessment and Cash’s Control library. For in scope controls, identify controls with dependencies on Sutton Bank or Marqeta.

column25:
2025 : Perform AML and Sanctions analysis of ACH and direct deposit coverage Closure Memo
 

 Document Details
 The information contained in this document is in response to a specific request made by the Cash App Compliance team. The information is confidential and proprietary and should not be made public unless consent is received by the responder. This document must be completed by the MAP DRI.
 

 

 

 Action Plan Details
 

 Issue ID: 1301061
 

 Action Plan ID: 1301061
 

 

 DRI: Michael Roemer
 

 

 MAP Due Date: 9/30/2025
 

 

 Issue Details: Perform AML and Sanctions analysis of ACH and direct deposit coverage
 

 

 Management Action Plan Details:
 Leveraging the outcome of Counsel’s contract review, ensure that Compliance conducts and completes a coverage assessment of potential impacted transaction channels (e.g. ACH, Direct Deposit transactions), evaluating them against existing product controls, Risk ML models, and transaction monitoring (TM) rules, and provides a corresponding analysis and/or recommendation.
 Action
 Conduct a comprehensive internal assessment.
 Review current monitoring coverage for impacted products/services (e.g. ACH and direct deposit) against the U.S. Cash Transaction Coverage Assessment and Cash’s Control library. For in scope controls, identify controls with dependencies on Sutton Bank or Marqeta.
 Identify specific control gaps, if any are identified.
 Document findings and recommend improvements
 Outcome
 A written analysis that outlines current monitoring coverage, identifies any risks or deficiencies, and provides recommended next steps, including whether additional action is required.
 

 Response: Cash App conducted a comprehensive assessment of its Automated Clearing House (ACH) monitoring processes following the identification of a potential gap in vendor-provided transaction monitoring. This evaluation examined whether existing internal controls adequately mitigate risks associated with ACH deposits and direct deposit services, or if additional measures are required. Specific recommendations to enhance transaction monitoring rules were approved to address gaps in ACH transaction oversight. These recommendations, informed by the ACH and Direct Deposit Coverage Assessment, build upon existing rule frameworks while incorporating targeted modifications and new implementations to ensure comprehensive coverage.
 

 

 Updated by Michael Roemer
 Reviewed action plan closure details and validate that closure details satisfy the required information needed to close this action plan.

column4: Updates to Cashbot and Customer Complaint Handling Requirements
column5:
Implement process to ensure Lending Complaints and Tier 1 Complaints will be routed to advocate for handling.
 Tier 2 and Tier 3 complaints will be resolved through the chatbot and the ML records will serve as evidence for complaint notes.
column20: Submitting for closure on behalf of Briana Peterkin, per attached screenshot and closure details.
column25:
Submitting for closure on behalf of Briana Peterkin (see screenshot attached), who has overseen remediation of this issue. Note Issue Closure comments as follows: 
 

 The closure evidence is contained within this document
 https://docs.google.com/document/d/1lAbgqCsn192R-n6UoGtifUiz9neErEYUh9ekPBUNjaY/edit?tab=t.0#heading=h.z97fqtqauuhh
 

 We are now able to quantify T3 handled by Cashbot, along with the Customer Issue Tag (CIT) which allows for trend analysis to be performed. Complaint Insights is now able to use this information for monthly complaint reporting. 
 We have an existing ML model that identifies complaint language in all customer messages, and created a separate model that assigns Customer Issue Tags (CITs) to all customer messages. Using this information, Cashbot LLM will now offer advocate handover if:
 *a Tier-1 complaint is detected.
 *any complaint is detected, 
 *and a lending-related CIT is tagged.
 (We always offer handover if customers appear stuck in a loop, regardless of complaint language. We always offer handover for requests to speak to a person (i.e., Tier-2 complaints).
column26:
Engineering design doc supporting the Cashbot related evidence can be found here - https://docs.google.com/document/d/15QA71-livDiSZD3PSuzVpjpVBbt40Brt3LHs9VMkPLE/edit?tab=t.0#heading=h.z97fqtqauuhh
 

 The below query was used in toolbox to review cases to validate the design is live 
 select 
  value:customer_token::string as customer_token, 
  value:conversation_token::string as conversation_token, 
  value:message_token::string as message_token, 
  value:should_handover::string as should_handover,
  value:is_complaint::boolean as is_complaint,
  value:customer_issue_tags as customer_issue_tags
 from cash_data_bot.raw.cash_support_chat_complaints_handover_results 
 where should_handover='true' and archival_metadata:received_at::string > '2025-09-20' limit 100;

column25:
July 25, 2025: The ATO Borrow and Retro Notion page was updated with the following-
 

  1. Regulatory error investigation timeframes were added including the 10 business days requirement for the error investigation; three business days to notify the consumer of the results following a completed investigation; one business day for the error correction after determining that an error occurred; and if an investigation is not completed within 10 business days, 45 days provided that provisional credit is given within 10 business days of the error notice.
 

 Additionally, the Lending Cases Knowledge Article, ‘Overview’ section was updated to include the regulatory timeframe error investigation described above.
 

  2. The Final Resolve workflow references were removed from the Duplicate Case, ATO Locked Account, and Previously Locked Account since the process was retired for these ATO Edge Case Workflows. The ATO Retro Agent Workflow (Pre ATO Investigation) > Step 4 Pushback will however continue to use the Final Resolve QT when a customer fails to provide the required information after four attempts.
 

  3. Instructions to follow when customer email is not on file (the Lending Specialist will send the communication to Risk to send the investigation results).
 

 This management action plan is complete.
 

 Note: Due to the Borrow migration to SFS, this issue was also tracked in the SFS issue tracking Jira platform, SFSERM-163 (https://block.atlassian.net/browse/SFSERM-163?focusedCommentId=12638984&sourceType=mention). SFS Operations team will utilize both the Notion workflow and the SFS Borrow ATO/Disputes SOP. The team has embedded the SOPs within the Notion page for reference. The SOP was updated on June 3, 2025 to include the regulation timeframe requirements noted in #1 above along with additional details that notice will be provided to the customer within two business days of provisional crediting.

column5: In response to the issues identified, the actions outlined below will be taken to remediate each finding. These updates will ensure Square Canada's policies and procedures align with the criminal interest rate provisions under Canadian law.

column25:
Comment by Issue Approver - Donna Adams: Issue Owner - Alex Di Carlo submitted an email on 10.29.2025 regarding completion and validation of identified issues of the required actions of the Q2 2025 GCMT Canada Criminal Code Interest Rate Review. The identified issue included documentation updates of policies and procedures. GCMT confirms review of the updates were completed and remediated.
 

 Reaching out as we've been able to complete the required actions as part of the GCMT Canada Criminal Code Interest Rate Review (October 31st target completion date). We've updated the required documentation to reflect the new Canadian APR cap requirements that were effective January 1st, 2025. Most notable changes were related to replacing the previous blanket 60% rate with the tiered structure that is currently in place based on entity type and financed amounts, and updating links that were no longer functional. We have left the changes as 'suggested edits' in the Google Docs to streamline your review - please let me know when we are able to finalize the edits.
 

 The documents that were updated as part of this review are as follows:
 1. Procedure: APR Cap - Manual Rebate
 2. Policy: Canada Commercial Loans
 3. Procedure: Usury Caps in Canada (only the two links on page one)
 

 Validation of reviewers is included within the documents - all edits made within the purview of this review were approved by @Alex Piciocchi @Jeff White @Raymond Ramirez @James Kim @Landon Hill. Special thank you to the team for their diligence in their reviews!
 

 Note: The Canada Criminal Interest Rate is applicable to Square's Merchant Cash Advance (MCA) product, as well as Flex Loans (covered within the scope of this review). As of right now, no related documents exist solely for MCAs - following your approval of the actions taken for this review, we will create a copy of the "Procedure: APR Cap - Manual Rebate" for Square MCAs.

column4: Enhance the SSN Comparison flow to create pathway to investigate SSN discrepancies
column5: Cash App Trust Identity & Controls along with relevant Legal and Compliance stakeholders will evaluate the appropriate solution to investigate, and reconcile, when applicable, data discrepancies between a customer's initial verification attempt, and their inputted SSN during Cash App's SSN Comparison flow.
column25:
Cash App Trust Identity Lifecycle has completed the work on the go-forward control enhancement for this issue, seen screenshot attached of the most recent PR deployment. 
 

 The new control pushes the customer to input their full 9 SSN, if that attempt fails (SSN does not match) they are prompted to input again, this information is then sent through the IDV waterfall. Trust Identity Lifecycle Eng also disabled the 60 day reset on these attempts, so that whenever the customers attempts a second time they are pushed to the waterfall; if the reset was enabled, the customer's attempts after the 60 day window would allow them to stay in a loop that never pushed them to the IDV waterfall for verification. 
 

 If the attempt passes, the customer will be able to obtain a Cash App Card. If the attempt fails, they will see the existing Cash Card/IDV blocker, and are prompted to reIDV (outside of this flow) within 14 days. Existing WIC controls are applied.

column4: Increase monitoring of statement data to identify problematic teams and address missing TransactionMetaData events.
column5:
"Regulatory Requirement: 
 Regulation E §1005.9(b)(1)(v) requires that, ""The statement shall set forth the following information, as applicable: The name of any third party to or from whom funds were transferred.""
 

 Finding Description:
 Periodic statements do not consistently show the name of third parties involved in the transfer (sender or recipient)."
 

 "Increase cadence of existing monitoring of statement data; establish patterns & identify worst offenders to know which teams to lean in on to fix more robustly. 
 

 Context: A known issue has been identified where 'TransactionMetaData 'events are missed for certain transactions, resulting in blank line items on consumer statements. This typically occurs when other teams modify or introduce new transaction types but fail to publish the corresponding metadata. Although a 'health check' is in place to track these discrepancies, it has not been closely monitored recently, hence the proposed Action to monitor more closely and delineate roles and responsibilities.
 "

column4: Develop and enhance Voice Strategy for CS
column5: Develop and enhance Voice Strategy for CS to include the capability to provide customers who call through the intake process with an estimated wait time for callbacks and general response time from Bitcoin Specialists.
column25:
Estimated wait time is played in the general IVR and details are below.
 

 EWT < 1 Min:
 We play the prompt that they will be connected shortly. But after 60 seconds (in case our EWT was not accurate, we give them the option to leave callback.
 EWT b/w 1 and 5 mins:
 We announce the EWT and give them an immediate option to leave callback or wait in the queue. Then after every 30 seconds we give them the option to stay in queue or leave VM.
 EWT > 5 mins:
 EWT announced and only given option to request callback. This is repeated 3 times giving them opportunities to request callback. After 3 tries we play prompt and hang up.
 

 Knowledge Article is linked in details tab, but below is snippet that advocates tell Bitcoin consumers about call back timeframes.
 

 Voice: Inform them about response timing:
 During hours (12 PM - 7:30 PM ET): Live transfer, or callback when the next specialist is available
 Important: Remind customer they must press 1 when prompted to receive a callback
 After Specialty hours: Callback within the next day

column4: Chat hours of operations expansion and IVR updates
column5: To address the inability to leave a voicemail outside of business hours, the following enhancements will be made: (1) Chat will be monitored outside of business hours (2) Customers who call outside of business hours will be told to contact chat for support
column25:
To meet requirements, we implemented a comprehensive upgrade to our customer service framework. This includes the introduction of a 24/7 support system via our Messaging channel, ensuring that customers receive timely assistance at any hour. Additionally, verbiage was added to the IVR to direct folks to chat if voice servicing is unavailable.
 

 IVR messaging after hours includes the following verbiage: Thank you for calling Cash App Support. Our phone lines are currently closed. You can reach us by phone every day from 8 AM to 9:30 PM Eastern Time. Need help now? Chat with us in the app—our team is available 24/7. Just tap your profile icon in the top right corner, then scroll down and tap Support to browse topics or start a live chat.
 

 Bitcoin-specific IVR after business hours: You have reached Bitcoin outside our normal business hours of 8am to 4:30pm PST, 7 days a week. You can still request a callback within our next business day by pressing 1 now. In the meantime, you can reach out to us in the app. Just tap the icon in the top right corner and tap support.

 Details: 
 >QA Policy Updates Reuben Malicoat initiated a discussion about updates to the QA policy, focusing on changes to an image and the review process. He clarified that the "NA" (Not Applicable) option for a review item does not require a comment unless the item would typically be filled out or is a special case. For example, if a legal tag is not needed, it can be marked as NA without a comment, as legal tags are not expected for most cases.
 "NA" Usage Clarification Reuben Malicoat and Molly Kostecki discussed scenarios for using "NA" in the QA policy. They concluded that if an item is simply not available in the normal course of business, no elaboration is needed for "NA". However, if the team is expected to grade on an item but is skipping it for an unusual reason, a comment justifying the >"NA" is required. Brandon Tiev noted that the previous version of the policy was redundant for items that consistently required a "no" or "NA" explanation.
 >QA Completion Deadline Reuben Malicoat informed the team that QA must be completed by the 24th day of the following month for the period being graded. Brandon Tiev mentioned that this deadline was not met the previous month. Reuben Malicoat explained that the 24th was chosen to allow time to review the QA with the team while the information is still relevant. Molly Kostecki confirmed that the main updates were the screenshot, the policy regarding NA usage, and the due date.

column4: Additional limits on creation of multiple accounts
column5:
In addition to the previously committed to work related to Plaid, Block has: 
 ● Prohibited the opening of restricted accounts that share attributes with denylisted customers (e.g., email, phone number, device identification, or linked financial instrument). This is a significant measure that will mitigate the risk of customers previously denylisted for various forms of suspected illicit activity from re-entering the platform. 
 ● Imposed a limit of 5 monthly transacting active accounts per customer, which is implemented utilizing shared customer attributes (e.g., use of same email, phone number, device identification, or linked financial instrument) (implemented September 30, 2023). 
 ● Implemented a new lifetime limit of $1,500 in transaction activity for restricted accounts (to transact above this limit, an account must go through full identity verification) (implemented September 30, 2023).
 ● Aggregating transaction limits (including the new lifetime limit) across restricted accounts by using an additional shared attribute–device identification. This control has been implemented for 95% of restricted accounts; the final 5% of restricted accounts has been used as a control group for testing and analysis purposes and will be covered by year end (expected December 31, 2023).

column4: Ongoing SAR filing monitoring
column5:
1. Cash App will put in place ongoing monitoring systems to measure the frequency with which SAR filings contain suspicious activity occurring long prior to the escalation/filing date. Specifically it will calculate, for each SAR filing, the elapsed time between first reported suspicious activity and SAR escalation or filing date, and will measure the distribution of these elapsed times across filed SARs.
 2. Management will define a ""tolerance threshold"" for the aforementioned measure on the proportion of SAR filings that include suspicious transactions long preceding the escalation/filing date.

column4: Complaint Trends Driven Issues Dashboard
column5:
Leadership identified a deficiency in reporting that provides a clear line of sight into complaint themes that are escalated by the Complaints Insights team. While detailed complaint data reporting exists, there is no centralized, executive-facing view of associated verified or potential Issues, complaint volumes, and remediation status. This inhibits leaders’ ability to take timely, risk-informed actions. The solution requires building a scalable, self-serve dashboard aligned to the revised Issue Management Program to surface complaint-driven issues and associated metrics.
 

 Milestones
 1. Confirm Executive Reporting Requirements - Validate which issue elements execs need visibility into for decision-making. Completed March 17, 2025 Briana Peterkin
 2. Data Source Mapping - Identify and validate where required complaint and issue metadata will be sourced (e.g., Protecht, Snowflake). In Progress August 15, 2025 CJ Brunke
 3. Build MVP Dashboard Create and test MVP dashboard displaying required metrics and complaint volumes. Not Started September 15, 2025 Subir Devaiah, CJ Brunke, Michelle Washington
 4. Exec Review Session Review MVP with stakeholders and gather feedback. Not Started October 1, 2025 Chrysty Esperanza, Owen Jennings, Ryan Budd, Tyler Hand
 5. Launch + Embed into Cadence Finalize dashboard and integrate into monthly Complaint Trends reporting. October 31, 2025 CJ Brunke, Michelle Washington
column25: We delivered the first enterprise-wide executive complaint report on November 7, highlighting cross-brand open issues identified through complaint trends and complaint-management compliance. Although the initial cascading issues have been addressed, we plan to expand and eventually automate this report to include broader program-health indicators—specifically SLA performance, regulatory-implication mapping, product-launch readiness and measurements, success metrics, and visibility into open issues.

column4: Cashbot Complaint Management Limitations
column5:
Cashbot currently lacks the capability to appropriately manage and escalate customer complaints, often resulting in customers being trapped in repetitive loops without resolution or a path to escalate. This gap presents a UDAAP risk, especially when the chatbot delivers inaccurate information or implies it can complete complaint-handling tasks it cannot perform.
 

 The issue was identified during Incident 5967 on May 16, 2025, when the PGM Support and Disputes teams observed a spike in unresolved disputes. In a compliant and customer-centric system, Cashbot should detect complaint language and route customers appropriately, either to a human advocate or a proper complaint channel.
 

 This deficiency is tied to Cashbot’s evolution from an FAQ-based tool to a more conversational interface, with Block’s Global Complaint Program not included in its design discussions. Product Counsel raised regulatory concerns (UDAAP) on May 28, 2025, triggering GCO-led remediation efforts. Work is in progress, and the remediation due date is September 30, 2025, extended due to resourcing constraints.
column20:
Enhance Cashbot’s ability to detect and route complaint language using machine learning (ML). Implement routing logic to ensure Tier 1 complaints and all Lending-related complaints are escalated to human agents. Tier 2 and Tier 3 complaints will remain in the bot, with ML records retained as complaint documentation. Trend analysis will be conducted on backend-tagged complaint volumes.
 

 Milestones

column4: The absence of exception monitoring
column5:
1- Initiate a targeted effort to mitigate fair lending risks associated with discretionary approval processes; this also includes hardship and disaster relief (currently in development).
 2- Identify all business areas and specific decision points where staff exercise discretion (e.g., fee waivers, due date extensions, APR adjustments) without hard-coded, documented parameters.
 2A- For each identified area, establish and document clear guidelines and objective criteria to minimize subjectivity and standardize these decisions where possible.
 2B- Where discretion is identified, but deemed to remain necessary, review implementation of a robust monitoring and testing program. This program would specifically track exceptions and discretionary decisions to detect any patterns that could suggest uneven application based on prohibited characteristics.
 3- Develop and implement controls to ensure adherence to newly established parameters, and to flag deviations for review.

column4: Failure to have a fair lending training program
column5:

 2- Develop a manual, which would, among other Program elements, outline fair lending-related policies, procedures and standards, and develop senior management reporting guidelines designed to comply with the requirements of CFPB ECOA Baseline Review Modules 2-5. And Interagency Fair Lending Examination Guidance.
 3- Review opportunities to develop training content tailored to specific job duties and responsibilities.

column4: Failure to conduct fair lending risk assessments

column4: AP ZD Aliases Request Draft Action Plan (non-final action plan for Issue Entry 1289149)
column5:
Draft (Work In Progress)
 

 Request:  
 1). Proposal to grant Afterpay (“AP”) teams such as Internal Dispute Resolution (“IDR” or “Esca”), External Dispute Resolution (“EDR”), Customer Service Operations (“CS”, “CS Ops”, “CS Ops Support”), etc., access to aliases in Zendesk (“ZD”);
 

 2). Steps to implement the aliases;
 

 3). Steps to monitor the aliases.
 

 

 Action Plan (Completion Dates TBD per @randrews)
 

 1) Proposal to Implement Alias Usage
 

 

 AP agents utilize ZD to communicate with customers. Some AP teams are able to use aliases in ZD (e.g. EDR, AP Pay-Monthly complaints “PM” team, and AP Business Process Outsourcing “BPO” team).
 Current Challenge:
 Although some AP teams may use aliases, other AP agents (i.e. IDR) are currently unable to utilize aliases due to possible system or operational limitations, leaving them more exposed to potential personal threats and emotional stress during their interactions with customers.
 Proposal:
 Grant AP agents access to aliases in ZD.
 

 

 

 

 Potential Risks:
 Increased Threats to Agent Safety: Standard Afterpay agents, who deal with potentially irate customers, are more exposed to personal threats as their real names are used during interactions. This increases the risk of agents being personally targeted.
 Emotional Stress and Decline in Agent Performance: Knowing that their identities are exposed can cause significant stress to agents, potentially impacting their mental well-being and performance.
 Operational Challenges: Continued exposure to safety risks without mitigating actions may lead to a higher turnover rate among agents, creating staffing and training challenges.
 

 Benefits of Alias Implementation:
 Enhanced Agent Safety:
 By replacing real names with aliases, agents are shielded from potential post-interaction targeting by irate or abusive customers.
 Consistency Across Teams:
 Aligning Standard Afterpay agent workflows with those of Cash App and Afterpay BPO agents ensures consistency across operations.
 Regulatory Compliance:
 Measures such as this align with best practices outlined by workplace safety standards, such as those from OSHA.
 

 

 Supporting Case Examples:
 Alias Issue - Case Examples Summary
 

 

 Issues Management Team Timeline:
 Develop intent for fixed stat / Action Owner: Clare Girdwood& Nicky Mitchell / Target Completion Date: September 1, 2025
 

 

 Implement fixed state intent / Action Owner: Clare Girdwood & Nicky Mitchell / Target Completion Date: September 15, 2025
 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 2) Steps to Implement Alias Usage 
 

 

 AP Operations may submit a request to the AP team that has configuration access to ZD to grant permissions for AP teams to use aliases.  
 

 Update ZD permissions for AP teams that do not have access to aliases.

column25:
This Item was raised at this months GRC meeting of Square AU. Screenshots attached for reference with the meeting maker details. 
 

 we invited Anj Kammanankada as a special guest to the meeting to discussed the findings and they outputs. Including future projects. 
 

 Also a shortened version has been added to this Quarters board meeting for a refference. see document of draft but this will be finalised at the next q4 board meeting but I know was not a hard requirement of this finding to be closed but just for records. 
 

 Notes attached and screenshots added to a link below. 
 

 (Anj) - Global Compliance monitoring & Testing Review - APP 11.2 Record Retention & Data Deletion 
 Global Compliance Monitoring & Testing (GCMT) conducted a review of Square Australia’s data retention and deletion practices between the period of 1 October 2024 - 31 March 2025. 
 Final Report is here and issues summarised below:
 Issue 1 (Moderate): This is a known issue by Block Inc., (Block) and accepted as a risk by Block’s innercore in November 2024 when Block’s Data Retention Standard was published (including the DRS Implementation Plan). The DRS Implementation Plan provides a roadmap for teams to implement controls throughout 2025. This includes deletion of data stored in offline central platforms (Snowflake and Databricks) to support DRS implementation, as well as alignment within Block Brands on whether data in online systems should be retained for legally defensible business purposes or deleted. 
 Issue 2 (Low): Privacy Portal (Square’s customer facing portal to handle data subject access requests) automatically cancelled deletion requests from Australia as it had not been configured for deletion requests outside Europe/UK. Remediated in October 2024. There is no legal right to request deletion under Privacy Act 1988 (Cth), but this is offered as a service for all Sellers. 
 

 https://docs.google.com/document/d/1AHCSxS9vNk_jdVjOHTwP-kXdgUV0Qs6Zvbh7AIHwx8E/edit?tab=t.0
