column4: Systemic Quality Oversight Gaps Across Customer Operations Post-Jan 2024 Layoffs-ACTION PLAN
column5:
Objective
 

 Restore and expand QA coverage across Risk Ops (RO), Customer Success (CS), and partner operations; improve statistical confidence and oversight; stabilize and scale Audit-the-Auditor (ATA); and unlock Quality Management System (QMS) value.
 Total request: 15 FTE (phased by priority) with minimal service disruption.
 Scope and FTE Summary (by Priority)
 

 P0 (immediate, 0–30 days)
 RO QA Partner Oversight & Alignment: 1 FTE (L4)
 CS Bitcoin QA: 2 FTE (L2–L3)
 P1 (near term, 31–90 days)
 Complaints QA (CCOT): 2 FTE (L2–L3)
 Disputes QA: 2 FTE (L2–L3)
 Audit the Auditor (ATA): 2 FTE (1 L3, 1 L4)
 P2 (mid term, 91–180 days)
 Banking QA: 2 FTE (1 L2–L3, 1 L4)
 AIC/Scams QA: 1 FTE (L2–L3)
 QM Program Effectiveness (QMS value unlock): 3 FTE (2 L3, 1 L6)
 Level mix (target): 1 L6, 3 L4, 3 L3, 8 L2–L3
 Timeline and Major Milestones
 

 Month 0–1 (P0)
 Approve P0 headcount and budget or self-funding reallocations
 Assign L4 RO Partner Oversight; restart CS Bitcoin QA program
 Kick off rubric calibration for partner scopes and Bitcoin
 Month 2–3 (P1)
 Hire/onboard Complaints, Disputes, and ATA FTE
 Shift Complaints scope to CCOT-only; reroute non-CCOT complaints to relevant QA teams
 Restore ATA throughput to ≥10 audits per QA specialist and re-launch cadence of calibrations
 Month 4–6 (P2)
 Hire/onboard Banking QA, AIC/Scams QA, and QMS Program Effectiveness team
 Expand Banking and AIC workflow coverage; deploy QMS features (AutoQA, intent taxonomy, summarization)
 Ongoing
 Monthly steering; quarterly re-forecasting; continuous partner calibration
 Workstreams, Actions, and Success Metrics
 

 RO QA Partner Oversight & Alignment (1 FTE L4) – P0
 Actions
 Establish single-threaded owner for Cartograph/partner QA alignment
 Implement monthly calibrations with partners; standardize rubrics and scorecards
 Create partner QA performance dashboard (SLA, variance, issue backlog)
 Self-funding
 From ATA temporarily if necessary, with explicit approval of reduced ATA throughput
 Success metrics
 ≤5% variance between internal and partner QA scores
 100% monthly calibrations completed
 Partner QA findings turnaround time ≤10 business days
 2. CS Bitcoin QA (2 FTE L2–L3) – P0
 

 Actions
 Resume Bitcoin QA oversight paused since Jan 2024
 Define sampling strategy (see risk/trade-off) and calibrate rubrics
 Reinstate reporting to CS/RO leadership and risk partners
 Self-funding
 None feasible per current bandwidth
 Success metrics
 Coverage: ≥95% confidence / 5% margin OR agreed sample methodology with documented risk acceptance
 SLA: evaluation cycle time ≤5 business days
 Defect escape rate reduced quarter-over-quarter
 3. Complaints QA (2 FTE L2–L3) – P1
 

 Actions
 Expand oversight to all phases of CCOT, including External Response
 Increase sample from ~160 to ~280 to reach 95% confidence, 5% MOE
 Implement case-complexity weighting due to 1–1.5 hr reviews
 Self-funding
 Modify scope to CCOT-only; push MET, Disputes, Social, Bitcoin complaints to their respective QA teams
 Success metrics
 Sample size ≥280/month with documented confidence/MOE
 100% CCOT phase coverage
 Turnaround time targets met without backlogs (>1 week backlog = red)
 4. Disputes QA (2 FTE L2–L3) – P1
 

 Actions
 Stand up oversight for: ATR, MQ External Loss Docs, Backstop, Remittance, Negative Balance Recovery, Disputes Advanced Support, ODFI interactions where applicable
 Prioritize workflows by customer risk/financial exposure
 Self-funding
 If not funded, reduce monthly evals 12→8 and/or push to partner where already embedded; document risk acceptance
 Success metrics
 Coverage across all listed workflows within 60 days of onboarding
 Error rate and loss-avoidance insights reported monthly
 Partner vs internal variance ≤7%
 5. ATA (Audit the Auditor) (1 L3, 1 L4) – P1
 

 Actions
 Split ATA into dedicated CS and RO streams; define scope and intake
 Restore per-QA specialist audits from 6–8 back to ≥10; re-establish calibration per skill
 Add Independent Review audits and FlowOps partner reviews
 Self-funding
 Not feasible; ATA currently at a deficit and expanding scope this year
 Success metrics
 ≥10 ATA reviews per QA specialist/month
 Calibration drift ≤3 percentage points per rubric
 Time-to-corrective-action ≤15 business days
 6. Banking QA (1 L2–L3, 1 L4) – P2
 

 Actions
 Expand oversight to: Transaction Monitoring (MQ & Sutton), BF CF1, ODFI ACH/Treasury Recalls, Link Analysis Referrals, Wire Recalls, Risk Reviews, RDC Returns/CF1, DANS, Exceptions, ROA/TVAs
 Assign L4 to lead cross-domain calibrations and risk prioritization
 Self-funding
 None; consider pushing CaC Suspension evaluations to partner where already implemented
 Success metrics
 Coverage of all listed workflows within 90 days of onboarding
 Material control gaps identified and remediated; quarterly trend reports to Risk
 SLA adherence ≥95%
 7. AIC/Scams QA (1 FTE L2–L3) – P2
 

 Actions
 Add oversight for: Estates Mgmt, Deceased/POA, Escheatment, Scams Suspensions, AHO Live Readiness
 Balance multi-LOB coverage with targeted sampling
 Self-funding
 Not feasible; expect fewer evals/agent; secure Ops agreement on sample sufficiency
 Success metrics
 Documented sampling plan with risk acceptance
 Escalation accuracy and customer harm prevention metrics reported monthly
 8. QM Program Effectiveness (2 L3, 1 L6) – P2
 

 Actions
 Stand up Program Effectiveness team to unlock QMS capabilities: contact intent taxonomy, interaction summarization, AutoQA
 Build enablement, reporting, and adoption playbooks; integrate with CS/RO roadmaps
 Self-funding
 Recommend redeploying existing governance analysts/strategists; net-new only for L6 lead
 Success metrics
 AutoQA coverage ≥30% of eligible interactions by 6 months
 20–30% reduction in manual evaluation hours in covered areas
 Time-to-insight (from issue to published finding) reduced by ≥25%
 Resourcing and Budget Approach
 

 Headcount ask: 15 FTE total; phased by P0/P1/P2
 Self-funding levers to approve:
 Shift Complaints QA scope to CCOT-only
 Temporarily borrow from ATA for RO Partner L4 if required (accept reduced audits temporarily)
 Push select Banking/Disputes workflows to partner where already embedded
 Redeploy governance analysts to QMS effectiveness; net-new L6 only
 Trade-offs documented with risk acceptance memos for leadership sign-off
 Onboarding and Enablement (per cohort)
 

 0–30 days
 Access, tools, rubric training, shadowing; baseline calibrations with partners
 31–60 days
 Independent evaluations with weekly QA calibrations; first insights report
 61–90 days
 Full productivity; trend reporting; corrective action loops with Ops/Risk
 Governance and Cadence
 

 Weekly: Workstream stand-ups; hiring tracker; risk log review
 Bi-weekly: Partner calibrations; defect trend reviews
 Monthly: Steerco with CS/RO/Risk; KPI pack (coverage, variance, SLA, backlog, loss avoidance)
 Quarterly: Scope rebalancing; sample size validation; capacity and budget re-forecast; QMS impact review
 Key Dependencies
 

 Updated rubrics and SOPs for expanded workflows
 Partner data access and calibration commitments (Cartograph and others)
 QMS/AutoQA configuration and data pipelines
 Reporting dashboards (coverage, variance, turnaround, AutoQA adoption)
 Risks and Mitigations
 

 Underfunding/partial funding: Document service reductions and risk acceptance; prioritize highest risk workflows; leverage AutoQA to offset manual demand
 Partner variance: Tighten calibration cadence and scorecard governance
 Long review times (Complaints): Complexity-weighted sampling; WIP limits to avoid backlog
 Change fatigue in Ops: Clear comms on scope shifts; phased rollout; feedback loops
 Decisions Required
 

 Approve P0 hires immediately (1 L4 RO Partner, 2 L2–L3 CS Bitcoin) or self-fund from ATA temporarily
 Approve scope change: Complaints QA to CCOT-only; route other complaint types to respective QA teams
 Approve P1/P2 headcount and self-funding levers (partner pushes, governance analyst redeployments)
 Approve QMS Program Effectiveness team structure (net-new L6; redeploy 2 analysts)
 KPIs and Targets (roll-up)
 

 Coverage and confidence
 Complaints: ≥280 monthly sample; 95% confidence, 5% MOE
 Disputes/Banking/AIC: 100% of listed workflows covered within 60–90 days of onboarding
 Quality alignment
 Partner vs internal variance ≤5–7% depending on domain
 Calibrations: 100% cadence adherence
 Throughput and timeliness
 ATA: ≥10 audits per specialist/month; corrective action ≤15 business days
 Evaluation SLA: ≤5 business days for CS/RO QA
 Efficiency and impact
 AutoQA coverage ≥30% of eligible interactions by month 6
 Manual QA hours reduced by ≥20% in AutoQA-enabled areas
 Loss avoidance and customer harm prevention trends reported quarterly
 Next Steps (2-week window)
 

 Confirm priorities and approve P0 headcount or self-funding approach
 Kick off hiring with Talent; open reqs for P0 and prepare P1 pipelines
 Stand up partner calibration meetings and Bitcoin QA restart plan
 Produce risk acceptance memos for any interim reductions (ATA, Disputes/Banking partner push)
 Deliver initial dashboard framework for coverage, variance, and SLA tracking
 Owner Assignments
 

 Program lead: L6 QM Program Effectiveness (once hired) or interim CS/RO QA Director
 Workstream owners: L4 RO Partner Oversight; L4 Banking QA; L4 ATA
 Talent/hiring: People Ops lead with program PM
 Reporting and QMS: Program Effectiveness team and Data/Analytics partner
 This plan phases critical coverage first (P0), closes high-risk gaps next (P1), and builds scalable effectiveness last (P2), while using targeted self-funding to reduce net-new asks where feasible.
column25:
Summary: 
 - With the re-organization of Risk, where QM resources were realigned from Customer Operations to Risk Operations, the RO QM team is working through some of the structural changes that have modified the scope of RO QM. Additionally, there are pending hiring decisions and work instructions that require development prior to initiating QM oversight. 
 - In Customer Operations, QM has hired 7 resources and has 3 open HIDs that are enabling the QM team to close coverage gaps. 
 

 Customer Operations QM Update: 
 

 Social: 2 resources hired (Indu Merritt and Lora Osborn); QA oversight is now in place, and the QA team is conducting 8 evaluations per advocate per month; based on the amount of QA evals being performed, we are exceeding the statistical sampling requirements of 90% confidence level with a 10% of margin of error. 
 

 Bitcoin: 2 resources hired (Stephanie Brito and Taylor Mills); QA oversight is now in place, and the QA team is conducting 10 evaluations per advocate per month; based on the amount of QA evals being performed, we are exceeding the statistical sampling requirements of 90% confidence level with a 10% of margin of error. 
 

 Complaints: 1 resource hired (Lily Anticouni) and 1 CE (to cover a LOA, Mohsin Lodhi); the QA team is delivering >230 Tier 1 External Complaints per month and >100 Internal Complaints per month; based on the amount of QA evals being performed, we are exceeding the statistical sampling requirements of 90% confidence level with a 10% of margin of error. The Complaints QA team also conducts 4 QA evals per advocate on complaint cases logged for MET, Disputes, Social and BTC. We have a plan in place to transition this volume to the respective QA teams (e.g. QA teams covering MET, Disputes, Social and BTC) during 1H 2026, which will further free up capacity for the Complaints QA team. 
 

 Audit the Auditor (ATA): 1 resource hired (Patrick Winston) and 2 HIDs targeted for onboarding in Nov ‘25 to support incremental growth at partner); target audits per QA Specialists have been shifted from 6 to now 8 per month. 
 

 QMS Tooling Enablement; HID for a Sr. Lead for this new team is active, targeting to have the leader in place by Nov/Dec. Additionally, have identified multiple resources that will form part of this team; all of this will be critical once the QM team is on one QMS platform, which is targeted for 1H 2026. 
 

 Risk Ops QM Update: 
 

 Disputes: 
 For the following workflows, Risk Ops has a hold as they are working on work instructions (QA can’t happen without up-to-date and accurate/detailed work instructions); anticipated completion is Q4 ‘25 - Q1 ‘26. 
 Associated Transaction Review (ATR)

 Backstop
 Remittance
 Negative Balance Recovery
 Discussions underway with Risk Ops leadership to hire 2 resources in order to initiate QA support for Disputes Advanced Support Escalations
 

 

 Banking Operations: 
 Risk Reviews for RDC CF1 are set to go live 11/1 
 Exceptions and ROA/TVAs is no longer a function and doesn’t require QA oversight
 For the following workflows, Risk Ops has a hold as they are working on work instructions (QA can’t happen without up-to-date and accurate/detailed work instructions); anticipated completion is Q4 ‘25 - Q1 ‘26. 
 Transaction Monitoring (MQ & Sutton)
 BF CF1
 ODFI ACH/Treasury Recall Requests
 Link Analysis Referrals
 Wires Recall Requests
 Risk Reviews
 RDC Returns
 Deposit Adjustment Notices (DANS)
 

 Scams and Account Integrity: 
 For Scams Suspensions and AHO Live Readiness, Risk Ops has a hold as they are working on work instructions (QA can’t happen without up-to-date and accurate/detailed work instructions); anticipated completion is Q1 ‘26. 
 Discussions underway with Risk Ops leadership to hire resources in order to initiate QA support for Estates Management

column4: Completed: APR updated and affected customers informed
column5:
We have implemented enhanced system controls within the Afterpay Loan Service to ensure strict compliance with the 36% effective APR threshold. This automated control prevents the origination of any loans that would exceed this cap. While our Target APR model is set at 35.5%, we recognized that with the introduction of 3-month loan terms, additional safeguards were necessary to account for potential variations in effective APR calculations.
 

 This enhancement builds upon our existing state-specific APR cap controls, extending the protection to ensure all loans, regardless of jurisdiction, remain under the 36% effective APR threshold.
 

 Regarding the identified cases, we have completed remediation for the five affected customers by:
 1. Adjusting their future interest charges to 0% using our interest rate override functionality
 2. Ensuring their total effective APR remains below 36%
 3. Communicating these adjustments to affected customers through approved notification channels
 

 These measures demonstrate our commitment to responsible lending practices and regulatory compliance.

column4: Incomplete Fidelius Token Mapping
column5:
Issue
 Our existing fidelius token logic maps to a single bank account number and a single routing number. However, most major financial institutions provide customers with several routing numbers, which could allow someone to bypass our controls by changing the routing number during the manual bank linking process. This issue exclusively arises when a customer manually links their bank account, as Plaid and Stripe do not allow users to select or change their routing number.
 

 To address this potential vulnerability, we should either update the token logic or implement a new approach that maps tokens at the institution level rather than to specific routing numbers. By mapping tokens at the institution level, we can effectively prevent workarounds that could circumvent our Max SSN controls.
 

 

 Additional Background
 In March 2025, during an investigation of an East West Bank Fraud ring, it was uncovered by the Transfers Eng Team that some customers were successfully able to link multiple (above Max 5) bank accounts to their existing Cash App accounts. The impact appeared concentrated to bad actors linking their East West bank accounts through the manual bank account linking flow. Plaid/Stripe which support ~85-90% of bank account linking traffic, do not provide users with the opportunity to enter/select a unique routing number. 
 

 Impact
 There are currently data limitations making it challenging to truly determine the pervasiveness of this issue. Product Compliance is engaging with the Transfers team to create a normalized bank account token and backfill it to Snowflake to support further Compliance analyses. This work is expected to be completed in 1Q26.
 

 Once we have the ability to size the true impact of the issue, we can better assess the risk associated with this potential vulnerability. Once determined, we will need Transfers Eng to change the Ftoken logic to map to financial institutions vs unique routing number.
 DRI: Trevor Luong
 Due: 1Q26
 Rationale: Mapping to the institution vs routing number will help ensure no future ability to exceed our Max SSN controls.

column4: Loan Agreement Update
column5:
The Retro Loan Agreements (First Electronic Bank (FEB) Originated, Section 8.c states that early repayments are based on state law, "...will refund any unearned amount of the Finance Charge in accordance with the law of your state of residence when you entered into this Agreement." 
 

 However, business practices do not consider state laws since: Early repayment refunds are not applied to FEB originated Retro loans, and repayment refunds are the same for all customers, regardless of state. Product Counsel will revise the language in this agreement to align with our current practice (more specifically: the Bank will refund pro rata any portion of the Finance Charge that exceeds 5% of the Amount Financed, proportional to the number of days remaining until the Due Date etc.).

column20: Closing on behalf of Approver Faisal Sohail. See screenshot for evidence.
column25:
Action plan evidence uploaded including rational for incomplete milestones. 
 

 AP Supporting Evidence doc: https://docs.google.com/document/d/1nSWg5H-oJfqZnxVnudywi2x3yABmRoLSyUtrIrwRdps/edit?tab=t.77543np3pv2y
 

 Rationale for Non-Completion of Remaining Milestones (Action Plan 6 – Remediation by Sanctions Compliance Ops)
 Following completion and evidence submission for Milestone 1 (Remediation of GPV CSJ population), Global Sanctions leadership determined that the remaining milestones (2 through 7) are not required for issue closure.
 Updated Context
 Go-forward preventive controls (implemented under Action Plans 5 and 8) now block creation of any new Account Owner or KYB record containing a sanctioned Top-Level Domain (TLD).
 Validation completed in October 2025 confirmed zero sanctioned TLD results post-fix across all onboarding surfaces.
 The remaining population consists solely of legacy merchants already identified by Sanctions Ops. Any required deactivation (DA) or freeze will occur through standard operational workflows rather than through separate remediation milestones.
 If merchants have not responded by 11/17/2025, they will be deactivated by Ops. Response volumes have slowly decreased but Ops expects responses to still populate in the TLD SSP queues as merchants reply.
 Merchants who need to be deactivated with a balance will be reviewed by Sanctions Ops. Sanctions Ops will determine the best action to take with the funds using a risk-based approach and escalate to GSC as needed.
 After 11/17, the TLD Review QRG will be added to the Square User Guide and responses will be reviewed as part of BAU operations.
 We acknowledge at that point, all the merchants who reply and are successfully mitigated will need reactivation at the time of case closure. Ops will be solely responsible for deciding if merchants meet mitigation requirements. Ops will not need to escalate these cases to GSC unless additional concerns are found.
 Leadership Determination
 TLD presence is a red-flag indicator, not definitive evidence of sanctions exposure.
 The residual risk associated with merchants using sanctioned TLDs is minimal, given (1) the go-forward technical control, (2) existing manual review processes, and (3) ongoing monitoring by Sanctions Ops.
 Therefore, completing the remaining remediation cohorts would not meaningfully reduce sanctions risk or change program posture.
 Conclusion
 The Global Head of Sanctions (Faisal Sohail) has reviewed the residual risk and formally accepted it within the program, acknowledging that the remaining work will be addressed once Ops completes the TLD SSP queue cases.
 Accordingly, Milestones 2 – 7 are considered closed, with no further remediation evidence required beyond the control validation and Milestone 1 completion already documented."

column4: CSD to be documented and maintained for product development
column5: Product Development : Business Ops maintains a Customer Summary Document (CSD), which is used by the business to document the specifics of the product/feature to be launched, in order to document product/feature rollout strategies. The CSD contains the Launch Requirements Document, which includes legal counsel guidance on the product/feature launch in the form of a Legal Memo. Business Ops will update with a change log in order to emphasize changes that were made to the product/feature that were made after compliance approved the launch of the product/feature.

column4: Product Compliance to create product launch governance procedures
column5: Product Compliance will create product launch governance procedures, which will instruct PCMs and cross-functional compliance partners on how to document launch requirements (e.g., controls) within the Launch Requirements Document (LRD), which is the document used to set what new or enhanced controls must be completed to support the product/feature launch (defined by phase of launch). The Procedure will specify how reconciliation steps will be performed in order to confirm that control requirements are fully documented, implemented, and approved prior to development advancing to the launch phase by which the control requirement must be completed. These Procedures will also instruct on: expectations for control execution (to guide DRIs when documenting any SLAs) and the escalation process for procedural breakdowns.

column4: KYC Data Inconsistencies Action Plan
column5:
1. Assess existing processes, supporting documentation, and available data to conduct a comprehensive current state assessment of the program framework. The assessment will evaluate the effectiveness, accuracy, and completeness of current practices.
 1.1 Evaluate the end to end KYC program framework, to identify any potential gaps, inconsistencies, control deficiencies, and opportunities for improvement. 
 1.1.1 Document current state workflows across the customer lifecycle, including the identification of applicable systems, data inputs and outputs. This includes documenting how customer information is captured, validated, routed, stored, and propagated across systems. 
 1.2 Review the existing customer data for completeness, accuracy, and consistency in alignment with internal policy requirements and regulatory expectations. 
 1.3 Evaluate monitoring, reporting, and metrics to determine whether they effectively capture and measure data integrity issues.
 1.4 Evaluate control effectiveness and monitoring
 

 2. Assess root causes and risk impact, including downstream AML and regulatory implications.
 2.1 Document identified gaps and control deficiencies including key findings and risk themes.
 

 3. Implement a feedback loop process, supported by system controls, to ensure KYC data remains complete, accurate, and up to date. This process will enable effective ongoing customer due diligence, risk-based monitoring, and timely detection of data discrepancies.
 3.1 Ensure controls are clearly defined, consistently documented, and coherently structured across customer types and business models, and whether they are implemented in a manner that supports consistent execution.
 

 4. Operationalize KYC guidance and procedures 
 4.1 Assess documentation and strengthen guidance for resolving inaccurate, incomplete, or inconsistent customer data. Updates should incorporate best practices for ensuring data consistency and completeness. 
 4.2 Procedures should integrate with system controls, operational workflows, and monitoring processes to support ongoing customer due diligence, risk-based monitoring, and sustained data integrity.
 4.3 Share policies and procedures with relevant stakeholders to gather feedback as needed, and incorporate input to finalize the documents.

column4: Device GPS-Applying a Sanctions Hold Adversity
column5: Due to Cash App collecting GPS data, if a customer opts-in, We occasionally see coordinates from sanctioned countries. Due to these signals, an adversity is applied to accounts with sanction country GPS signals on a daily basis. Absent a mechanism to automatically apply an adversity solely due to a GPS signal occurrence, a manual process is run each day to identify those accounts with the signal and apply the adversity. The below process outlines how that occurs.

column4: KYB Refresh Overdue for High Risk Cash for Business (C4B) Customers Action Plan
column5:
1. Current State Assessment
 Conduct a comprehensive review of the Cash for Business (C4B) KYB infrastructure to establish a baseline understanding of existing processes and capabilities. 
 -Document current C4B account onboarding procedures and data collection mechanisms
 -Review existing KYB policies, procedures, and supporting documentation
 -Identify available data systems and reporting capabilities
 -Map out the KYB refresh workflow
 -Analyze gaps between onboarding enhancements and refresh capabilities
 -Identify process inefficiencies, control weaknesses, and opportunities for improvement
 

 2. Risk Impact Assessment
 Evaluate the compliance and regulatory implications of overdue KYB refresh for high risk customers. 
 -Quantify the population of high risk C4B customers overdue for KYB refresh 
 -Assess downstream AML compliance risks
 -Identify regulatory exposure and potential enforcement implications
 -Evaluate reputational and operational risks
 -Determine priority remediation based on risk tier 
 

 3. Corrective Actions & Root Cause Remediation
 -Implement targeted interventions to address identified root causes and mitigate compliance risks. 
 

 3.1 Policy & Procedure Updates
 -Establish mandatory KYB refresh requirements aligned with Cash App KYB Standard
 -Define refresh frequency and triggers for high risk customers
 -Document procedures for collecting, validating, and storing updated business information
 -Define and document ramifications for customers who fail to complete refresh
 

 3.2 Design and implement a structured process to detect, assess, and act upon material changes in customer information. 
 -Establish triggers for identifying material business changes (ownership, location, business type, risk profile changes, etc.)
 -Deploy streamlined KYB refresh workflows to collect updated business information
 -Create escalation pathways for significant changes requiring immediate review
 -Establish review cycles aligned with KYB Policy requirements
 

 3.3 Control Enhancements
 -Implement system controls to flag customers overdue for KYB refresh 
 -Implement system controls to capture and flag customer information updates
 -Establish data validation and completeness checks
 -Implement validation checks to ensure data completeness and accuracy
 

 3.3 Targeted Training
 -Provide guidance on high risk customer identification and handling
 -Educate teams on AML compliance requirements and regulatory expectations
 

 3.4 Monitoring Mechanisms
 -Establish dashboards to track KYB refresh completion rates
 

 4. Remediation of High Risk Customer Population
 -Execute a structured remediation program to bring the 320,000+ high risk customers into compliance with KYB refresh requirements. 
 

 4.1 Customer Identification & Segmentation
 -Identify all high risk C4B customers overdue for KYB refresh
 -Segment customers by risk tier
 -Prioritize remediation based on regulatory risk and compliance timeline
 

 4.4 Due Diligence Review
 -Conduct due diligence reviews on refreshed customer information
 -Reassess customer risk profiles based on updated business information
 -Document findings and any changes to risk classification
 -Implement enhanced monitoring for customers with increased risk indicators
 

 4.5 Account Actions & Enforcement
 -For customers who fail to complete refresh by deadline: implement account restrictions, transaction limits, or suspension as appropriate
 -Document all account actions and compliance decisions
 -Establish/document appeals process for customers disputing restrictions
 

 5. Formalization into Business as Usual Operations
 -Embed KYB refresh processes into standard operational workflows through governance and training. 
 -Document final KYB refresh procedures and guidance 
 -Provide comprehensive training to all relevant teams 
 -Create performance metrics and KPIs to monitor ongoing compliance
 -Implement feedback loops to refine processes based on operational experience
